“This team is going to beat some people,” Izzo said. “This team is going to have some ups and downs because that's what young teams do. But boy, if (Rhoades) can hold this team together, I think this team's got a chance. They seem to get along well together. They pull from one another. I was impressed. And I'm not just saying that to make everybody feel better.”

It’s worth reading the rest of Izzo’s complementary remarks on Penn State, and for a season that had almost no good parts, this feels like a moment worth at least recalling back to in the sake of boosting morale.

Recapping the rest of a loss feels moot, but keeping it close and earning Izzo's admiration feels special – even though his prediction really didn’t come true, no one was beaten.

Give him the keys

Well, as soon as Penn State resumed its Big Ten schedule, the expected struggles persisted with eight straight conference losses after the first pair. However, on Feb. 1, Kayden Mingo, a 6-foot-3 guard from Farmingdale, New York, a freshman and Penn State’s top recruit of all time, scored a buzzer-beating highlight spin move to secure the Nittany Lions' first Big Ten victory.

Mingo lived up to most hype this season, with numbers that put him in elite company and really just show the do-it-all player he can be. Going into numbers, stretches or coaches’ commentary pales in comparison to this play against Minnesota; this was the paramount moment of the rookie’s breakout campaign.

Flirting with Dylan Mingo

Keeping it in the family with Kayden’s little brother, Dylan, we’ve got a slightly different moment that I’ve got to vouch for that is a little special. Penn State was in high running for recruiting the five-star combo guard to play with his brother, until he ultimately decided to head to UNC on First Take.

But Penn State was there, the blue and white were in the top four with massive programs in the Tarheels, Baylor and Washington and got that brief moment on ESPN – for a team that doesn’t get that recognition, or even that consideration from prospects, it’s crucial to at least acknowledge a moment that goes against the grain of historical narratives, no matter how it happened.

Reed-sanity run

The lone senior on Penn State’s incredibly youthful squad, Josh Reed’s services were leaned on and necessary in this past season. The Atlanta native averaged a mere 4.8 points per game in his last season with Cincinnati, but over doubled that figure in his only Happy Valley season.

The greatest moment of it all? A 13-game stretch where Reed reached double-digit scoring and even broke his career high twice, eventually setting the bar at 25 points. The wing was playing with an unprecedented level of personal confidence, and it rubbed off on his teammates, creating a memorable blip in Penn State history.

Reed did eventually falter on Senior Night, honoring him, with the streak snapped that night in a crushing 32-point loss to Ohio State.

Pat Kraft knows hoops exist?

Well, that Senior Night, possibly the lowest point this season, is where we’ll finish our journey, funny enough, with positive moments.

The basketball program at Penn State has long been a laughingstock, both men’s and women’s, and has been seen as the main sport neglected by the administration when it comes to allocating needed resources. But on that blowout to the Buckeyes, Athletic Director Pat Kraft was there in attendance for all 40 minutes. At least we can say he knows Penn State basketball exists, and now sees how bad it really is; maybe those resources can finally come. After all, they rose in KenPom over 60 spots – potential.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling holds 7 top seeds for NCAA Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Following seven Penn Stater wrestlers winning Big Ten titles, the NCAA seeds have been released for the national tournament beginning on March 19.

The Nittany Lions will have seven wrestlers at No. 1 seeds.

Luke Lilledahl, Shayne Van Ness, PJ Duke, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines, Rocco Welsh and Josh Barr all earned top spots in their respective weight classes. All seven of those wrestlers became 2026 Big Ten Champions.

At 133 pounds, Marcus Blaze earned the No. 3 seed after finishing second at the Big Ten Championships. Blaze sits behind Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest and Ohio State’s Ben Davino in the weight class. If both wrestlers win out, it’ll be a rubber match between Davino and Blaze in the semifinals.

At 141 pounds, Braeden Davis sits at the No. 14 seed. Davis finished seventh at the Big Tens and will face Utah Valley's Haiden Drury in the opening round.

Cole Mirasola at heavyweight is the No. 9 seed. Mirasola ended the Big Ten Championships with a fifth-place finish.

As a team, Penn State will attempt to go for a fifth straight national title beginning on Thursday, March 19, in Cleveland at Rocket Arena.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball opens Big Ten play at home against Iowa
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an unsuccessful 0-5 road trip, Penn State returns home and begins Big Ten play against Iowa. The three-game series is slated at 5:30 p.m. Friday, 2 p.m. Saturday, with the series finale at 1 p.m. Sunday. All games will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

The Nittany Lions are in a tough position entering the series on a five-game losing streak and a 4-11 overall record. Many upset losses in favorable contests against nonconference opponents has forced the blue and white to get its season back on track facing tough conference play.

Meanwhile, the Hawkeyes bring a two-game winning streak and a solid 11-5 record into Happy Valley, so Penn State recording its first series win of the campaign will be a tall task.

Key trends

Iowa possesses the best team batting average in the Big Ten at .344 and has scored the third-most runs at 145. Despite the Hawkeyes only totaling 10 home runs, they thrive with small ball as their 192 hits rank first in the conference.

The Nittany Lions pitchers need to limit runs this weekend if they want any chance of winning the series. It’s been a rocky year on the mound for Penn State, ranking last in the conference in team-ERA at 8.30 and surrendering the most hits (157) and most runs (132).

Most of the blue and white’s losses have been blowouts, providing the offense with little chance at keeping the Nittany Lions close in games. Right-hander Colin Fitzgerald was expected to be the anchor in the pitching staff, but the Maine transfer has struggled in three of his four starts.

The redshirt senior holds a 6.75 ERA and allowed 22 hits, nine walks and 12 earned runs over 16 innings. However, Fitzgerald delivered a solid performance in his second start in Penn State’s 5-0 win over Richmond, throwing a scoreless six innings and totaling eight strikeouts.

If the Lititz, Pennsylvania, native can channel some of that performance against Iowa, the Nittany Lions will be in good shape. Fitzgerald having a quality outing against a powerful lineup is also good for his confidence for the rest of the season.

Two other pitchers who might start games this weekend are right-handers Ben Hudson and Isaiah Shayter. Hudson provided a quality outing in Penn State’s 9-3 win over Grand Canyon, but his three starts since have been rocky.

The West Virginia transfer holds a 7.36 ERA, while surrendering 18 hits and 15 earned runs. Shayter’s 14.34 ERA was ballooned after his last start in the blue and white’s 14-4 loss against Texas Tech.

The freshman went just 1.2 innings and allowed five earned runs off five hits and five walks. So, the Elizabeth Town, Pennsylvania, native must last longer if he gets a start against Iowa.

Players to watch

The Hawkeyes’ lineup is stacked throughout, but there are some notable hitters the Nittany Lions must contain. Mitch Wood is one of the best all-around batters in the Big Ten.

The senior possesses a .414 average, 1.330 OPS, 12 hits and 15 RBI. Iowa doesn’t hit home runs often, but he leads the team in the category with three.

Gable Mitchell spearheads the offense with a team-best .484 average, 31 hits, 17 RBI and 12 walks.

While Penn State’s offense ranks in the bottom three for many key offensive stats in the Big Ten, Bryce Molinaro was a bright spot in its two-game series against Georgetown.

The redshirt junior homered in both games and brought his home run total to four. Michael Anderson continued his hot start and smashed his team-best seventh home run in Wednesday’s 5-4 win against the Hoyas.

Iowa’s pitching staff enters the series with a 5.18 team ERA, allowing 133 hits and 80 earned runs. The Nittany Lions will likely face the Hawkeyes’ ace, right-hander Maddux Frese.

The junior owns a 2.50 ERA, totaling 13 strikeouts, while also surrendering just 15 hits, 3 walks and five earned runs over 18 innings.

Penn State’s pitching and offense must click together to record its first series win this weekend.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball drops second game against Georgetown, going winless in series
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State baseball faced off against Georgetown in Washington, D.C., in its final game of a two-series matchup. Ultimately, Penn State would lose to Georgetown 5-4.

Penn State started out the day in the batter's box. Outfielder Cohl Mercado fouled out to start the order. Infielder Michael Anderson flew out, followed by infielder Spencer Barnett, who went down swinging. A strong start for Georgetown right-handed pitcher JT Rabb.

Nittany Lions freshman right-handed pitcher Ethan Bauerschmidt started the game on the mound in the bottom of the first. The Hoyas started off hot with a triple down the right field line by outfielder Ashtin Gilio.

Outfielder Travis Ilitch continued with a single. Infielder and pitcher Connor Peek flew out to center field, but that allowed Gilio to make it home for the game's first run. Bauerschmidt threw one out, and catcher Connor Price grounded out to end the inaugural inning.

Penn State fired back quickly, coming from a Bryce Molinaro home run to right-center field. Though the hot start would fizzle with two groundouts, Preston Yaucher went down swinging. Raab let up a double from Jayden Davis and walked Joey DeMucci, who went on to steal second base.

The second stint on defense for the blue and white was a fast one. Bauerschmidt struck out the first two Hoyas batters looking. Then, infielder AJ Solomon popped one up to shortstop to end the second inning. The score was tied 1-1 going into the third.

Back to the top of the order for the Nittany Lions, Mercado flew out to start the hitting. Anderson and Barnett hit back-to-back homers to center and right-center, respectively. Molinaro grounded out after his homerun in the last inning and Jack Porter struck out swinging. Penn State led 3-1, only scoring by going the distance.

Gilio flew out on his second at-bat. Ilitch singled through the right side to get back on the positive for Georgetown. Bauerschmidt hit Peek with a pitch to advance the runners. Jeremy Sheffield moved the runners again after an error from shortstop Yaucher.

Christian Hamilton reached on a fielder's choice, advancing the runners and allowing Ilitch to score unearned. Hamilton was then caught stealing to end the inning. Penn State still led 3-2 after an inning of mishaps.

The Hoyas started the 4th with a pitching change; they swapped Rabb for freshman right-handed pitcher Ashton Arroyo. Davis got to first with a bunt, followed by Jesse Jaconski getting hit by a pitch. A Yaucher single led to a Davis score and Jaconski getting thrown out when he tried to advance to third.

Georgetown made another pitching change from Arroyo to left-handed pitcher Andrew Jergins, now their third of the game. Yaucher got caught stealing second to end Penn State's at-bats.

Price was walked, then got to second with the help of Dylan Larkins bunt, a sacrifice play. Dante Pozzi hit a homer to left field for a two-run play. After that, the Hoyas had two groundouts to end the 4th. The score stood tied up 4-4 going into the 5th inning.

Mercado struck out swinging and Anderson got the first hit for the Nittany Lions in the inning. Barnett then grounded a ball into a double play. A quick inning of batting for Penn State.

A pitching change got Bauerschmidt off the mound and gave right-handed pitcher Braden Leek his chance. Coach Mike Gambino also made changes to catcher and first base; he moved Avery Smith to catcher and DeMucci to first.

Leed began his pitching with a walk for Ilitch, who got caught stealing for an out. Peek lined out, and Sheffield swung out to end a fast offensive series for Georgetown.

The Hoyas made another pitching change; they took out Jergins and tried graduate student Griffin O’Connor. Molinaro popped up for the first out of the 6th inning. Porter was walked and Davis singled. The advancements stopped after Jaconski and DeMucci both flew out.

Penn State also began its defensive 6th inning with a pitching change. Out went Leed and in went lefty Logan Olsen. Hamilton singled to first and Price struck out looking. Olsen walked Larkins and both Price and Larkins stole second and third. Olsen messed up again, hitting Dante Pozzi with a pitch.

After a rough start for Olsen, he got switched out for righty Kyle Emmons. Solomon struck out looking, followed by a groundout from Gilio to end the 6th. The score remained tied 4-4.

O’Connor continued his nice start; he made quick work of the 7th. Yaucher grounded out to second, Mercado did the same, and Anderson went down swinging,

The Nittany Lions made yet another pitching change. This time, they swapped Emmons for Robert Brown III, a freshman right-handed pitcher. Brown III started off well, Ilitch and Peek both grounded out and Sheffield singled. Hamilton got thrown out at first to end the quick 7th; the score was still tied 4-4.

Avery Smith started at-bat for Penn State, and he hit a single. Molinaro and Porter followed up with outs, though Smith got to second. Davis lined out to centerfield for another quick batting performance from Penn State.

Brown III continued his hot start, striking out Price looking. Larkins lined out to right field, but Pozzi hit a home run over left field. Brown III got back on pace with a pop-up out from Solomon to move to the final inning. Georgetown led 5-4.

The Hoyas started the last inning with a pitching change; O’Connor went out for right-handed pitcher Ethan Rucker. Rucker made quick work of the Nittany Lions batters. Jaconski and DeMucci both struck out swinging and Yaucher grounded out to third to end the game.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to face Iowa in its Big Ten opener on Friday at 5:30 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey captain Tessa Janecke named finalist for Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State senior forward Tessa Janecke has been named a top-three finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award.

In addition, she has also become the first player in program history to receive this honor. This award is presented annually to the top player in NCAA Division I women’s hockey.

TESSA JANECKE. HISTORY MAKER.Our captain becomes the FIRST Top-Three Finalist for the Patty Kaz in program history 🐐📰: https://t.co/53Ku8mPsyP#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/79QROSnq7s— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 11, 2026

Janecke recently returned to Happy Valley after representing Team USA at the 2026 Winter Olympics, where she helped the United States capture a gold medal in Milan. But her achievements didn't stop there as she became the first player in program history to be named one of the final three candidates for the prestigious award.

Janecke’s recognition comes after an outstanding season and career with the Nittany Lions. Penn State's captain is the program’s all-time leading scorer and has earned multiple honors, including Atlantic Hockey America Player of the Year and Forward of the Year.

The winner will be announced on March 21 during a ceremony at the HUB-Robeson Center in State College as part of the NCAA Women’s Frozen Four festivities.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Who should Penn State men’s hockey start in net for the postseason?
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Guy Gadowsky faces a decision Wednesday evening: who starts in net for the Nittany Lions in the postseason?

Penn State has two very capable goaltenders in Josh Fleming and Kevin Reidler, and both have split netminding duties since the beginning of the 2025-26 campaign. However, one must take the reins ahead of the most important playoff stretch in program history.

Who should do it? It should be Fleming, and here’s why.

The rationale

Let’s start with the simple stuff.

Fleming leads the team with a 2.59 goals-allowed average, a .924 save percentage and has earned his club a point in 68.75% of his starts. Reidler, on the other hand, owns a 3.31 goals-against average, .901 save percentage and has earned his team a point in 61.11% of his starts.

Yet, despite Fleming leading Reidler in each category, he has started in two fewer games this season. Some may argue Gadowsky was keeping Fleming fresh by splitting time between the two goaltenders, but who starts in the postseason seems like a real decision he is mulling over.

To further Fleming’s case, he was named a Hobey Baker Award nominee and the National Co-Rookie of the Month for January. Additionally, his previously stated .924 save percentage ranks him 10th-best in the country. Reidler’s .901 save percentage puts him just outside the top 50.

Not to mention, in each of their four games against No. 1 Michigan and No. 3 Michigan State, the top-ranked and fifth-rated scoring offenses in the country, Fleming allowed a 3.50 goals-allowed average, while Reidler surrendered a 4.75.

In those contests, Fleming allowed five goals or more zero times, while Reidler hit the mark in three of the four games.

Now, after seeing Fleming has been superior in the basic statistics, let’s delve into the advanced metrics.

Fleming holds a .928 save percentage at even strength, an .898 save percentage shorthanded, a .924 save percentage on close opportunities and an .858 high-danger save percentage.

Reidler owns a .910 save percentage at even strength, an .887 shorthanded save percentage, a .907 save percentage on close opportunities and an .851 high-danger save percentage.

Yes, the trends continued, Fleming leads Reidler in all four major categories, and the numbers, while they seem it, aren’t all that close. And that’s not even the most eye-catching metric. The freshman has saved a whopping 13 goals above expected, while Reidler has stopped -0.2.

However, those are season-long statistics. So, what do the most recent ones say?

While it hasn’t been pretty for either goaltender in the Nittany Lions’ 2-6-2 rut over their last 10 contests, stretching back to the late January Beaver Stadium outdoor game, Fleming has outplayed Reidler over the past month.

In Fleming’s last three appearances, he has recorded a .917, .829 and .941 save percentage. Reidler, in his last three games, has posted an .875, .879 and .800 save percentage.

This is no knock on Reidler; he has been strong in net for Penn State this season. His nine games recording an .920 save percentage or higher, is more than Fleming’s seven and he’s played well outside of a few outlier games.

However, as Gadowsky noticed during the season when he moved Fleming to the Game 1 starting netminder, the Canadian has been the more consistent guy for the Nittany Lions between the pipes and gives the team a better chance to win.

Thus, Gadowsky should give Fleming the nod for the entirety of the postseason. Fleming should be fresh and he has shown he can stand toe-to-toe against the best goaltenders in the country, while stymying some of the most lethal offenses in the nation.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State releases jersey numbers for 2026 roster
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After eager anticipation from the fanbase, Penn State’s jersey numbers have been fully updated on the team’s official website.

Here’s what you need to know.

Iowa State transfers

Wide receiver Chase Sowell: 0

Wide receiver Zay Robinson: 2

Quarterback Rocco Becht: 3

Linebacker Caleb Bacon: 10

Other transfers

Grambling State wide receiver Keith Jones Jr.: 15

St. Francis offensive lineman Hunter Albright: 76

Freshmen

Quarterback Kase Evans: 12

Quarterback Peyton Falzone: 14

Cornerback Josiah Zayas: 17

Wide receiver Amarion Jackson: 19

Cornerback Tyrell Chatman: 20

Safety Bryson Williams: 28

Running back D’Antae Sheffey: 29

Safety Christian Askew: 30

Offensive lineman Mason Bandhauer: 56

Wide receiver Ben Whitver: 81

Defensive end Jackson Ford: 89

Evans, Zayas, Jackson, Chatman, Williams and Bandhauer are all former Iowa State signees.

Jackson, who was listed as an athlete upon his signing, will start out as a wide receiver instead of a defensive back.

Ford was the lone player to remain committed from Penn State’s 2026 recruiting class. There were 25 players who backed off their pledge following the firing of James Franklin.

Falzone was originally committed to Virginia Tech before changing his commitment to Penn State in April. He then flipped to Auburn in June before re-committing to the Nittany Lions on National Signing Day in December.

Whitver and Askew are former JMU signees. Sheffey is a product of State College Area High School.

Offensive tackle Pete Eglitis, defensive end Elijah Reeder, linebacker Keian Kaiser and punter Lucas Tenbrock, who are all former Iowa State signees, will enroll in the summer and likely get their number then.

Walk-ons like wide receiver/cornerback Henry Lustig, wide receiver/cornerback Deymein Doctor, linebacker Josh Banks, tight end Jake Lukac and running back Amar’e Glover also aren’t listed. Lustig is the son of special teams coordinator Justin Lustig, while Lukac is the son of former Nittany Lion tight end Mike Lukac.

New numbers for returners

Wide receiver Koby Howard, defensive end Yvan Kemajou, cornerback Donte Nastasi and offensive tackle Garrett Sexton were the only Penn State returners who had a number change.

Howard went from No. 3 to No. 6, as Becht will return to the number he wore at Iowa State.

Kemajou will wear No. 19 after sporting No. 99 a season ago. He’ll take Tennessee transfer Chaz Coleman’s number.

Nastasi will go from No. 81 to No. 16. The State College native is the son of former Penn State wide receiver Joe Nastasi.

With Buhr keeping his number from Iowa State, Garrett Sexton went up one number, now wearing No. 71. Sexton rocked No. 71 at Arrowhead High School in Wisconsin, and will take ex-Penn State offensive lineman Vega Ioane’s number, whom he texted about making the switch.

“He sent me a laughing emoji and let’s go or something,” Sexton said at the THON Explorers Program. “It is pretty cool wearing V’s number.”

Other roster news

LaVar Arrington II has changed positions, officially going from linebacker to defensive end after previously listing his Instagram bio as such.

Cooper, who started out as a safety at Iowa State despite playing cornerback last year before his season-ending injury, will return to cornerback.

Redshirt sophomore Caleb Brewer is still listed as an offensive lineman on the official roster, but he will reportedly be making the switch to the defensive line.

All other returning players will wear the same number as last year.

title: ‘Very physical and very aggressive’ | Northwestern defeats Penn State men’s basketball behind turnovers
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Rhoades made one thing clear during his press conference on Monday when asked what Penn State needs to do in order to make a run in the Big Ten Tournament: limit turnovers.

Rhoades also said he emphasized minimizing the number of turnovers to his team ahead of Penn State’s first-round game against Northwestern inside the United Center in Chicago. However, that goal didn’t come to fruition as 17 Penn State turnovers led to 24 points for the Wildcats, who earned a 76-66 win.

“I thought the key to the game was the turnovers and the points off turnovers,” Rhoades told reporters in Chicago postgame. “I thought that was the game right there.”

For reference, Northwestern only had eight turnovers. Penn State scored just seven points off of those opportunities, highlighting the advantage the Wildcats had in this aspect.

Both squads were fighting back and forth throughout the first half, and even though the Nittany Lions had seven turnovers that became nine points for Northwestern, they were only down by one possession at halftime, 34-32.

The close contest continued into much of the second half with Northwestern holding a 60-55 lead at the 8:06 mark. However, consecutive turnovers by Penn State that followed allowed the Wildcats to pull away.

Guard Kayden Mingo lost control of the ball at the 6:53 mark, and Northwestern’s Tre Singleton scored a layup on the other end. Fellow guard Melih Tunca had another lost-ball turnover on the ensuing Penn State offensive possession, which led to a layup by the Wildcats’ Jordan Clayton.

“Northwestern plays very physical and very aggressive in their half-court,” Rhoades said. “They get turnovers in their half-court defense. I told our guys, the two things that were the key to the game is we can't turn the ball over and we have to rebound because if you go down there and you don't rebound, you have to sustain your defense for another full length of a shot clock.”

Those two turnovers allowed Northwestern to grow its lead to 64-55, causing Rhoades to spend a timeout. Coming out of that break, 7-footer Ivan Jurić traveled, which led to two more points for the Wildcats with guard Jayden Reid knocking down two free throws.

With 5:11 left in the game, Northwestern had its first double-digit lead of the night, and its last six points came off a Penn State turnover. A Nittany Lions turnover happened again soon after, and forward Nick Martinelli scored a dunk on the fast break, giving the Wildcats a 68-55 lead with 3:48 left and prompting another timeout by Rhoades.

At that point, Penn State was in a hole it proved it could never recover from. Singleton scored another basket off a turnover by the Nittany Lions with just over a minute left as well.

It’s been a trend as of late for Penn State to allow a sizable advantage in points off turnovers. Ohio State had 19 points off turnovers in its win on March 4, while Rutgers scored 15 points off turnovers in its victory last Sunday. Northwestern just became the latest team to beat the Nittany Lions due to turnovers.

“That was the game,” Rhoades said. “Maybe the physicality pushed us out a little bit, messed up our timing. You fight the pressure a little bit. I'm not gonna say we were lazy — our guys played hard today, played the right way. We got off to a good start. That’s the next step for us. If you're going to beat a Big Ten team in March, can't give them the ball.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball falls to Northwestern in Big Ten Tournament, ending season
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State stepped onto the hardwood in Chicago’s United Center for what could’ve been the last time in the 2025-26 season, and for some on both sides, what could’ve been the last time ever.

Ultimately, it would be the blue and white’s last time together this season, after a 76-66 loss to Northwestern.

For the first time in the new 18-team format for the Big Ten Tournament, the bottom-seeded Nittany Lions sent out a first unit of Kayden Mingo and Dom Stewart in the back court, with Ivan Jurić and Josh Reed anchoring the forward positions, while freshman wing Mason Blackwood received his first-ever collegiate start.

The No. 15 seed Wildcats sent out a group with program legend Nick Martinelli as the pinnacle of their offense, just as Penn State’s senior, Reed, it could’ve been his last game.

The clash started early, as the two units stood tied at 12 points each after five minutes of play, and the two narratives came to a head. 7-footer Jurić received feed after feed inside, with four of the blue and white’s first made buckets. The big man outpaced Martinelli with 10 points, who notched seven at that same point despite entering as a top-five Big Ten scorer.

Both squads traded buckets over the next few minutes, with the offensive effort dispersed among more of the rosters. The blue and white found an offensive sparkplug off the bench in the form of Freddie Dilione V, who made back-to-back slick scores. That brief sequence would be his only real offensive contribution on the night.

At the five-minute mark, freshman guard Melih Tunca, who’d dealt with a nagging injury in recent weeks, drained a three-pointer at the shot clock buzzer to tie the game at 26 with a quick release. Yet, Martinelli got a few buckets back to claw Northwestern back into the lead with a little over a minute of play left in the first half.

The Wildcats boasted a 34-32 lead going into the break, as Martinelli carried a game-high 13 points into the locker room, only narrowly followed by Jurić with 12. Both squads posted efficient marks from the field, but the Nittany Lions shot a 50% clip from downtown, double that of Northwestern. Although the Wildcats grabbed five more boards and allowed three fewer turnovers at the break.

When both teams returned to the court, the levelheaded action resumed. Mingo converted an and-one, but Martinelli hit a three on the other end to cancel out the bucket, and after three minutes of play, Northwestern led 42-38.

Some more buckets ticked up the score, and Dilione got plucked for a fastbreak Northwestern bucket; he then threw a sloppy pass, which was almost taken away, yet Mingo scored on the possession.

The Nittany Lions played solid defense on Northwestern in the next few tries, but an unguarded Martinelli easily grabbed an offensive rebound for the putback and his 20th points of the night.

Approaching the halfway mark of the second frame of action, the Nittany Lions’ turnover woes continued to plague them, as the Wildcats held their largest lead, 56-49, at that point. Although Jurić soon thereafter reached 20 points on a big slam, which also tied his career high and cut into that lead.

The big man then picked up his fourth foul, which forced him to head to the bench in fear of losing his services.

Without Jurić, the offense went flat, and after Northwestern airballed, the five-point deficit appeared closeable, but Mingo let the ball slip through his fingers. The Wildcats then converted a three-point play, and Jurić waited to check back in. Normally reliable scorer, Dilione, went quiet in the second, and he wasn’t alone.

Things just looked sloppy as the blue and white attempted to claw back. Jurić’s pass to Reed inside went out of bounds, and the morale seemed distraught with an 11-point deficit. With two minutes of play, Penn State continued to charge right at its opponent, showing no sign of surrender.

In that charge, however, the blue and white hit a wall, with its season ending in the process.

The Nittany Lions were led on the offensive end by Jurić with 22 points, followed by Reed, Mingo and Dilione as double-digit scorers. Martinelli led all players with 24 points, as teammate Jayden Reid finished with 14.

Penn State recorded a 51% shooting mark on attempts from the field, followed by a 43% mark from three-point range, with team figures of 17 turnovers and 22 rebounds. Northwestern logged 28 rebounds and scored 24 points off turnovers to the Nittany Lions’ seven points off turnovers.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls in series opener with Georgetown in another lopsided loss
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a series sweep against Texas Tech, Penn State hit the road again and faced Georgetown on Tuesday afternoon for the first of a two-game series.

The Nittany Lions entered the contest with a subpar record of 4-9 and the worst team-ERA in the Big Ten at 8.71, so their pitching staff had to limit runs against the Hoyas, who entered atop of the Big East at 10-6.

Penn State’s losing streak extended to four games in its 8-2 loss against Georgetown, behind more poor pitching and cold bats.

A pair of right-handers battled it out on the mound with Charlie Hendrix starting for Georgetown while Frankie Sanchez got the ball for Penn State. Both offenses went down quietly in the opening frame, but applied pressure in the second.

Right fielder Jack Porter laced a leadoff single up the middle and then Hendrix hit second baseman Jesse Jaconski. Porter was now in scoring position with just one out, but Hendrix retired first baseman Joey DeMucci and left fielder Maddox McDonald to end the threat.

Sanchez also ran into some trouble in the bottom of the second. Christian Hamilton poked a one-out single through the left side and Braxton Templin then drew a walk. However, Sanchez got Dylan Larkins to ground into an inning-ending double play to shortstop Preston Yaucher.

The blue and white broke the scoreless tie in the third. Yaucher launched a deep ball into center and raced around the bags for a leadoff triple. Then the Nittany Lions got some help from the Hoyas’ defense as center fielder Cohl Mercado’s routine ground ball to shortstop Connor Peek was bobbled and allowed Yaucher to score.

Though it was just a 1-0 lead, Penn State hasn’t possessed many leads throughout its slow start. However, the Nittany Lions wouldn’t have the lead for long as Georgetown answered right back and plated four runs across in its half of the third.

AJ Solomon worked a long one-out walk and Ashtin Gilio gave the Hoyas the lead with one swing of the bat. The junior smothered a two-run home run deep over the center field wall and put the Hoyas up, 2-1.

Sanchez was a little rattled and walked Travis Ilitch on five pitches. Now facing the heart of Georgetown’s lineup, Connor Peek crushed a double into right-center and set up runners on the corners.

After Sanchez retired cleanup batter Jeremy Sheffield, Coach Mike Gambino brought in left-hander Matthew VanOstenbridge.

Yet the sophomore didn’t stop the bleeding. Hamilton delivered a clutch two-out single and extended the Hoyas lead, 4-1. Now down three runs, the Nittany Lions would have to play catch-up again to pull off the victory.

Right-hander Andrew Citron retired the blue and white in order and the Hoyas added two more runs in their half of the fourth. Larkins drew a leadoff walk and then was shifty on the basepaths as he stole second.

VanOstenbridge committed an error that allowed Larkins to quickly reach third. The pressure was on and Solomon drew another walk-off, a wild pitch that let Larkins score. The errors continued for the Nittany Lions’ defense as Gilio reached base off an error by DeMucci at first.

Ilitch then laid down a perfect bunt single and bases were now loaded with still only one out. Gambino turned to right-hander Ben DeMell in the jam. The redshirt senior didn’t get help from his defense as the errors continued in the inning.

Peek reached first and scored another run off the third error of the frame by Bryce Molinaro at third. DeMell stayed composed and surrendered no further runs, but Penn State was in a massive 6-1 hole.

After a quiet fifth inning, Molinaro helped cut into the five-run deficit in the sixth. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native launched his third home run of the season into left center and chipped the Nittany Lions closer, 6-2.

DeMell provided a few shutdown innings, but left-hander Logan Olson struggled with his command in the seventh and Georgetown capitalized on bases loaded with no outs and plated two more runs.

While the blue and white’s pitching continued struggling throughout this game, its bats also didn’t produce much. Penn State struck out 10 times and totaled just five hits and one walk.

With the loss, the Nittany Lions fell to 10-4 on the campaign.

Up Next

Penn State looks to split the series against Georgetown at 4 p.m. Wednesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse begins Big Ten play against USC
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After over a month of action, Penn State’s winning streak is still alive.

The Nittany Lions are 8-0 on the year for the first time since 1991 and have a chance to continue that against USC at 9 p.m. on Thursday as Penn State kicks off Big Ten play.

While the Trojans have a ranked win already this year, the blue and white’s undefeated record likely gives it the upper hand coming into Thursday.

All-time matchups

The Penn State-USC lacrosse rivalry has only been around for a year since the Trojans joined the conference in 2024. Since then, the two teams have played one game, with Penn State coming out victorious.

The Nittany Lions had a heyday when USC came to Happy Valley last year, winning 16-8 with Tewaaraton Award watch list member Erika Ho leading the way.

Ho scored four of the blue and white’s goals against the Trojans, along with a hat trick from now-graduated Brooke Long. Penn State outscored USC 10-2 in the second half as Trojan goalie Charlotte Morton recorded just a 20% save percentage.

2026 Trojans

Since joining the NCAA in 2013, USC has been a dominant lacrosse program with six NCAA Tournament appearances in 13 years.

This year, that success continues as the Trojans are 6-2 to start the year, earning an upset win over then-No. 24 Brown for their first big win of the season.

While USC doesn’t have the strength on attack that the Nittany Lions do, it has seen its five leading scorers each record at least 10 goals on the year. Sophomore Kaylee Fravert leads the way with 13 goals for the Trojans.

Junior Reese Robinson has also been having herself a year, sitting second on the team in points with 11 goals and 11 assists. Only junior attacker Emma Bunting leads her, as she’s picked up 17 assists on the year.

The Trojans haven’t had a clear starting goalkeeper this year, as the duty’s switched between Morton and sophomore Ellie Thomas, who’ve each started four games.

Thursday’s matchup

No matter who the starting goalkeeper is on Thursday night, she’ll have her hands full with the No. 2 offense in the country.

The Nittany Lions have two players with over 20 goals on the year and four with over 20 points.

On the goal-scoring end, freshman Rocquette Allen and senior Payton Wainman each have 22 goals, while junior Delaney Radin leads with 37 points from 11 goals and 26 assists — the most in the Big Ten.

While USC will be trying to keep up with the Nittany Lions’ high-scoring affair, it will likely struggle to score on junior goalie Sydney Manning, who sits at 14th in the country with a 50% save percentage.

After starting the season with three out of four games under 50%, Manning has gone on a strong run in net with three of her last four games over 50%, including a perfect night against Drexel to wrap up February.

Penn State comes into Thursday night’s matchup as the more dominant team, but with the start of the Big Ten season and a game under the lights, USC will have a chance to take down the No. 18-ranked Nittany Lions.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball set for West Coast trip to Oregon State, open Big Ten play against Oregon
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After spending several weekends down South, Penn State is set to take off to the West Coast for a group of games in the Beaver State. The Nittany Lions will face Oregon State for a midweek matchup before heading to Eugene for their first Big Ten series against No. 21 Oregon.

The blue and white stand at 18-6 on the season following a 2-2 weekend at the Hokie Invite in Salem, Virginia.

Oregon State

Penn State will face the Beavers at 8 p.m. Wednesday in the fifth all-time meeting between the teams. Oregon State has won every matchup against the Nittany Lions, though the teams haven’t met since 2014.

The Beavers are 10-17 in 2026 and most recently went 1-5 at the Jane Sanders Classic last weekend. Meanwhile, the Nittany Lions dropped their last two games at the Hokie Invite and hope to get back on track.

Oregon State holds a .288 team batting average, with five players hitting above .350, led by Jaeya Butler’s .426. The junior infielder leads the Beavers in every offensive category with 36 RBI, 10 doubles, 94 total bases and 14 home runs, the third most in the nation. Butler is difficult to pitch around, recording 40 hits with just two walks.

The Beavers’ average isn’t far behind Penn State’s .311. The Nittany Lions have their own standout slugger in freshman infielder Allison Oneacre.

Oneacre has started her rookie season strong, leading Penn State with a .394 batting average, 37 RBI and 10 home runs. She also ranks second on the team with 28 hits and six doubles.

While the offenses are similar, there is a disparity in the circle. Oregon State relies more on its bats, as the Beavers pitching staff has a 5.27 ERA, while Penn State’s sits at 2.47.

Oregon State’s best pitcher has been Bailey Murray, who leads the team with a 4.23 ERA and 36 strikeouts in 54.2 innings. The freshman mostly works in relief, making 23 appearances and six starts.

Oregon

Penn State opens Big Ten play with a weekend series at No. 17 Oregon, starting Friday at 9 p.m. This is the teams’ first meeting as conference foes, but the Ducks hold a 6-0 all-time edge, with the last matchup in 2022.

Oregon is 18-7 after a 4-1 weekend. Statistically, the Ducks post numbers similar to Penn State, making this a matchup to watch.

Oregon’s .320 batting average is slightly above Penn State’s, with three players hitting over .400, led by Cal transfer Elon Butler.

Butler is the pioneer of the Ducks’ offense, leading the team with a .440 batting average, 28 RBI, 33 hits, 11 doubles and six home runs.

Aside from Oneacre, another bat that’s been producing for the Nittany Lions is junior utility player Brooke Klosowicz, who owns a .370 batting average and 23 RBI.

In the circle, Penn State has the edge over Oregon’s 2.97 ERA. The Ducks’ top arm is right-hander Lyndsey Grein, who has a 1.98 ERA and 80 strikeouts in 60 innings.

The Nittany Lions have found success in the circle by relying on five arms consistently. No pitcher has recorded more than 34 innings this season.

So far, left-hander Mackenzie Duncan has Penn State’s best numbers in the circle with a 1.14 ERA and 44 strikeouts.

Against Oregon’s competition level, the Nittany Lions will likely need all hands on deck in the circle.

A strong showing in Eugene against an evenly matched team could set the tone for Penn State’s Big Ten campaign.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State women’s lacrosse stays hot after 2-0 week
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Things are continuing to go Penn State’s way after another 2-0 week under coach Kayla Treanor.

The Nittany Lions moved to 8-0 on the year for the first time since 1991 after defeating Colgate 20-8 and San Diego State 18-6.

Here’s a look at what’s been going right for the blue and white as it’s looked unstoppable through a little over a month of action.

Erika Ho’s return

After leading the Nittany Lions in scoring last year, senior attacker Erika Ho had a strong start to the year with six goals and five assists through three games.

However, the Tewaaraton Award watchlister suffered an injury near the end of the Bucknell game and missed the following two games.

Ho returned Feb. 28 in a limited role, but was back full time against the Raiders, scoring a goal following four shots. Four days later, she was back to her old self with two goals and an assist against the Aztecs, shooting with 100% accuracy.

With a lot of depth in this year’s Penn State offense, the team hasn’t had to rely so much on Ho scoring a majority of the goals, and have used her skills throughout the season.

No. 2 offense

Penn State’s 8-0 record has been made possible as the Nittany Lions have outscored opponents 125-64 this season.

The Nittany Lions boast the second-best offense in the nation, led by freshman Rocquette Allen and senior Payton Wainman, who have combined for 44 goals this year.

In the two games last week, the duo combined for 10 of Penn State’s 38 goals as Wainman recorded her third hat trick of the year.

However, it hasn’t been just Wainman and Allen who’ve been dominant for the Nittany Lions. Kelly MacKinney, Anna Salerno, Delaney Radin and Ho have each scored double-digit goals this year as the Nittany Lions saw 14 different athletes find the back of the net in 2026.

Delaney Radin’s success

While she’s only started one game for the blue and white this year, Radin has been one of the most successful players on the team.

Radin leads the Big Ten with 26 assists and sits atop the Nittany Lions with 37 points on the year.

Against Colgate on Wednesday, she tied the Penn State school record for the most assists in a single game, as she earned eight assists. She also went 1-for-1 with shooting, adding to the Nittany Lions’ 20 goals.

While Radin only recorded one assist against San Diego State, she scored two goals and was one of five players on the blue and white with multiple goals.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s hockey opens Big Ten Tournament hosting Minnesota
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s back-and-forth season has finally come to where it matters most: the postseason.

The Nittany Lions kick off the Big Ten Tournament with a quarterfinals matchup against Minnesota at 7 p.m. on Wednesday.

While the Nittany Lions came into the season with the No. 1 prospect in the 2026 NHL Draft’s in Gavin McKenna, things haven’t gone as planned, especially over the last few weeks.

The blue and white will be fighting for a spot in the NCAA Tournament on Wednesday, as a loss will put it in the danger zone, while a win would give the Nittany Lions better odds at continuing their path to a second-straight Frozen Four.

The two teams met up earlier this year in Minnesota, as the blue and white came out with a win and a loss. Reese Laubach led the way with two goals in the 3-2 loss, while Charlie Cerrato and Jackson Smith each found the back of the net in the 2-1 victory.

Since the matchups in November, the Nittany Lions have been plagued by injuries, and will be without Dane Dowiak, Alex Servagno and Nick Fascia on Wednesday.

After missing nearly two months of action, Cerrato returned from injury on Friday, earning an assist in Wisconsin’s series sweep.

The last month-and-a-half of action hasn’t been exactly how Guy Gadowsky’s squad envisioned ending the season with a 2-6-2 run, as Laubach said, “I feel like guys are starting to check out, and I think we feel too comfortable,” following Thursday’s loss.

While the Nittany Lions have struggled, they’ll have a chance to get back on track against the Golden Gophers, who are just 11-21-3 on the year.

Since December, Minnesota has only won four games, but it recently upset No. 2 Michigan 4-2 on Feb. 26.

Junior goalie Luca Di Pasquo has kept things alive for the Golden Gophers lately with a .912 save percentage on the year. While he’s been the starting goalkeeper for Minnesota, the Nittany Lions have gone between Kevin Reidler and Josh Fleming, who’ve recorded save percentages of .901 and .924 on the season, respectively.

On the ice, Minnesota is led by sophomore forward Brodie Ziemer, who leads the team with 23 goals.

Penn State doesn’t have a single player above 20 goals on the year, but has five different players with double-digit goals, as compared to Minnesota’s two players.

Matt DiMarsico and JJ Wiebusch are the teams top two goal scorers with 17 and 16, respectively, but it's McKenna who has been the difference-maker on offense. He leads the team and the nation with 34 total assists as the primary facilitator for the unit.

While both teams are coming into the matchup following rough stretches, the Nittany Lions have the home-ice advantage — though the extent of that advantage remains up in the air with Penn State students on spring break.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: No. 9 Penn State men's lacrosse sweeps Big Ten weekly awards for first time since 2020
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 9 Penn State men's lacrosse swept the Big Ten weekly awards for the second time in program history, doing so for the first time since 2020. The Nittany Lions posted a 19-7 victory over then-No. 7 Cornell on Saturday.

For the first time since 2020, and third time ever, there’s a sweep in the @b1glacrosse weekly honors 😤Gills ➡️ Specialist OTWHawkins ➡️ Freshman OTWLehman ➡️ Offensive POTWRoss ➡️ Defensive POTW pic.twitter.com/yoAyf6yvAk— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) March 10, 2026

Reid Gills was named the Big Ten Specialist of the Week, dominating the faceoff dot with a 13-for-16 mark against the Big Red.

Goaltender Preston Hawkins took home the Big Ten Freshman of the Week honor, making nine saves on 15 shots in his third win as a Nittany Lion. The freshman also caused two turnovers and picked up five ground balls.

Junior Kyle Lehman was named the Big Ten Offensive Player of the Week with his seven-point afternoon against Cornell, scoring a "sock trick" of six goals on eight shots and adding a singular helper.

Senior Alex Ross --- the Big Ten Defensive Player of the Week --- notched an assist in the game on a goal scored by Liam Matthews. He also picked up four ground balls and caused three turnovers as Penn State's top defender.

It's championship week, and Penn State is on the rise.

The women's squad rose one place to No. 25 in the latest USTFCCCA poll, while the men's team remained at No. 7.

Indoor Checkpoint No. 7 ✅We've reached the final indoor checkpoint of the USTFCCCA TFRI. Now is the time to find out which programs set themselves up well enough to contend for national titles at the various NCAA Championships this weekend.https://t.co/qj0d6lXcAH pic.twitter.com/J8wYH7AToj— USTFCCCA (@USTFCCCA) March 10, 2026

The Nittany Lions are coming off a dominant performance at the Big Ten championship, where Handal Roban, Ryan Henry and Hayley Kitching earned individual titles.

Both squads are preparing for the NCAA championship on Friday and Saturday, as the blue and white qualified 12 competitors for a chance at a national title.

title: Penn State women's lacrosse's Delaney Radin, Rocquette Allen earn weekly honors
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's season continues to be successful, and its players are being rewarded for it.

Attacker Rocquette Allen was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Week for the third week in a row, while junior Delaney Radin was named Big Ten Offensive Player of the Week.

👑 𝐓𝐇𝐑𝐄𝐄 👑 𝐏𝐄𝐀𝐓 👑Rocquette is the first since Madison Taylor to win Big Ten Freshman of the Week three consecutive weeks‼️ pic.twitter.com/5gdRA1enge— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 10, 2026

𝗢𝗯𝘃𝗶𝗼𝘂𝘀𝗹𝘆 😮‍💨Delaney earns her first Big Ten Offensive Player of the Week honor‼️ pic.twitter.com/fOE7Ag1nNe— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 10, 2026

Allen recorded four points in last week's contests as she tallied two goals per game, becoming the first player since 2023 to earn the honor three weeks in a row.

On the other hand, Radin recorded 12 points on the week, including a school record-tying eight assists against Colgate.

The following game, she crossed the double-digit mark for goals on the year, as she's tallied 11 goals and 26 assists this season.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey's Kendall Butze earns back-to-back AHA Defenseman of the Month honors
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State senior defenseman Kendall Butze continues to stack up conference recognition, earning AHA Defenseman of the Month for February, marking her second straight monthly honor.

back-to-back 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐃𝐞𝐟𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐌𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐡 honors for Kenny 🙂‍↕️📰: https://t.co/LbzMIfQOxF#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/bZhNCLfbht— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 9, 2026

Butze closed out the regular season strong, recording five points, one goal and four assists, across the final four games as the No. 3 Nittany Lions went 3-1 down the stretch.

She delivered one of her biggest performances in a 4-2 win over RIT, scoring the game-winning goal and adding two assists at Pegula Ice Arena.

She also contributed defensively, blocking four shots while helping anchor a unit that posted a 5-0 shutout against Lindenwood during the final weeks of the regular season.

With the February honor, Butze ensures that she finishes the regular season going back-to-back as AHA Defenseman of the Month.

Penn State will face the winner of UConn and Princeton at 2 p.m. Saturday in Pegula Ice Arena.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball jumps one spot in latest AVCA rankings
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State's men’s volleyball has held steady at No. 16 weeks in a row, but made a change as it finally went up to No. 15 in the national rankings.

⬆️1⃣ spot in the @AVCAVolleyball Poll#WeAre pic.twitter.com/OoUT9Fj9iC— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) March 9, 2026

Though being swept by No. 2 Long Beach State in three sets on Saturday night, the Nittany Lions still powered through the polls.

Penn State and Pepperdine have a historic rivalry, with their last major meeting in the NCAA tournament occurring on May 3, 2008.

With the Nittany Lions' new number in the ranks, they are set to face No. 6 Pepperdine at 9 p.m. Wednesday in Malibu, California.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Levi Haines says winning fourth Big Ten title in a row is ‘pretty cool’
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
There’s a small list of Penn State wrestlers who can claim four Big Ten Championships.

Levi Haines joins that list after winning his fourth straight title this weekend.

“I guess pretty cool,” Haines said of winning. “Right place at the right time. This one was just like the other ones. It’s just good practice getting ready for the nationals.”

Haines joins David Taylor, Ed Ruth and Aaron Brooks as Nittany Lions to have won four Big Ten Championships in their careers. What Haines did, however, was what made it more special. He’s the only one of the four to win four straight, not having redshirted during his career.

Haines also did it in a unique way. He started at 157 pounds and wrestled at that weight his first two seasons before jumping to 174 pounds for his final two years.

It adds even more uniqueness given the fact he didn’t redshirt at all, something Cael Sanderson often utilizes if available with his wrestlers. A 2025 Big Ten Champion, Tyler Kasak, redshirted this year at 157 pounds, but Haines’ capabilities were undeniable, allowing him to wrestle four years in a row without having to use a redshirt.

“He just goes out there and just does what he does,” Sanderson said. “Just that consistency is probably number one, right? Just the guy that wants to learn.”

Haines is the only one of the four Nittany Lions to win four titles in four consecutive years. Taylor, Ruth and Brooks all either redshirted or needed a fifth year to earn their four titles.

The Nittany Lion had a tough battle with Nebraska’s Christopher Minto in the Big Ten title match. It was a rematch of their match in the BJC earlier in the season, one that got a little chippy. In this match, Haines was the beneficiary of an illegal hold, allowing him to win 2-1.

“He’s a consistent, tough kid, and obviously he’s a great wrestler, and he’s gotten better and he wrestles a hard pace, he’s good in every position,” Sanderson said.

Ahead of the finals, Haines was preparing to enter into the BJC one final time in his illustrious Penn State career. Sanderson kept the perspective and focal point at the task at hand, with eight Nittany Lions having the opportunity to win a title.

“The finals were about to start, and in four hours — we’re rolling the mats up,” Sanderson said. “So let’s take advantage of this and go get what we want.”

Haines closed out his home arena debut the same way he began it — victorious. The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native received a standing ovation after his championship win, as the fans showcased their appreciation for what Haines has done and could still do in his four-year career.

It’s not something that comes around too often, and for a team that doesn’t often look at titles like most, the accomplishment allowed Sanderson to appreciate the efforts from his 174-pound wrestler.

“We want to get what you can in the short amount of time you have to compete, and that’s something that Levi’s done,” Sanderson said. “Win four Big Ten Championships, that’s a pretty darn cool thing to do.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball set to take on Georgetown for a two-game series, ahead of conference opener against Iowa
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
On Tuesday, Penn State will travel to the nation’s capital to compete in a two-game series against Georgetown, three days ahead of its conference opener against Iowa.

The blue and white have yet to play a conference match; that begins next week against the Hawkeyes. The Nittany Lions are coming off three tough losses against Texas Tech, making them 4-9 overall so far.

For each game against Texas Tech, Penn State lost by a margin of at least eight runs, losing game one 15-7, game two 21-6, and game three 14-4.

While it seemed that Penn State’s successful hitting runs only happened in spurts, the loss was earned mainly through pitching.

Colin Fitzgerald had to be removed after the first inning due to the six runs allowed in his start, and relief appearances couldn’t improve on the outing.

The next two matchups between the Red Raiders and Nittany Lions saw similar storylines. The second game, Penn State allowed Texas Tech to come out strong in the first inning and go on a run. The Red Raiders put up seven of their 21 total runs just in the first inning.

From then on, the Red Raiders outnumbered the Nittany Lions’ runs in each inning, until the game was concluded in the seventh inning due to the mercy rule.

Match three was no different, as pitching continued to struggle in the 14-4 loss. This game was also cut short in the seventh inning.

Because the Nittany Lion defense continued to give up runs early on, it made it difficult for the offense to answer.

As Penn State now heads into the two-game series against Georgetown, it must figure out a way not to allow the Hoyas a lead in the first inning.

When the blue and white allow early leads to creep in, they continue the game at a disadvantage and lose the needed momentum on defense to put themselves in a position to eventually score on offense.

Georgetown also has yet to play a conference match and is now 10-6 overall on the season. The Hoyas are coming off a three-game series against George Mason University, where they picked up a loss and a pair of wins.

While they lost by five runs in their recent loss, the Hoyas only won by one run in each of their wins.

Prior to seeing George Mason, Georgetown has seen mixed results on the season.

Games against Winthrop University and Maryland saw blowouts for the Hoyas. However, there were several games this year in which Georgetown was the offensive dominant team, even shutting out St. Peter's 13-0.

In matches against FDU and George Mason, the Hoyas were neck and neck with their opponent, with the exception of a single nine-run win against FDU.

With similar previous experiences this season, it seems to be a game of hitting for both Penn State and Georgetown as they enter this series.

While rocky pitching has played a major role in self-inflicted damage for both teams, their ability to hit early and then go on a run has been key to their victories.

This game is a crucial confidence builder for Penn State heading into conference play, as that begins next week. Georgetown, however, will wait until April 2 to start conference play against Xavier University.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s volleyball continues California road trip against No. 6 Pepperdine
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State men’s volleyball recently ventured north of 2,500 miles out west to visit No. 2 Long Beach State, just to get swept.

The Nittany Lions stick around in California for two more matches – the first of which is a 9 p.m. showdown against No. 6 Pepperdine in Malibu.

Hot streak, meet hot streak

The two opposite-coast teams have both been on a tear lately, except for a single falter from each unit.

Penn State has won four of its last five matches, with the lone loss coming to the Beach just a few days ago. That defeat aside, the blue and white were winners against Merrimack, Harvard twice and Princeton.

Mark Pavlik and co. won three of four matches by way of sweep, but will have a much harder time doing that against the Waves – winners of four of their last five as well.

Pepperdine too enters Wednesday’s match coming off a loss to No. 3 Hawaii, but won its first of the series against the Rainbow Warriors.

The Waves – prior to squaring off against Hawaii – won their series against Jessup and a single-game home stand against Lincoln Memorial, only surrendering one set across the three matches.

While both squads have been on a roll, something’s gotta give come Wednesday.

Psycho killers

Pepperdine sophomore Cole Hartke has the second-most total kills in the nation, with 226, but leads in kills per set, averaging 4.56.

A second-team All-American a season ago, the 6-foot-11 Hartke tallied a season and career-high mark of 29 kills against Lewis back in January. He ended the night with 35 total points.

Hartke’s efforts alone are not enough to put Pepperdine in the No. 1 spot of total team kills per set, though, as it ranks 15th, recording 12.4 kills per set – putting it 10 spots higher than Penn State, which averages 12 flat.

Teammate Ryan Barnett lags behind with 160 on the season, averaging 2.96 per set. Barnett was a first-team All-American last season after leading the team in kills with 402. He recorded 26 kills in his squad's two matches against Hawaii.

Nittany Lion kill leader Sean Harvey has 174 on the year, has the fourth-best kills per set mark in the EIVA with 3.35 and has the second-best hitting percentage with .294 – sitting only behind teammate Tristan Hassell with a .323 mark.

Hassell is the blue and white’s second-highest kill-getter with 96 on the season, averaging 2.94 per set.

The freshman has popped off at times, including a 17-kill night against Princeton in February, but could only muster two against the Beach.

Best of blocking

Penn State ranks first in the EIVA in total blocks with 155.5, averaging 2.83 per set.

The Nittany Lions can attribute a lot of their productivity to middle-blocker Owen Rose, who also leads the EIVA in blocks with 76 and in blocks per set, averaging 1.41.

The two-time first-team All-EIVA honors recipient and second-team All-EIVA man a season ago is third in the nation in total blocks and second in blocks per set.

6-foot-7 Rose recorded three blocks against Long Beach State and notched a career-high mark of 14 total blocks against Harvard in February.

Similarly, Pepperdine is one of the nation’s best when it comes to blocks, as it records 2.6 per set and possesses 141.5 total.

Middle-blocker James Eadie leads the way in those efforts with 58 total blocks on the season, averaging 1.07 per set.

The Newport Beach native has 93.5 points on the season as a result – the fourth most on Pepperdine.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State DE Odafe Oweh agrees to deal with Washington Commanders
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Odafe Oweh is headed back to the DMV.

The Washington Commanders have agreed to terms with Oweh, who will be getting a 4-year, $100 million contract, including $68 million in guarantees, per Ian Rapoport.

Oweh made a name for himself during his short stint with the Los Angeles Chargers, tallying 7.5 sacks after he was traded by the Baltimore Ravens. He had three sacks in Los Angeles' playoff loss to the New England Patriots.

The former Nittany Lion was drafted 31st overall by the Ravens in the 2021 NFL Draft. Oweh tallied 23 sacks during his time in Baltimore.

Oweh played at Penn State from 2018-20, earning first team All-Big Ten honors after his redshirt sophomore year.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey falls in both USCHO poll, NPI
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has dropped in both the USCHO poll and NPI rankings after a disastrous weekend against then-No. 11 Wisconsin.

The Nittany Lions fell into a tie with the Badgers for the 10th spot in the USCHO poll, USCHO's official X account tweeted Monday. Penn State was previously ranked sixth, dropping four spots in the poll.

pic.twitter.com/CkNFi5TsWB— USCHO.com (@USCHO) March 9, 2026

Additionally, the Nittany Lions dipped to No. 11 in the NPI rankings, which ultimately determines the teams that qualify for the NCAA Tournament. Penn State was rated sixth before being swept last weekend by the Badgers.

Regardless, the Nittany Lions still hold a 99.8% chance to make the NCAA Tournament, and an 88% chance to earn an at-large bid, according to College Hockey News' probability matrix.

Penn State looks to improve its odds of making the bracket on Wednesday, as it hosts Minnesota, which sits 37th in NPI, in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament.
