Penn State’s penalty kill unit stood strong, defending every breakout rush with suffocating pressure on the puck carrier, successfully ending the man advantage.

The horn soon sounded after the kill with the Nittany Lions ahead 1-0.

Second period

It was Katie DeSa’s turn to face instant offensive pressure as Mercyhurst came out firing to begin the second period. The Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native positioned herself with the shooter and made a few pad saves to keep the score 1-0.

Penn State generated chances as the middle frame neared its halfway point. Abby Stonehouse found herself one-on-one with a defender. The Bienheim, Ontario, native attempted to toe drag past the defenseman; however, was quickly shut down before getting past the skates.

Most of the first ten minutes were played closely by both defenses. The two squads had an answer for every threat of pressure put on by the opposition.

However, the blue and white were able to cash in on a quick developing play in its offensive goal. Sophie Morrow sent a wrist shot from the top of the faceoff circle that was redirected by Nicole Hall. The Stockholm, Sweden, native beat the left pad of Luggin to make the score 2-0.

The third line was responsible for the first two goals for Penn State in the championship game.

The Nittany Lions weren’t done scoring. After a penalty by Payten Evans put Penn State on the power play, Katelyn Roberts ripped a wrist shot from the slot that went five-hole and into the net, extending the lead to 3-0.

The horn sounded, putting an end to a two-goal second period for the Nittany Lions. 20 minutes remained in the championship game.

Third period

The Lakers opened up the final period of play, scoring their first goal. Abby Poitras found the loose puck behind DeSa’s net and scored a wrap-around goal.

Penn State tightened up defensively after letting up the early third-period goal. That was until miscommunication on the blue line led to a breakaway for Sophie McKinley that was denied by DeSa.

This opportunity for Mercyhurst sprouted chances on both sides of the ice as the pace of play began to ramp up with under 10 minutes left in the game.

DeSa had to remain dominant in net to hold off the late surge of offense produced by the Lakers. Mercyhurst directed a total of 16 shots at the netminder in the final period.

Luggin was pulled from her net with just under three minutes left in the game. The Lakers had two minutes to net two goals in order to keep their championship hopes alive.

It was Berlin Lolacher who scored the second goal for Mercyhurst with just over a minute left, shortening the lead to one for the blue and white.

However, it was too little too late as the Penn State faithful rose to their feet after the final 60 seconds began to fade off the clock. The horn sounded and the Nittany Lions were AHA champions once again.

Up Next

Penn State will play in the quarterfinal round of the NCAA Women’s Ice Hockey tournament on March 14.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball’s offense falters, loses first game at Hokie Invite against JMU
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After going 2-0 on day one of the Hokie Invite, No. 24 Penn State had an opportunity to carry its momentum against JMU on Saturday afternoon. The Dukes entered the contest at 10-10 and were fresh off a win over Bryant earlier in the day.

Despite being red-hot offensively this season, the Nittany Lions’ bats were mostly shut down and they fell 5-3 against JMU.

Right-hander McKenna Young started her second game in two days and swiftly retired JMU’s batters in the opening frame. One of the keys to the blue and white’s early-season success is striking first and the squad continued that trend.

The scoring started with right-hander Payton List hitting leadoff shortstop Kaylie Walters. Center fielder Natalie Lieto then smashed a double into right center and scored Walters from first. Despite Penn State leaving Lieto stranded in scoring position, Young now worked with an early 1-0 lead.

However, it wouldn’t take long for the Dukes to knot the game right back up as Bella Henzler led off the second with a solo shot into left. A couple of batters later, Kendra Lewis blasted a ground-rule double into left-center. Yet, Young minimized the damage and JMU settled for just one run in the inning.

The Nittany Lions went down quietly in their half of the second with List picking up two strikeouts and coach Clarisa Crowell turned to her bullpen in the third. Right-hander Abigail Britton delivered a pivotal shutdown inning and Penn State’s bats soon went to work.

Lieto led off the bottom of the third frame with a bang and smothered a solo dinger over the batter’s eye into center, giving Penn State a 2-1 lead. Yet, the Dukes took the lead right back in the fourth.

Emily LeGrand and Bella Henzler started the momentum with back-to-back singles. List then helped herself on the mound and dropped a single into right. Right fielder Cara Bohner fumbled the play, which plated LeGrand and allowed List to advance into scoring position.

With runners on second and third and still no outs, Britton was in a jam. However, the LaVale, Maryland, native allowed just one more run to the plate. After Lewis struck out, Kira Ortega gave JMU the 3-2 lead off a sacrifice fly to center.

After the blue and white couldn’t get much offense going, Crowell brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris. The Olathe, Kansas, native allowed a few singles in the fifth, but kept the deficit to just one run.

Bar a few bumps early, List was dealing against a tough Penn State offense. Later in the fifth, Lieto drew a one-out walk, then advanced to second off a wild pitch and third baseman Allison Oneacre followed with a walk of her own. However, Lieto was left stranded at third as Penn State’s big bats couldn’t knock her in.

The Dukes knocked in a pivotal insurance run in the sixth. Ortega lifted a solo home run to left center and extended JMU’s lead, 4-2. Meanwhile, the Nittany Lions were held scoreless once again in the bottom half of the inning.

Now in the late innings, Penn State felt the pressure down two runs. First baseman Gabby Gradishar led off with a walk and then advanced to second on a wild pitch. However, she was caught stealing third by JMU’s catcher Henzler.

While the Nittany Lions failed to get much going offensively, the Dukes added another run to their lead in the seventh. Cali Legzdin reached on an error by Walters at shortstop and Henzler knocked her in with a one-out single to left field.

The blue and white put together a rally in its last breath, but ultimately came up short and fell to 18-5 on the season with the loss. Lieto doubled down the left field and Oneacre scored her with a single into left center, but List didn’t falter and closed the game out.

Up Next

Penn State takes on its third-ranked opponent of the season against No. 11 Virginia Tech on Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey earns commitment from 2008-born forward William Yared
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added a piece to its future.

2008-born forward William Yared announced his commitment to the Nittany Lions on Saturday.

‼️🚨BREAKING🚨‼️The Penn State Nittany Lions have received a commitment from William Yared!🦁🦁🦁The ‘08 born Forward from Quebec has 25 points in 46 GP during the 2025-26 season for @SJSeaDogs Ranked the #163 NA skater by @NHLCentralScout for the 2026 NHL Draft pic.twitter.com/COwbUSI9Yr— College Puck NXT (@CollegePuckNXT) March 7, 2026

Yared is ranked as a 4.5-star recruit and is ranked by NHL Central Scouting as the No. 163 North American skater in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft. He is from Quebec, Canada.

The forward currently plays for the Saint John Sea Dogs of the Quebec Maritimes Junior Hockey League (QMJHL). Yared has 10 goals and 15 assists in 46 games played this season.

Yared, 18, could join Penn State as early as next year.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball to hire Calvin Booth in a consulting role
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is reportedly set to bring in an alum with NBA front office experience.

The Nittany Lions are hiring Calvin Booth in a consulting role to assist with roster management, according to Jon Sauber of the Centre Daily Times.

Source: Penn State men's basketball is hiring former Denver Nuggets GM Calvin Booth to assist with roster management.More here:https://t.co/f1jtDMwTr8— Jon Sauber (@JonSauber) March 7, 2026

Booth was the general manager for the Denver Nuggets from 2020-25. He was a part of the 2023 Nuggets team that won the NBA championship, but his contract wasn't extended after the 2024-25 season.

The former Nittany Lion was drafted in the second round of the 1999 NBA draft by the Washington Wizards. He played in the NBA until 2009, making stops with the Dallas Mavericks, Seattle SuperSonics, Milwaukee Bucks, Philadelphia 76ers, Minnesota Timberwolves and Sacramento Kings as well.

Booth, who played in Happy Valley from 1995-99, is Penn State's all-time career leader in blocks with 428. He made 114 appearances with the Nittany Lions, averaging 11.3 points and 6.4 rebounds per game across his career.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 14 Penn State dominates No. 9 Cornell, continues strong Ivy League run
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
After dominating Yale last weekend, No. 14 Penn State men’s lacrosse continued its Ivy League slate on Saturday, matching up with reigning national champion No. 9 Cornell at Panzer Stadium.

In their last meeting, the Big Red eliminated the Nittany Lions in the semifinals of last season’s NCAA tournament, where the blue and white fell 11-9 at Gillette Stadium in Foxborough, Mass.

The meeting also held a deeper meeting for coach Jeff Tambroni, who coached Cornell for ten seasons before coming to Penn State in 2010.

Tambroni would dominate his former team, beating the Big Red 19-7.

After a big save by Preston Hawkins, Kyle Lehman worked his defender, driving in front of the cage and burying the ball in the net to put Penn State up 1-0 early.

Following the ensuing faceoff, midfielder Michael Faraone drove over the top and fired a shot past Big Red goaltender Matthew Tully to double the blue and white’s advantage.

With just under nine minutes remaining in the quarter, John Jude Considine split down the alley and worked in front of the Cornell net, where he finished to put the Nittany Lions up 3-0.

Less than a minute later, Lehman found himself wide open on the left side of the net, allowing him to wind up and power a low shot past Tully for the second time, forcing a Cornell timeout.

Liam Matthews gave Penn State a 5-0 advantage with a bouncing shot that found the nylon as the Canadian attackman fell to the turf.

Moments later, Jon King picked off a Big Red pass and sprinted downfield, finding Jack Iannantuono on the fastbreak, the freshman firing a shot into the top right corner of the net to stretch the Nittany Lion lead to six.

After that score by Iannantuono, Lehman scored again on a nearly identical look to his second goal, securing the first-quarter hat trick with a shot from Tully’s left side.

Iannantuono scored his second goal of the game early in the second quarter following a great find from Chase Robertson, putting the blue and white up 8-0.

Cornell finally got on the board with 10:22 remaining in the quarter, with Ryan Waldman driving to the crease and finishing past Hawkins, cutting the lead to seven.

The Nittany Lions got it right back, however, when Iannantuono cut from behind the net and found Lehman, who matched his jersey number with his fourth goal of the contest.

Lehman’s career day continued after he received a feed from Andrew Beard on the doorstep of the Cornell crease, and finished again for his fifth goal of the game.

The Big Red got their second when Matthew Perfetto fired a shot past Hawkins with a flag down on the field, a penalty that would stay on the board due to its major classification.

That penalty would bear no fruit for the Big Red, and the game went into the half with Penn State leading 10-2.

Cornell opened the second-half scoring with a goal by Willem Firth on the end of a behind-the-back feed by Ryan Goldstein. Penn State mounted a quick response with Beard scoring on a close shot assisted by Robertson.

Firth got another for the Big Red, cutting the lead to seven on a pass from Brian Luzzi. Not long after, Lehman secured his sock trick on an unassisted goal that doubled his previous career high of three goals.

Senior defenseman Alex Ross got in on the Penn State point-scoring, assisting Matthews on a goal that the attackman would finish underhand.

Faraone scored his second of the contest with a wraparound dodge from behind the cage, tucking the ball past Tully to bring the Nittany Lion lead to ten goals. He got his hat trick soon after on another unassisted goal that brought the score to 15-4.

A turnover in the defensive zone put King on another fastbreak, then worked the ball to Lehman, who assisted Matthews to give the Canadian his hat trick as well.

The goals kept flowing in the late stages of the third quarter, as Beard found the back of the net on a low shot fed by Matthews to bring the lead to 13 as the frame expired.

Iannantuono joined in on the hat trick trend with a wicked low-to-high shot on the run that got past Tully and tucked under the crossbar.

The seven-goal Nittany Lion run was ended by Perfetto, and followed soon after with another Cornell goal by Luke Robinson.

Redshirt attackman Enzo Grieco scored his first collegiate goal on a feed from Wesley Scurci, a duo both produced by Mars Area High School just outside of Pittsburgh.

In the dying moments of the fourth quarter, Cornell got a goal from Goldstein, cutting the score to 19-7. That score would hold until the final buzzer, as Penn State secured a statement win over a strong opponent.

Next Up

The Nittany Lions will head to Charlotte for a neutral-site matchup with No. 6 North Carolina on Saturday at 6:30 p.m.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling begins Big Ten Championships with successful first session
author: Andrew Deal, AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 1 Penn State began its quest for another Big Ten title, this time in its home, the Bryce Jordan Center.

During the first session, nine Nittany Lions advanced to Saturday night’s semifinals, with the other still holding a chance to earn an auto bid to the NCAA Championships.

The Nittany Lions finished with 81 points in the opening session, leading the pack heading into Session 2.

Here’s how the first part of Day 1 went down for the Nittany Lions.

125 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs. No. 8 Dean Peterson (Iowa)

Luke Lilledahl stepped on the mat to a roar from the Penn State faithful and gave his fans a show.

After a couple of scrambles in the first period, Lilledahl tallied the first takedown in the second frame to regain the lead. The Nittany Lion controlled the match the rest of the way, notching another takedown in the third period.

Riding out Peterson, Lilledahl held the Hawkeye in the air and dropped him back down to the mat, solidifying his decision victory, 8-3.

133 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs. No. 9 Dylan Shawver (RUT)

Marcus Blaze started as the aggressor in the match, as he secured the first takedown of the match and took an early lead.

The freshman escaped to extend his lead in the middle period and continued to take down Shawver. That cycle repeated in the third period for Blaze, who continued to be the leader in action. The Perrysburg, Ohio, native earned four takedowns in the match.

Blaze dominated his way through Shawver, earning a major decision 16-3.

141 pounds

First round: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 11 Dario Lemus (MD)

Braeden Davis kicked things off for the Nittany Lions, wrestling Dario Lemus in the opening round.

Both wrestlers started with a feeling out process, with neither scoring points. Davis notched the first takedown part way through the opening frame, but got himself into a precarious situation. Lemus took advantage getting four nearfall points with Davis fighting to stay off his back.

Trailing 4-3, Davis immediately followed with a reversal to regain the lead. However, with just a 5-4 lead, he’d need to ride out Lemus. He did just that to end the second period, before riding Lemus for the entirety of the third, securing a 6-4 decision. His decision put the first points on the board for the Nittany Lions.

Quarterfinals: 141: No. 6 Braeden Davis (PSU) vs. No. 3 Vance Vombaur (MINN)

Braeden Davis conceded the first takedown of the match to Vance Vombaur. Davis responded with a takedown of his own, taking the lead. However, that was the extent of his offense.

Vombaur answered Davis’ takedown with one of his own before getting him on his back for four nearfall points. Trailing 11-5 entering the third period, Davis needed points quickly. However, Vombaur did exactly what he did in the second period, scoring a takedown and getting four more swipes for nearfall points.

Vombaur rolled to a 20-5 tech fall victory, sending Davis into the consolations.

149 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs. No. 8 Ryder Block (IOWA)

Shayne Van Ness and Ryder Block started off even. Both wrestlers took shots and it started the same way it did in their last match together, with Block earning the first successful takedown and leading 3-0.

Van Ness started to ramp up in the second period with an escape and a point due to a stall from Block. Block continued to stall and lost the match because of it, with Van Ness earning the victory 5-4 without any takedowns.

157 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 2 PJ Duke (PSU) vs No. 7 Brandon Cannon (OSU)

A matchup that was hyped up to be a title match, whether at the Big Ten Championships or the National Championships, took place in the quarterfinals.

Brandon Cannon found success early, taking down PJ Duke to secure the first points of the match. Duke, however, responded with a takedown of his own before notching another one before the end of the first period.

The strength of the Nittany Lion was on full display throughout the final two periods, as he rode Cannon for a good amount of time, notching a takedown in the second period before going on full attack in the third. Duke rolled to a major decision victory, 20-7, after three takedowns in the final frame.

165 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs No. 8 Paddy Gallagher (OSU)

Mitchell Mesenbrink did Mesenbrink things. The junior started off with a takedown after Paddy Gallagher had a few unsuccessful attempts. Mesenbrink got the takedown on Gallagher and rode him out for the rest of the first period.

There wasn’t much action from Gallagher throughout the rest of the match, as Mesenbrink didn’t really let him find a way to break through.

The junior continued to be on top of Gallagher and dominated the match with a 10-2 major decision victory.

174 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 9 Collin Kelly (ILL)

Levi Haines proved he’s the top wrestler in the country against Collin Kelly, as he scored the first takedown of the match.

The second period saw double of the first, as Haines notched two more takedowns and picked up a point from Kelly stalling. In the third, the Nittany Lion secured two final takedowns to get the tech fall win.

184 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs. No. 8 Angelo Ferrari (IOWA)

The boo birds came out when Angelo Ferrari took a knee, while the first period was a whole lot of nothing between him and Rocco Welsh. Ferrari took his knee, and Welsh couldn’t find a way to attack.

Ferrari earned an escape point and then whiffed on an attempted takedown on Welsh, who showed elite defense and didn’t allow Ferrari to get him down on the mat. The sophomore earned an escape and knotted it up at one.

Ferrari had a few attempts to takedown Welsh, but Welsh found a way out each time.

Welsh got the riding time over Ferrari and got the victory in tiebreakers 2-1.

197 pounds

Quarterfinals: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 8 Gabe Sollars (IND)

Josh Barr began his match quickly with an early takedown of Gabe Sollars. Throughout the rest of the first frame, Barr took down Sollars three more times, getting out to a 12-3 lead.

The second period featured more of the same, as Barr ended the match early after three takedowns, winning by tech fall, 21-6.

Heavyweight

First round: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 13 Gabe Christenson (NU)

Cole Mirasola attacked from the start, securing a takedown and getting the pin on Gabe Christenson in 25 seconds.

Quarterfinals: No. 4 Cole Mirasola (PSU) vs No. 5 Braxton Amos (WIS)

Cole Mirasola has had a trend of starting out in front and that continued in this match. Mirasola secured a takedown over the much larger Braxton Amos and took a 3-1 lead into the middle period.

The second period lacked action, with neither wrestler doing much offensively.

Mirasola escaped and extended his lead 4-1. Amos added an escape point in the third period, but couldn’t do anything else, leading to a 4-2 victory for Mirasola.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey's Reidler, Fleming deliver juxtaposing performances in loss to No. 11 Wisconsin
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a 7-3 loss to No. 11 Wisconsin on Thursday night, No. 6 Penn State suffered another lopsided defeat to the Badgers on Friday as it fell 5-2.

“They fought until the end,” Guy Gadowsky said. “That was important, because last night, that’s not what happened.”

Sophomore goaltender Kevin Reidler started the game strong, allowing a single goal in the first period and saving 14 shots from the Badgers.

The second period, however, was a much different story.

Less than 30 seconds into the frame, a shot from Gavin Morrissey ricocheted off of Cade Christenson’s back and past the Swedish netminder.

Just over a minute after the score from Morrissey, a strong feed from sophomore forward Ryan Botterill found linemate Oliver Tulk, who was waiting just in front of Reidler and tucked the puck past him.

And again, Wisconsin found its third goal in the first five minutes of the period when Blake Montgomery powered a one-timer from the Big Ten logo past the Penn State goaltender.

After the Badgers’ fourth consecutive goal to begin the matchup, Reidler was pulled by Gadowsky in favor of freshman Josh Fleming. The outing marked Reidler’s third straight game allowing four or more goals, and the second poor defensive performance in a row for the blue and white.

“The hook was made entirely to change the mojo,” Gadowsky said. “(He took it) like a champ. He said ‘I totally get it.’”

In relief, Fleming put in a shift that starkly contrasted that of Reidler. The Montreal, Quebec, native made 15 saves, good for a .941 save percentage on the evening.

“Flem’s one of the best goalies in the country,” forward Ben Schoen said.

Schoen’s point is valid, as Fleming ranked fifth in the country with a .931 save percentage entering the weekend, though that mark dropped to .924 after the Wisconsin series.

He’s had his struggles in recent games, including Thursday’s meeting with Wisconsin, where he was scored on seven times. He’s given up three or more goals in five of his last six appearances dating back to the January series with Michigan State.

Gadowsky declined to name his initial playoff starter after Friday’s loss to Wisconsin, but both netminders will enter the postseason on a negative note after the regular season’s final series.

Still, the team has full confidence in the tandem’s ability throughout a potential playoff run, one that will start on Wednesday when the Nittany Lions host Minnesota in the opening round of the Big Ten Tournament.

“Those guys are our backbone,” Schoen said. “Both of them have been terrific all season; we left them both out to dry both nights. None of it’s on them. It’s all on us.”

Last season, the Nittany Lions were riding high when the postseason came around, and the momentum culminated in an appearance in the Frozen Four. This year, they entered the playoffs on a three-game losing streak, and at what seems to be the lowest point of the season so far.

“Last year we were fighting for our lives from the start of January,” defenseman Jarod Crespo said. “That taught us that every game matters so much, and this year the mentality has shifted a little bit away from that. We need to get back to that mentality.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball pitching struggles early, begins Texas Tech series with loss
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State continued to produce in the hitting department after scoring 12 runs in its lone home game against St. John’s. The Nittany Lions hit the road once again after earning their fourth win in mercy rule fashion.

The blue and white headed south to open up a three-game series against Texas Tech. After giving up six runs in the first inning, Penn State was unable to mount a comeback, ultimately dropping Game 1 of the series 15-7.

Colin Fitzgerald got the start on the bump for Penn State. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native recorded 10 strikeouts and let up four earned runs in his last start.

However, the redshirt senior faced immediate struggles in the first inning against the Red Raiders. After the first two batters reached, third baseman Connor Shouse doubled in Kyeler Thompson to score the first run of the game.

With no outs and bases loaded, Shortstop Linkin Garcia doubled, followed by a single from Caden Ferraro, adding three more runs to the scoreboard. Matt Quintanar later added to the scoring after hitting a double down the left field line, scoring two more runners.

Fitzgerald’s outing came to a close after allowing six runs in the first inning. Mason Horwat came in to relief and ended the inning after one pitch.

Catcher Avery Smith took the six runs put up by Texas Tech personally as he opened the top half of the second inning, hitting his first collegiate career home run that went 328 feet. Three-straight outs ended any other answer the Nittany Lions had in the second.

Both sides were unable to record a hit following the homer, as it was the pitchers who stepped into the spotlight.

That was until left fielder Justin Turcovski’s hit slowly made its way into right field. The Nittany Lions were able to build off the one-out single after shortstop Preston Yaucher doubled in a run. However, a ground ball back to the pitcher led to miscommunication on the bases, resulting in a double play to close the first half of the fourth inning.

The Red Raiders managed to add to their six runs after Penn State was unable to draw up a double play to end the fourth. With the throw being late from second base to first, Shouse scored from third base. The Nittany Lions were able to get out of the fourth after a flyout, keeping the score 7-2 entering the fifth.

Penn State cut into Texas Tech’s lead after third baseman Bryce Molinaro hit a three-run home run to make the score 7-5. The Nittany Lions followed the homer with a two-out single that ultimately ended Lukas Pirko’s outing for the Red Raiders, and brought Jesse Rusinek in for relief.

The blue and white weren’t able to add any more runs on the reliever, ending the first half of the fifth down two.

Ben DeMell entered the game in place of Horwat. The redshirt senior was able to record an out before letting up two runs off a double from Thompson. Matt VanOstenbridge came in to relieve DeMell; however, the sophomore gave up a run against his first batter. Penn State ended the fifth with a strikeout, trailing 10-5.

The Nittany Lions went three-and-out to begin the sixth inning. This was not the same outcome for the Red Raiders in the bottom half. Ferraro sent a solo shot 434 feet over the center field wall to give Texas Tech an 11-5 lead to end the sixth.

Following a scoreless first half of the seventh inning from Penn State, the Red Raiders added two runs to their score off the reliever Frankie Sanchez.

The Nittany Lions found themselves back on the scoreboard after bringing in two runs off of Bryce Suiter, who replaced Jackson Burns.

The bullpen woes continued for the blue and white in the bottom of the eighth. Reliever Ship Shenosky allowed back-to-back home runs, making the score 15-7 as Penn State had one more chance to rally back.

Three straight batters were sent down in order to close out the game.

Penn State gave up 17 total hits and went to its bullpen five times in the loss. A complete opposite display compared to its recent home win and previous Fitzgerald start.

The Nittany Lions showed signs of offense through a pair of home runs and base hits in an attempt to mount some sort of comeback. However, after any sign of offense from the blue and white, the Red Raiders shut it down by answering with a couple of runs of their own.

Up Next

Both teams will return to Dan Law Field at Rip Griffin Park at 3 p.m. on Saturday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball continues series in Old Dominion State
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State softball returns to the dirt to face off in the Hokie Invite for a two-day, four-game series after cancelling their home tournament due to inclement weather.

The blue and white started their trip to the Old Dominion State playing against Radford. Shutting down the Highlanders in five innings, the Nittany Lions dominated in the dirt with a 15-4 victory.

Quickly after a powerful performance by the blue and white, they faced off against the Boston College Eagles for the second time this season. Earlier this season, Penn State closed off its second game of the season, shutting out the Eagles, 7-0.

For the second time this season, the Nittany Lions defeated Boston College, 8-3, to start off the series undefeated.

Continuing their weekend on the dirt with their first matchup against James Madison and Virginia Tech this season, the blue and white have a chance to keep the results rolling.

James Madison

The Nittany Lions continue their trip to Salem, Virginia, taking on James Madison for their seventh meeting in program history. The Dukes led the Nittany Lions 4-2.

Penn State looks to avenge its four-game losing streak against James Madison. The last time they defeated the Dukes was in 2012, when they won 5-4.

A current 9-10 record, the Dukes hold on to their 2026 campaign. Going on a two-game losing streak, falling in their first match of the Hokie Invite against Boston College, 6-7. Then they were shut out by Virginia Tech, 7-0.

Junior, infielder, Kendra Lewis leads the Dukes with a .462 batting average and 1.663 OPS. Emily LeGrand leads James Madison with 13 RBI.

Right-hander Kirsten Fleet has an ERA of 4.54 and her high of eight strikeouts so far this season.

Virginia Tech

Penn State faces off against its second-ranked opponent of the season, host Virginia Tech Hokies.

Saturday’s matchup will be the eighth meeting between the Hokies and the blue and white in program history. Penn State trails 3-4, losing 6-3 in the 2025 season.

The last time the Nittany Lions defeated Virginia Tech was back in 2017, 7-1.

No.11 Virginia Tech looks for its 11th consecutive win this season. Having successful wins against the then-ranked Georgia. Beating the Bulldogs in back-to-back matches, 9-3.

For the Hokies, Jordan Lynch is at the top of the charts with a .484 batting average and has an ongoing hit streak of 14.

Pitcher Emma Mazzarone leads Virginia Tech inside the circle with single-game highs of 10 strikeouts and a pair of complete games.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse set for West Coast battle with San Diego State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is hot, and so is the weather it’s going to play in on Sunday.

The Nittany Lions travel west for a West Coast matchup against San Diego State, kicking off at 3 p.m. on Sunday.

With a 7-0 start to the season, here’s a look at the upcoming contest against the far-inferior Aztecs for their first-ever matchup in school history.

2026 Aztecs

Coming off a 5-11 season last season, this year’s San Diego State squad is on pace for a just as bad, if not worse, season.

This year, the Aztecs are 1-4 to start the season, struggling to improve on its attempt at an improved season.

After opening the season with a one-goal loss to Cal, San Diego State put up a dominant performance against FDU, as freshman midfielder Lilah Turley and freshman attacker Barrett Doan each recorded a hat trick in the team’s 15-5 victory.

However, their success was short-lived, as the Aztecs then lost two straight games, 12-10 to Oregon and 15-7 to East Carolina. Then a big loss to USC on Thursday.

Against the Ducks, San Diego State pulled ahead with an early 6-5 victory, but didn’t stand a chance as Oregon piled on five goals in the third quarter. Nearly two weeks later, the Pirates scored seven goals on the Aztecs, leaving them unable to fight back.

Redshirt senior Caitlin Boughton has been the go-to goalkeeper for San Diego State this year, but has recorded a struggling 41.1% save percentage, allowing 53 goals.

Sunday’s matchup

Boughton will have her work cut out for her on Sunday, as the Nittany Lions have two players with 30 points and two players with 20 points on the year, neither of which the Aztecs have.

Senior Payton Wainman and freshman Rocquette Allen have been dominant throughout the early parts of this season, each recording 20 goals. Junior Kelly MacKinney sits in third on the team in 13 goals, but also has 18 assists to go along with.

Leading the team in assists, however, is junior Delaney Radin, who’s tallied 25 assists in seven games. Against Colgate on Wednesday, she tied the individual single-game assist record as she recorded eight assists along with a goal.

In net for the blue and white is junior Sydney Manning, who started the season a little shaky but has since moved up to the third-best save percentage in the Big Ten.

In her last three games, the 2024 Freshman of the Year recorded 22 saves as compared to just 15 goals against, posting a 50.6% save percentage on the year.

Manning’s goalkeeping abilities will likely limit San Diego State's Keira Doyle-Odenbach and Mia Maechtel, who lead the team with a combined 21 goals and will need success scoring for any sign of hope against the hot Nittany Lions.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s hockey loses to Wisconsin, swept at Pegula Ice Arena
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State and No. 11 Wisconsin met in the 34th and final game of the regular season at Pegula Ice Arena on Friday night, concluding a historical five-month stretch for the Nittany Lions program.

The Nittany Lions, fresh off a 7-3 loss on Thursday, were looking to flip the script and salvage a series split against the Badgers.

However, in the annual “Wear White” game, Penn State dropped yet another contest, losing 5-2 to Wisconsin, and falling to 2-6-2 in its last 10 games.

“Yesterday, we were disappointed that we didn't fight to the end,” Penn State coach Guy Gadowsky said. “I thought tonight we did. We've earned a right to host through what we did in the year, and our job right now is to prepare as best we can for Wednesday night. Obviously, we didn't want the last home series to go like this.”

Here’s how the Nittany Lions dropped their regular-season finale, including an injury report and what’s next.

First period

Penn State-Wisconsin opened with plenty of back-and-forth action on Friday, but two problems that have plagued the Nittany Lions recently caused trouble — penalties and off-puck movement.

Two Penn State minor penalties tilted the ice towards the Badgers in the first period, and while Wisconsin didn’t score on the opportunities, it led to an onslaught of chances in the latter half of the frame.

Still, the Badgers were first to strike on Friday, in part due to a lack of off-puck movement by defenseman Jackson Smith.

At 8:23 of the first frame, left wing Christian Fitzgerald skated up the left side of the ice and beamed a centering pass to a cutting right wing Quinn Finley on a 2-on-2 rush.

Smith, who was skating backwards, was unable to reach Finley, as he redirected the puck past goaltender Kevin Reidler and into the back of the net, giving the Badgers a 1-0 lead.

That was all the scoring in the period; however, Penn State had plenty of high-danger chances, which included two breakaways. Forward Gavin McKenna had the latest opportunity after receiving a cross-ice saucer from forward Aiden Fink, but the shot was snared by Wisconsin netminder Daniel Hauser.

Second period

Wisconsin put a firm grasp on the game rather quickly to begin the second frame, tallying three goals in the first five minutes.

Gavin Morrissey, the Badgers’ leading scorer, fired a harmless point shot with minimal traffic in front of Reidler just 22 seconds into the period.

However, defenseman Cade Christenson, who skated through the goalmouth, was pelted on his backside with the puck, which ricocheted off him and into the back of the net.

Then, a minute later, left wing Ryan Botterill controlled possession at the top of the left circle, where he drilled a pass to center Oliver Tulk, who was waiting on the backdoor of Reidler. Tulk deflected the puck in to give Wisconsin a 3-0 lead.

Left wing Blake Montgomery capped off the trifecta after a one-timer from the point that cleared through traffic and zipped past Reidler, making it 4-0 Badgers.

“They’re a really good team, and they played very well in the structure,” alternate captain Jarod Crespo said. “And I think there were times where we broke the, down really well. And what you're talking about in that timeframe, we just weren't breaking them down. We weren't winning necessarily, the pump battles that we have. There's 50/50 battles. You got to win those, and at that point they were winning a lot of them.”

Immediately after Montgomery’s tally, Reidler was pulled for goaltender Josh Fleming, who started Game 1 on Thursday. Fleming’s presence was felt early, making a few key stops.

After a lopsided first half of the frame, left wing Shea Van Olm gave the packed Pegula Ice Arena crowd a jolt, after he ripped a one-timer from the top of the right circle bar down, cutting Penn State’s deficit to 4-1.

The Nittany Lions built off that momentum quickly, as McKenna found himself in the offensive zone with a high-danger opportunity shortly after. McKenna dangled past Hauser from the right dot, where he leaked the puck into the slot. Center Reese Laubach backhanded the puck past Hauser, making it 4-2 Wisconsin.

Third period

Wisconsin scored early again in the third period. A beaming wrist shot by Van Olm in the offensive zone rang around the boards and set up the Badgers in a 2-on-1 rush. Botterill led it, finding Tulk for his second goal of the game, which gave Wisconsin a 5-2 lead.

With 9:49 remaining, Montgomery committed a five-minute major penalty and game misconduct for the second night in a row. And for the second consecutive night, the Nittany Lions couldn’t take advantage of it, coming out of the five-minute power play scoreless.

“I like how they approached the last 24 hours a lot,” Gadowsky said. “I think they recognized the fact that we didn't fight to the end. I think they're disappointed by that. They addressed it. Coaches addressed it. And the result, I thought tonight, from that aspect, was really good... We didn't win the game, but night and day difference of fighting to the end, and that's that is something that I don't think should ever leave Penn State hockey.”

Injury report

Penn State center Charlie Cerrato returned to the lineup after a nearly two-month absence.

Nittany Lions defenseman Nick Fascia is still listed as day-to-day after being cut with a skate against Ohio State two weeks ago.

Up next

No. 3 seed Penn State will host No.6 seed Minnesota at Pegula Ice Arena on Wednesday for the first round of the Big Ten Tournament. It’ll be a single-elimination game.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: No. 24 Penn State softball boasts another strong offensive performance, defeats Boston College
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fresh off a 15-4 win over Radford, Penn State took the field in Salem, Virginia, for a Friday night matchup with Boston College. This marked the second meeting between the teams this season, with the Nittany Lions previously defeating the Eagles 7-0 on opening weekend at the NFCA Leadoff Classic.

Penn State had another offensive outburst, defeating Boston College 8-3.

The Nittany Lions grabbed the bats first, facing right-hander Kelly Colleran. The Boston College pitcher got off to a fast start, striking out slugger Allison Oneacre on three pitches.

Center fielder Natalie Lieto knocked a double for the blue and white’s first hit of the game, but Colleran stood strong, keeping Penn State off the scoreboard in the opening frame.

Left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris got the starting nod for the Nittany Lions, allowing two singles in the home half of the first.

The Eagles had two runners on with one out, but couldn’t capitalize, keeping the game at a scoreless tie after the first frame.

As the first batter of the second inning, Breanna Hanik launched the first home run of her collegiate career to give the blue an early one-run lead.

With two runners in scoring position and no outs, first baseman Gabby Gradishar ripped a two-RBI single, bringing the Penn State lead to 3-0. A single from Oneacre brought Gradishar around for another run, giving the Nittany Lions a four-run advantage heading into the bottom of the second.

The blue and white defense finished its strong inning, setting Boston College in order to end the frame.

The Eagles made a pitching change, bringing left-hander Alyx Rossi to the circle to face the five, six and seven hitters in the Penn State order.

Hanik led things off for the second straight inning, this time with a double. The freshman couldn’t be brought around as Rossi settled in, keeping the Nittany Lions’ lead at 4-0.

In the bottom of the third, Boston College threatened to chip into the Penn State lead after singles from Kae DePerio and Emma Knight put two runners on with one out.

Kellepouris and the Nittany Lions’ defense escaped the jam to keep the shutout intact.

The blue and white added another run in the fourth inning after Oneacre launched a solo home run for the 10th long ball of her rookie season. Defensively, Penn State recorded another three-up, three-down inning to grab the bats once again.

Catcher Madison Seidel singled for the Nittany Lions in the top of the fifth, but nothing came of it as Boston College took over at the plate down 5-0.

DePerio and Nicole Riddell reached base to put runners on second and third with one out for the Eagles. Penn State went to the bullpen, bringing in right-hander Mackenzie Duncan.

A fielding error committed by shortstop Kaylie Walters off the bat of Knight brought both runners in as Boston College looked to chip away.

A single from Abby Ptak and a walk from Janis Espinoza loaded up the bases for the Eagles with two outs down three runs.

Duncan ended the threat, striking out Adriana Martinez to keep the Nittany Lions’ lead at 5-2 entering the sixth inning.

Walters reached on a single and Lieto on a hit by pitch to put two runners on for Brooke Klosowicz with one out. Klosowicz delivered, knocking a double to bring in Walters.

A Michela Barbanente bunt single and groundout from Hanik scored two more runs for Penn State as the Nittany Lions led 8-2, gearing up for the home half of the sixth.

Right-hander Bridget Nemeth came out to the circle for the sixth as the blue and white had two more defensive outings to close out its victory.

Another Walters error put DePerio on, but Boston College didn’t threaten any more as Penn State kept its six-run lead heading into the final frame.

Rossi and the Eagles’ defense stood strong in the seventh, posting a one, two, three inning. However, Boston College had some work to do in its final at-bat, still down 8-2.

Knight ripped a home run deep to center field to start off the inning and get a run back for the Eagles as quickly as possible.

The Nittany Lions’ defense recorded two fast outs, bringing up Espinoza as Boston College’s last hope.

Nemeth had other plans, striking out the Eagles’ second baseman to complete Penn State victory.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions open their Saturday slate at the Hokie Invite with a 1 p.m. meeting against JMU.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball starts off Hokie Invite with huge win over Radford
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Penn State was forced to cancel its home invitational because of inclement weather, the Nittany Lions traveled to Virginia for the Hokie Invite and faced Radford in their opening game on Friday afternoon.

Despite having its winning streak broken, the blue and white jumped another spot in the rankings at No. 24 after a successful 3-2 weekend at the Tennessee Invitational. Meanwhile, the Highlanders entered the contest with just a 6-11 record and would have their hands full with shutting down Penn State’s blazing hot offense.

The Nittany Lions started the weekend strong and routed Radford with a 15-4 win behind another impressive offensive outing.

It didn’t take long for the Nittany Lions’ bats to get rolling as they jumped on junior right-hander Morgan Cooper in the first and plated seven runs. Third baseman Allison Oneacre reached base off a hit by pitch, then center fielder Natalie Lieto laid down a bunt single.

With no outs, designated player Brooke Klosowicz launched a two-run home run to center, giving Penn State a 3-0 lead. Second baseman Michela Barbabente, catcher Macy Chamberlin and right fielder Cara Bohner all drew walks, setting up bases loaded with just one out,

The Highlanders turned to their bullpen and brought in sophomore right-hander Dakota Redman to face the jam. However, the Nittany Lions just kept raking. Pouring it on early, left fielder Breanna Hanik cleared the bases with a triple into right center and quickly ballooned the lead to 6-0.

The blue and white tacked on one more run in the opening frame when shortstop Kaylie Walters scored Hanik off a groundout. In a massive hole already, Radford chipped away at the lead by plating a run in its half of the first against right-hander McKenna Young.

Amber Wolfe powered a two-out double into right field and Hailey Scott drove her in with a single into left field. Despite Scott advancing into scoring position off the throw into home, Young escaped the inning with no further damage.

Both squads were kept quiet in the second, but the blue and white’s bats exploded again in the third. First baseman Gabby Gradishar reached with one out off an error by first baseman Abby Bossler, and quickly plated after Bohner’s single into center was fumbled by center fielder Scott.

Hanik followed with a single of her own and scored Bohner from third, extending Penn State’s lead to 9-1. After Hanik advanced to third off a stolen base and a wild pitch throw by Redmon, Walters drew a walk and swiped a bag.

With runners in scoring position, Oneacre reached base off a routine groundball to shortstop Grace Bechtol and scored Hanik. All runners were safe off the play which allowed Lieto to plate two more runs off a smacked single into right.

The Highlanders turned to right-hander Natalie Jones, but she also couldn’t stop the bleeding. Klosowicz totaled her fourth RBI of the day with an infield single, extending Penn State’s lead even further, 13-1.

Young kept dealing and the Nittany Lions added another run in the fourth. Brevyn Kellepouris hit an infield single then advanced into scoring position off an error by second baseman Layla Miles.

Walters then advanced Kellepouris to third with another infield single to the left side of the diamond. Kallie Karwowski plated Kellepouris with a bunt to the pitcher, making the score 14-1.

However, Radford would pour in three runs of its own later in the inning. After allowing one run in the first, Young was smooth sailing through the next few frames. Amanda Lee broke the offensive drought, smothering a one-out double into left center.

Facing her first scoring threat in a while, Young walked and then hit the next two Highlander batters. Jada Holt then reached on a fielder’s choice to first baseman Gradishar and plated two runs.

Grace Hogie scored another run off a groundout to second baseman Barbabente, pulling the Highlanders slightly closer, 14-4. Yet, the Nittany Lions got one run back in the fifth off some small ball and still held a large lead.

Young pitched the whole game and closed the contest out in the bottom half of the inning. The Brodhead, Wisconsin, native allowed just five hits, four runs and accumulated five strikeouts over her five innings of work.

Up Next

Penn State continues its slate of games at the Hokie Invite and faces Boston College at 6 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball announces spring schedule
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
While the Nittany Lions’ season ended last in December, they are still here and ready to play in April.

Mark your calendar 🗓️✌️ chances to see the 🦁🦁 this spring#WeAre pic.twitter.com/wlNrQiy3hQ— Penn State Women’s Volleyball (@PennStateVBALL) March 6, 2026

Mark your calendars because they are set to go against Villanova on Saturday, April 11th.

The Nittany Lions have dominated their limited matchups against Villanova, winning 4-3 in the all-time series, with notable victories, including their last 3-0 sweep on August 29, 2015.

Penn State dominated Maryland in their most recent matchup against the Terrapins 3-0 on Senior Day at Rec Hall on November 23, 2025.

And fans get another chance to see the Nittany Lions on the court again to face the Maryland Terrapins, seeking revenge, the following weekend, Sunday, on April 19th.

Both games are set for the first serves of the matches at the Rec Hall, University Park, PA.

MORE WOMEN’S VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling duo has seedings adjusted for Big Ten Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After much controversy over the pre-seeding of the Big Ten Championship, two Penn State wrestlers saw their seed adjust.

Levi Haines has jumped to the No. 1 seed following the initial pre-seedings, while Braeden Davis jumped from No. 7 to No. 6.

Haines was listed as the No. 2 seed in the pre-seedings behind Nebraska's Christopher Minto. Haines defeated Minto in the Bryce Jordan Center on Jan. 30 and was the consensus top-ranked wrestler at 174 pounds via the InterMat rankings for the whole season.

Now, the two wrestlers switch spots, with Haines holding the top seed and Minto now the No. 2 label.

Davis also jumped a spot, slotting him in at the No. 6 seed. He'll take on Maryland's Dario Lemus in the first round. During the dual season, Davis defeated Lemus by major decision in College Park.

Penn State now has seven No. 1 seeds, a No. 2 seed in PJ Duke, a No. 4 seed in Cole Mirasola and a No. 6 seed in Davis.

The Big Ten Championships begin at 10 a.m. Saturday for the opening session in the BJC.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: SEE IT: Penn State men's hockey's White Out game uniforms released
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State men's hockey's uniforms for the White Out game against No. 11 Wisconsin have been released via X.

⚪️🏒 SO. FRESH. SO. CLEAN. 🏒⚪️#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/AGSPehPj0c— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) March 6, 2026

In addition to keeping white helmets with the signature blue stripe down the middle, the Nittany Lions will wear white jerseys with light gray horizontal stripes, featuring a new logo in the center.

Instead of blue pants, Penn State adopted a fresh light gray color with blue and white diamonds stretching the length between the jersey and the players' shin guards.

Over their legs, the Nittany Lions will wear white socks with thin light gray stripes, echoing the jersey's sleeves and torso.

Penn State is set to face the Badgers in Game 2 of its series at 7 p.m. on Friday, aiming for a split after falling 7-3 in Game 1.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: SPA announces Laundry Day as Malcom Todd opener
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Student Programming Association (SPA) announced that the NYC pop-rock band, Laundry Day, will open for Malcom Todd at the Bryce Jordan Center on March 24.

Laundry Day is best known for combining genres such as indie pop, hip-hop and alternative rock. With over 300,000 monthly listeners on Spotify, their most popular songs include, “SUPERMODEL,” “I KINDA LIKE THAT” and “FRIENDS.”

University Park students can purchase general admission floor tickets and lower-bowl tickets for $20, and the general public can buy lower-bowl tickets for $45 on Ticketmaster.

title: Report: Ex-Penn State center Yanic Konan Niederhauser to miss rest of 2025-26 NBA season with injury
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Yanic Konan Niederhauser's rookie season in the NBA has ended early.

The former Penn State standout and current Los Angeles Clippers center endured a season-ending lisfranc injury in his right foot that will require surgery, according to Clippers beat writer Joey Linn.

The Clippers announce Yanic Konan Niederhäuser was diagnosed with a Lisfranc injury in his right foot that requires surgery.He is out for the remainder of the season.— Joey Linn (@joeylinn_) March 6, 2026

Konan Niederhauser picked up the injury during a game against the Indiana Pacers on Wednesday night. After eight minutes, in which he recorded six points, four rebounds and two blocks, he suffered the injury near the basket, and he couldn't put any pressure on his right foot as he was helped off the floor by trainers.

The 7-footer was showing some promise recently as he began to see more playing time after center Ivica Zubac was traded from the Clippers to the Pacers earlier this year. Konan Niderhauser had 11 points, nine rebounds and four blocks during a 114-101 win over the Golden State Warriors on Monday.

Konan Niederhauser logged 41 appearances this season, averaging 4.3 points and 2.9 rebounds per game with 64% shooting from the field. He was Los Angeles' first-round pick in the 2025 NBA draft last June, marking Penn State's first-ever player to be selected in Round 1.

The Switzerland native spent one season with the Nittany Lions in 2024-25 after transferring in from Northern Illinois. He averaged 12.9 points, 6.3 rebounds and 2.3 blocks per game, earning an All-Big Ten honorable mention.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 16 Penn State men’s volleyball prepares for ranked match against No. 2 Long Beach State
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 16 Penn State looks to take home its eighth-straight win against No. 2 Long Beach State at 10 p.m. Saturday from Long Beach, California.

Despite the recent streak of wins, Saturday’s late-night matchup won’t be an easy feat for the Nittany Lions.

The Beach enter the contest at 11-2 on the year, while also on a two-game winning streak with a 6-1 home record. The only two losses of the season were handed to the Beach by No.1 UCLA.

Not only that, but the Beach also entered this match following Wojciech Gajek earning the Big West Freshman Of The Week honor after a two-game series against CUI.

With a 2-0 sweep on the weekend, Gajek tallied 24 kills and eight service errors across both games.

But the duo of Owen Rose and Gaige Gabriel has been massive for the Nittany Lions on defense. With a combined 122 blocks on the year, the two have been a key piece to preventing the opposing attack.

As a result, the blue and white currently have the second-best block per set average in the NCAA at 2.856. Rose also ranks No. 2 in the individual category.

Rose has had quite the season with Penn State so far this year. The redshirt senior was awarded the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week award on Feb. 23, marking his fifth time receiving the honor. The middle blocker also set a school-record 14 kills against Harvard on Feb. 20.

However, the Nittany Lions have tallied significantly less service aces on the year when compared to the Beach. At only 74 aces on the year, Long Beach State bests the blue and white with 122.

The Beach lead the NCAA with the most aces, as well as the best aces per set average at 2.71.

Skyler Varga and Alex Kandev take the top two spots, respectively, with the highest aces per set averages in the NCAA. The two account for 73 of the 122 aces this season.

Varga was also announced to the AVCA National Collegiate Men's Volleyball Player of the Year Watch List earlier this season for his dominant performance thus far. He currently leads the Beach with 180.5 points on the year.

Penn State is also bested in hitting percentage. With the Beach at .372, they rank No. 4 in the NCAA. The blue and white trail with .276, putting it at No. 21 in the NCAA.

But the blue and white have Sean Harvey, who’s been a weapon on the attack all season. And with 201.5 points on the year, he holds the advantage over Long Beach State’s Varga in points this year.

The redshirt sophomore currently has 167 kills on the season and earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week honor following a two-game series against Princeton.

With both teams looking to keep their hot streaks alive and star-studded rosters on both ends of the court, Saturday’s game is bound to be an intriguing matchup.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Preview: No. 6 Penn State men’s gymnastics faces No. 5 Michigan in Ann Arbor
author: Jimmy Blair | The Daily Collegian
description:
The blue and white are coming off a 316.700-308.200 victory against No. 12 Springfield. This year, the Nittany Lions are 3-1 in meets and undefeated at home. Their lone loss came against No. 3 Nebraska in Lincoln. This includes a first-place finish at the West Point Open and a tie with Illinois for first at the Sam Mikulak Invitational.

Penn State has proven that the squad can consistently get results, no matter the level of the matchup. This will be huge going into a top-six matchup on the road against a conference foe in the Wolverines.

Coach Randy Jepsen will be counting on his top performers to come through in big moments in Ann Arbor on Saturday, especially Landon Simpson. The Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, native has been a consistent top performer for the blue and white all year long, including placing first overall at the West Point Open.

Kellen Ryan will also eye an elite performance in the top-six matchup, being one of the blue and white’s strongest pieces all year as well. With a first-place finish in the Individual Finals of the West Point Open under his belt, he’s eyeing a strong showing against the Wolverines on Saturday.

The reigning national champion Michigan Wolverines come into this matchup with an impressive resume under their belt. Their only loss this season came against top-ranked Oklahoma at home, 321.850-320.650. They are coming off a first-place finish in a Tri-Meet against Springfield and Arizona State.

The Wolverines have also defeated No. 7 Illinois in a dual meet, as well as a first-place finish in the Ohio State Open against Ohio State, Illinois and Greenfield.

Aaronson Mansberger will look to be a top performer for Michigan against the blue and white on Saturday. The sophomore from Carmel, Indiana, was a part of the 2025 Big Ten All-Freshman Team, and has won three straight pommel horse titles this season, including a career high of 14.900 at the Ohio State Open.

This will be a clash of two of the top gymnastics programs not only in the Big Ten, but in the whole country. The last meeting between these two sides was on Feb. 1, 2025, in State College, where Penn State took home the victory 319.000-313.900.

Following the dual meet with the Wolverines, Penn State will close its 2026 regular season campaign on Sunday, March 22, at home against No. 4 Ohio State. The blue and white will aim for victory against these two top-ranked conference rivals before gearing up for the Big Ten and NCAA Championships in April.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Column | Know yourself before you’re by yourself
author: Daniela White | The Daily Collegian
description:
As an avid overthinker who hated doing self-reflection and sitting in my own thoughts, I knew I was in for some trouble when leaving to go abroad.

I was never the type of person to journal, and when I did, I would keep a diary I wrote in for two days and would pick it back up every other few months when something pissed me off so bad I felt like I couldn't talk to anyone else about it. When I had yoga class in high school, I would nap during the meditation time instead of doing self-reflection and tuning into my inner emotions.

Before leaving for Barcelona, I knew I had to get over that hurdle, so I went on car rides after car rides, blaring music and just sitting in my thoughts. I thought of who I was as a person, what I wanted in life and what I regretted.

When I finally arrived in Barcelona, I was lucky enough to be coming with two girls I knew from Penn State; they acted as a safety cushion to fall back on if I didn’t meet new people to hang out with or felt homesick. They were a failsafe.

Of course, I didn’t fully rely on them. I met new people, exchanged contact information and started to put myself out there. But when you meet a great group of people, it’s hard to do things alone.

When the time came for us to travel, I found myself going along with the group. But I truly wanted to go off on my own for a little adventure, even if it was just for an hour or two. Then, the time finally came in Paris.

As an avid non-snoozer, I always found myself waiting for our big group to wake up from our late afternoon recharging naps. But this time instead of trying to wake them up quicker so we’d have more time to explore, I left.

I walked along the calm yet lively streets of Paris entirely by myself.

And while you could say this is nothing different than walking alone in Barcelona by myself — arguing they're both a foreign country I’m not from — I consider myself part of Barcelona right now. While I’m nowhere near being a local, I know my way around and have fallen into the customs. I know who to call if I feel lonely or need help and how public transportation works. But in Paris, I was all alone for that single hour.

When I was walking alone in Paris, I felt a new sense of independence flow through me. I was walking alone in a new city. I was able to do whatever I wanted, not needing to stick to the group’s itinerary catering to seven people’s interests. It was a liberating feeling and I instantly grew addicted, and a week later, I would be going on my first (partial) solo trip in Dublin, Ireland.

While I’ve had my share of relishing my alone time, being alone in a foreign area is another thing altogether. I learned you really have to know yourself to be able to travel alone for more than 24 hours.

After spending a day with my Penn State roommate, who’s studying at Trinity College for the semester, she left for her spring break. I was left entirely alone in a country where I didn’t know a single soul. All I had with me were my AirPods and my mind to keep myself occupied and with that left a lot of thinking and self-reflection.

Now, if I had taken this trip one year prior, I think I would’ve had a mental breakdown. I used to be the type of person who wallows in their feelings or pushes them deep down until they’re forced to the surface. It's hard enough to keep bad thoughts away when you’re alone in your dorm room or walking to class at University Park. But when you’re thousands of miles away from your main sources of stability and support, that level of discomfort is heightened a hundred times more than it already is.

I was nervous at first, I’ll admit, but then I learned it’s all about putting one foot in front of the other and finding peace in being by yourself. I put on some tunes sewn into the aesthetic Dublin had going on and strolled through St. Stephen’s Green, popped in and out of vintage shops and tried some fish and chips. I hopped on a train to a neighboring town of Howth and sat myself on a cliff that overlooked the Irish Sea at sunset.

While I would’ve had a great time exploring the city with friends, experiencing a new city entirely by myself was a liberating experience I’ll cherish forever.

When I played “Dreams” by the Cranberries while looking at the horizon in Howth, I took a deep breath, inhaling the freedom and quiet beauty around me. I enjoyed the serenity at my own pace and appreciated spending time with myself, only having my thoughts to keep me company.

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: No. 13 Penn State men’s lacrosse set to host No. 9 Cornell in rematch of 2025 national semifinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 13 Penn State rebounded from a one-goal loss to Navy with a 17-7 victory over Yale on Feb. 28 in its 2026 Panzer Stadium debut. Searching for their first winning streak of the season, the Nittany Lions will host No. 9 Cornell in Happy Valley at 1 p.m. on Saturday for their Youth Lacrosse Day game.

Penn State comes into the weekend with a 3-2 record, having defeated Colgate and then-No. 3 Princeton while losing to Villanova and Navy. The Big Red — defending their national championship — enter State College with a 3-1 record, most recently falling to No. 2 Richmond, 9-8, in Ithaca, New York.

The rematch

Penn State and Cornell had a pair of intense battles in the 2025 season, with the Big Red taking the more important victory, 11-9, in the national semifinals at Gillette Stadium. However, earlier in the season, the Nittany Lions managed to come back from a four-goal deficit to steal a 13-12 overtime victory on the road.

Cornell went on to defeat Maryland in the national championship game with an 18-1 overall record, the one blemish on its record at the hands of the blue and white.

Close games

Both teams have been a part of neck-and-neck scorelines throughout the early-goings of the nonconference schedule.

Penn State opened its season with a high-scoring, 18-14, victory over Colgate inside Hobula Hall before both of its losses resulted from one-goal deficits. Villanova upset the Nittany Lions in their second game of the season, 14-13, in overtime while Navy handed Penn State its first road loss, 12-11, in regulation time.

Cornell’s advantage is that it’s been able to close its tight game against an unranked team in the win column. The Big Red visited Albany — who’d been receiving votes in the USILA Poll going into the season — claimed an 11-10 victory as Willem Firth scored the game-winning goal with 34 seconds remaining in the fourth quarter. In Week 2, Cornell took down then-No. 16 Denver, 13-9, after being down, 9-7, early in the fourth quarter.

Age discrepancy

While Penn State features a largely youthful roster on attack, the Big Red’s offensive core is led by a pair of juniors in the wake of Tewaaraton Award winner CJ Kirst’s graduation.

Firth and Ryan Goldstein, both third-years, pace Cornell’s point production. Firth leads the team with 21 points, broken up between 12 goals and nine assists with three game-winning tallies. Meanwhile, Goldstein’s 18 points are split between eight goals and a team-high 10 helpers.

The Nittany Lions’ scoring effort is more spread out than Cornell’s. Sophomore attackman Hunter Aquino — the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year — leads Penn State with 17 points on 12 goals and five assists. Freshman Jack Iannantuono follows closely behind with 16 points, contributing a career-high six point performance against Yale.

Aquino, Firth and Goldstein were all named to the Tewaaraton Award watchlist.

Echoing the front, both teams’ goaltenders share the same age gap.

Matthew Tully, junior from Foxborough, Massachusetts, guards the crease for the Big Red. With a 3-1 record and an even 50 saves so far this season, Tully posts an impressive .588 save percentage and 8.90 goals-against average.

On the other end, freshman Preston Hawkins inherited former Nittany Lion Jack Fracyon’s net and has gradually improved over the course of the five games, currently holding a 2-2 record with a .443 and a 10.42 goals-against average. Backup goaltender Hayes Schreiner, a redshirt freshman, appeared only in the season opener, earning his first career win after replacing Hawkins midway through the second quarter.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball set to visit Rutgers to close out regular season
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Since Penn State’s season began on Nov. 3, it’s been a slippery slope of ups and downs as the Nittany Lions stumbled to the bottom of the Big Ten.

With the conference tournament looming, the blue and white have just one more test against a team that resides just a few spots above it in the Big Ten standings.

Penn State heads to Piscataway, New Jersey, to take on Rutgers in its final game of the regular season. Tipoff is scheduled for noon Sunday.

The Last Meeting

The Nittany Lions and the Scarlet Knights met earlier this season — on Feb. 18, — and it was the first time the blue and white were favored entering a Big Ten game.

Rutgers came prepared, though, and walked away with an 85-72 victory backed by guard Tariq Francis’ 22 points.

Penn State hasn’t been the favorite to win in any game since and won’t be against Rutgers on Sunday, with the Scarlet Knights currently being given a 63.4% chance of winning, according to ESPN Analytics.

“It’s hard, it’s frustrating, it hurts, but nobody’s coming to save us,” Mike Rhoades said following the Rutgers loss in February. “You just got to keep plugging away.”

Similarities, Differences

Rutgers has two more conference wins than Penn State, but the similarities between the two foes go beyond just their positions in the Big Ten.

With the exception of Maryland, Northwestern, Minnesota, Washington and Oregon, the Nittany Lions and Scarlet Knights have both lost to every other Big Ten team, with the blue and white coming off a blowout loss to Ohio State Wednesday and the Scarlet Knights coming off their second loss of the season to No. 8 Michigan State Thursday night.

Just like they do in the conference standings, the two teams sit right near each other in many Big Ten statistical categories, including the bottom two scoring defenses, two of the worst scoring margins and two of the worst field goal percentage defenses.

Penn State and Rutgers are also both at the bottom of the three-point percentage defense category in the conference – something that plagued the Nittany Lions in their crushing defeat at the hands of the Buckeyes who went 16-of-23 beyond the arc for a 70% clip.

One of the differences between the blue and white and the Scarlet Knights, though, is the rebounding.

Penn State sits dead last in offensive rebounding, while not doing too bad on the defensive end, sitting at 12th. Coincidentally enough, Rutgers sits dead last in rebounding defense and 13th in rebounding offense, so just about the same, just reversed.

With both teams struggling to knock down three-pointers, and also finding it near impossible to defend the deep shots, it feels like the team that can get it going from three-point land should be able to walk away with the dub.

Top Dog vs. Top Dog

Gone are the days of Dylan Harper and Ace Bailey — both so far having good seasons with the Spurs and Jazz, respectively — so it’s up to Rutgers guard and leading scorer Francis, who leads the Big Ten in free throw percentage, to contain Penn State leading scorer Freddie Dilione V.

Dilione, who has struggled at times this season, emerged as the Nittany Lions’ go-to scorer due to guard Kayden Mingo catching the injury bug from time to time.

Mingo, currently battling an undisclosed injury, is considered “day-to-day,” but it feels unlikely for him to suit up on Sunday, seeing as there’s not much to play for.

Francis is also a top-10 scorer in the conference and has had three 30 point performances this season, with two against Big Ten foes Northwestern and Oregon, two wins that can be greatly attributed to his efforts.

“Another thing is letting a good player get going early,” Rhoades said after the Rutgers game earlier this season. “When you see that ball go in early, that rim gets bigger and bigger.”

Francis’ 22 points against the Nittany Lions back in February matched Josh Reed’s 22 points from that night, which at that point was the senior’s career high.

With Penn State having trouble letting games “snowball” out of control at times this season, as Rhoades mentioned in his press conference following the Ohio State blowout, it must be wary of Francis’s scoring abilities while also limiting his passing options.

The leading assist-getter for the Scarlet Knights can set up those three-point scoring opportunities like it’s nothing and if the blue and white doesn’t limit those passing lanes, things might get out of control for the Nittany Lions.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Staff predictions | How Penn State wrestlers will perform at the Big Ten Championships
author: The Daily Collegian Wrestling Staff
description:
Postseason wrestling is back for Penn State.

With the Big Ten Championships set for this weekend at the Bryce Jordan Center, the Nittany Lions will have an opportunity to capitalize against conference competition, both as a unit and as individuals.

Here’s how our staff thinks Cael Sanderson’s team will perform this weekend.

Lyle Alenstein: 6 individual champions/team title

Winning the team title should be a lock, but I believe there'll be six wrestlers who win individual championships to contribute to that victory.

Those Nittany Lions will be Luke Lilledahl, Shayne Van Ness, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines, Rocco Welsh and Josh Barr.

Van Ness, Mesenbrink and Barr will cruise to their victories due to a field that isn’t as competitive as the others. Lilledahl and Welsh have a few guys in their brackets that could possess a challenge, but the tandem had been wrestling great to end the year and should stay hot come this weekend.

Haines, who was ranked as the No. 2 seed for the tournament despite being No. 1 in the InterMat rankings all year and is undefeated, will have motivation he didn’t need. It should be a dominant showing for the 174-pounder because of this.

I think the talented freshmen duo in Marcus Blaze and PJ Duke are more than capable of bringing home a title, but will fall short this time around. Lucas Bryd, the reigning national champion from Illinois at 133, will beat Blaze in the finals while Nebraska’s Antrell Taylor, who also won the national tournament last year, will handle Duke in the title bout.

Much like the freshmen, I believe Cole Mirasola at heavyweight has what it takes to win it all, but won’t due to a talented field. Michigan’s Taye Ghadiali, Nebraska’s AJ Ferrari and Ohio State’s Nick Feldman are all more than capable of bringing home a title. Mirasola lost to Ghadiali and Ferrari, but beat Feldman.

Braeden Davis has arguably the best wrestler in the country in the 141-pound division in Jesse Mendez from Ohio State, who is competing with Mesenbrink for the Hodge Trophy. Davis has struggled as of late, and will be fighting for a spot to qualify for the NCAA Championships.

Andrew Deal: 5 Individual champions/team title

Relatively speaking, Penn State has a tendency to underperform in the Big Ten Championships, and then overperform in the National Championships.

But the question with this Nittany Lion team is, what really is underperforming and overperforming. This team has a very strong likelihood to break the national points total again. Lilledahl, Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr are my five selections for individual conference titles, which will lead to the conference championship.

Despite the stir-up with Haines’ seeding, he’s beaten all of the top competition in his bracket and is the heavy favorite. Lilledahl, Van Ness, Mesenbrink and Barr all are No. 1 seeds and have wins over their respective No. 2 seeds, seemingly making them locks for a title.

The other wrestlers are where it gets interesting. Blaze and Duke are true freshmen and have tough potential matchups. Blaze would have to get past either Byrd or Ohio State’s Ben Davino to get a title, and I think he’s more poised for a potential national championship. Duke also has that same challenge, needing to get past Cannon and Taylor to stand at the top of the podium.

Welsh and Mirasola have a loaded weight class, and Davis has the other Hodge Trophy favorite in Mendez in his bracket. While Penn State certainly has a chance to get more than five individual titles, the five that I mentioned are the surefire picks to come out victorious.

AJ Piazza: 5 individual champions/team title

Penn State should be a lock to take home the team title. The Nittany Lions have seven title favorites, yet, I don’t see the team ending the Big Ten tournament with seven wrestlers taking home the hardware.

I believe Penn State has four locks to win Big Ten championships including Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr. The other Nittany Lion that I think will stand at the top of the podium is Welsh.

For Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr, they have dominated their weight classes, all remain undefeated and are ranked No. 1. I believe there’s a drop off in each weight class below these four wrestlers. The only challenge could be Blaze because it’s a matchup that hasn’t happened before, but I’d think Mesenbrink would still take the victory if it happens.

Welsh could have a tricky road to the finals as of right now there’s a chance he would face Iowa’s Ferrari in his first match after a bye. Ferrari was the No. 1 wrestler at 184 before Welsh defeated him, and Welsh could have a tricky final match against McEnelly, who Welsh has never wrestled.

For Lilledahl, Blaze and Duke I think there’s a chance they can win at the Big Ten’s, it just can’t be guaranteed. All three wrestlers have tougher tasks than other names mentioned. Lilledahl could have a rematch against Bouzakis, Blaze could face Lucas Byrd, a wrestler that he has never faced. Duke currently would have a matchup with Ohio State’s Brandon Cannon right out of the gate and could face Taylor again, who the freshman struggled with.

Mirasola is someone I could see surprising and wrestle better than expected, I just don’t believe he could defeat the top two wrestlers in his weight class. Davis has struggled tremendously in the second half of the season and I don’t see him going far in the tournament.

Evan Smith: 4 individual champions/team title

I believe Penn State has seven Big Ten title favorites, two Big Ten title contenders and Davis, who is in a separate tier. However, in a conference with so much parity, I predict some surprise losses.

I’ll start with the winners — Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr face weight classes where there’s no one I can see beating them. Haines not being the immediate No. 1 seed is simply ridiculous, as he will win with only a couple potential challenging matches.
