Nebraska’s Christopher Minto, who is the No. 1, would not be one of those matches.

For Mesenbrink, the only potential matchup that would interest me is Purdue’s Joey Blaze, as he’d wiped the floor with everyone else in the conference, including No. 2 seed Michael Caliendo. Van Ness and Barr have similar stature over their classes.

While my intro sounds harsh towards Davis, he’s going to run into a Mendez-shaped wall. Mendez is the Hodge Trophy favorite in my mind, and no one in the 141-pound bracket will change that. I expect Davis to be able to make a run to the semifinals if he gets past Rutgers’ Joey Olivieri in the first round.

Welsh faces the hardest weight class in this tournament despite the No. 1 seed. Iowa’s Angelo Ferrari can easily stall out a victory and Minnesota’s Max McEnelly is one of the best wrestlers in the country. I’m not confident enough to predict either of those men could shock Welsh.

Lilledahl also has a tough class and I think a healthier Nic Bouzakis of Ohio State can steal a victory. Marcus Blaze runs into defending national champion Byrd.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘We got to buy in’ | Penn State’s Reese Laubach gives alarming insight after team's loss to Wisconsin
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a blunt postgame media session with Penn State coach Guy Gadowsky on Thursday, center Reese Laubach walked into the media room alongside alternate captain Aiden Fink and caught everybody’s attention with his response to the first question.

“We got to buy in,” Laubach said. “I feel like guys are starting to check out, and I think we feel too comfortable. Obviously, we’re locked into the third seed in the Big Ten, but I think we’re getting complacent.”

No. 6 Penn State fell 7-3 to No. 11 Wisconsin at Pegula Ice Arena on Thursday night, dropping to 2-5-2 in its last nine contests after an abysmal third period, where the Nittany Lions were outscored 4-0 and outshot 21-6.

Laubach’s aforementioned words aren't what anyone wants to hear in Game 33 of 34 in the regular season — Penn State needs to make a change, and fast. The Big Ten Tournament starts on Wednesday, March 11, and the NCAA Tournament just 16 days after, if the Nittany Lions were to make it.

Laubach said it’s both an effort and focus thing, and that last year’s team made it to the Frozen Four because of how bought in they were.

“I don't need to say it, but the skill on this team now is light years ahead of the team last year we had and, yeah, I mean, you guys saw what we did with the team last year, right?” Laubach said. “We were one game, one goal, really, from playing in the national championship game. So, we know we can do it, and we know what it takes. It's just that little bit of hunger to do what the team needs you to do. Start playing for the team, not ourselves.”

Gadowsky and Fink said the team gave up at the end of Thursday’s contest. Once Wisconsin’s Adam Pietila scored to make it 6-3, there was a drastic shift in energy. The Nittany Lions would go on, after Pietila’s goal, to commit three penalties in the final six minutes.

That can’t happen this late into the season. The team needs to dig in as its approaching do-or-die time.

“I think you fight right till the end, and I think that was given up in my mind,” Gadowsky said. “I've been proud of this team for not taking the excuses of the lineup (being shorthanded) and always giving us the best regardless, not taking any excuses, but that at the end, felt like, ‘Ah, I have an excuse to be stupid because it's over,’ and I don’t like it.”

However, Laubach doesn’t feel like the season is slipping away. He said what is good about hockey is that things can change in an instant.

“Remember the feeling,” Laubach said. “Obviously, it sucks, but we got to flush it.”

This is likely superstar forward Gavin McKenna’s only season donning the blue and white and the futures of star players such as Fink and defenseman Jackson Smith are uncertain. It’s more important than ever for the Nittany Lions to capitalize off this year.

“Shoot, like if we don't figure it out soon — we got three games left, so we got one game tomorrow where we need to figure it out and bounce back, then we get into the biggest part of our season,” Laubach said.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Recapping Carolyn Kieger’s career with Penn State women’s basketball
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After back-to-back years missing out on the Big Ten tournament, Penn State is parting ways with coach Carolyn Kieger.

During her seven years with the Lady Lions, she recorded an 84-123 record with just one winning season coming in 2023.

Outside of her 22 wins during the 2023-24 campaign, the Lady Lions have struggled greatly under Kieger, with small growth during her first five years before hitting a cliff the last two seasons.

Kieger arrived in Happy Valley in April 2019 after 12 years under the leadership of Coquese Washington, who was recently fired from Rutgers. The blue and white recorded some of its best basketball in school history under Washington, including four straight NCAA tournament appearances.

However, the Lady Lions failed to make the NCAA tournament during Kieger’s seven years, and started out her first season with just seven wins.

As Penn State’s record grew from seven wins to nine wins to 11 wins, so did Makenna Marisa’s play. As arguably Kieger’s most successful player with the blue and white, Marisa grew from 9.2 points to 13.4 points to a career-high 22.2 points per game in the 2021-22 season.

With three straight exits in the first round of the Big Ten tournament, the Kieger project appeared to be unsuccessful during the first few years.

However, she was slowly assembling her squad in Happy Valley, adding Leilani Kapinus and Ali Brigham for the 2021 season and Shay Ciezki in 2022. That year, the Lady Lions had their most successful season since 2017, winning 14 games and reaching the second round of the conference tournament, nearly upsetting Michigan.

The following year, Penn State spoke of its Sweet 16 aspirations — a place the Lady Lions hadn’t been since 2014.

While things looked good to start the year, as Penn State opened with a 7-1 record, things quickly fell apart in the second half of the season. Following a season-ending injury to Tay Valladay, one of the team’s leaders, the Lady Lions lost six straight to fall out of NCAA tournament contention.

Despite picking up the 2021 Ann Meyers Drysdale Award winner Ashley Owusu, the Lady Lions couldn’t find success in the absence of Valladay.

After losing in the second round of the Big Ten tournament for the second year in a row, the blue and white earned a No. 1 seed and made its way to the tournament semifinals. The Lady Lions earned wins over George Mason, Belmont and Mississippi State before falling to Villanova.

The 2024 offseason was the first area of decline for Kieger, as she lost five players to the transfer portal — including Ciezki and Kapinus, who would’ve been the face of the Lady Lions the following year.

With low expectations, Penn State posted its worst record in four years as it won just 10 games during the 2024-25 season, including one conference victory.

The Lady Lions were led by former Bellarmine center Gracie Merkle, who dominated the court with her 6-foot-6 frame. But despite her and Gabby Elliott’s chemistry and Moriah Murray’s 3-point abilities, the blue and white lost seven straight games to enter 2025, before losing 10 straight to end the season and miss out on the Big Ten tournament.

Despite losing seven more players to the transfer portal and nearly Murray and Merkle, the Lady Lions entered the 2025-26 season with lofty expectations to make the NCAA tournament with the help of Rutgers transfer Kiyomi McMiller.

Once again, Penn State started the nonconference season strong with a 6-1 record, but soon fell apart as it lost 14 of its next 15 games.

While McMiller got hot to end February with six straight 30-point games — tying the Big Ten record — the Lady Lions missed out on the Big Ten tournament for the second straight year.

Throughout her time in Happy Valley, Kieger put together strong rosters with the 2023-24 Lady Lions and 2025-26 team, but couldn’t find success as Penn State began its search for the fourth head coach in Lady Lion history.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball heads to Virginia for Hokie Invite
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Heading into their final season-opening road series tournament, the Nittany Lions planned a last-minute trip to Virginia to play four games against nonconference opponents.

The blue and white had a 10-game winning streak until they faced No. 1 Tennessee. Going 3-2 in Knoxville, Penn State has a chance to regain momentum and continue its dominance on the field.

Host No. 11 Virginia Tech is one of the four opponents the Nittany Lions will face in the Hokie Invite, as well as Radford, James Madison, and Boston College for the second time this season.

Starting their weekend on Friday against Radford, then Boston College to close out their first doubleheader of the tournament. The blue and white are set to play their final two games against James Madison and Virginia Tech on Saturday to close out their weekend in the Old Dominion State.

Radford

Penn State heads back to the dirt for its 21st game of the season at 1 p.m. on Friday against the Radford Highlanders. This will be the sixth overall matchup that these two programs will have against one another, dating back all the way to their very first meeting in 1994.

The meeting last season at the Hokie Invite was the last time the Highlanders faced the Nittany Lions, who ultimately took the 5-3 win. Radford was the top-ranked team in the Big South Conference in 2025, going 14-4 within conference play and a 30-26 overall record.

A breakout star for the Higlanders has been right-handed pitcher Dakota Redmon. The Big South Conference announced Monday that Redmon was the Pitcher of the Week after her dominance in the circle this past weekend.

Earning a pair of wins for Radford against UConn and Liberty, throwing complete games for both matchups. Allowing a total of eight hits, two earned runs within both games, while striking out eight batters.

The Highlanders currently sit 5-11 overall in their 2026 campaign. They open their time in Salem, Virginia, against Bryant, then shortly after against the blue and white.

Boston College

For the second time this season, Penn State will face off against the Boston College Eagles.

The Eagles have been in a drought as they have lost six consecutive games, but facing North Carolina for an ACC weekend series, they were able to muster out a 10-2 victory to close out their weekend and drought. Handing the Tar Heels their very first loss of the season.

This win gave Boston College its sixth win of the season after falling short in 12 previous games.

The blue and white will meet the Eagles Friday night, with hopes of a redo of their second game of the season shuting them out 7-0.

Being their ninth overall meeting in program history, Penn State leads 7-1.

Junior pitcher, Kelly Colleran, threw a complete game, holding the Tar Heels to only three hits and striking out two batters. Catcher, sophomore Abby Ptak leads the Eagles with a .444 batting average and a 1.144 OPS so far in the 2026 season.

Penn State is set to face off against Boston College at 6 p.m. on Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s hockey drops first of final regular-season series to No. 11 Wisconsin
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Back home in Hockey Valley for the final series of the regular season, No. 6 Penn State’s battle with No. 11 Wisconsin held some high stakes Thursday night.

Heading into the weekend, the Nittany Lions had a chance to lock up their second consecutive trip to the NCAA Tournament, while the Badgers, currently sitting on the bubble, could make a strong bid for the postseason with a statement performance.

Wisconsin was the team that took advantage Thursday night, sealing a 7-3 victory to take Game 1 of the series.

“We’ve got to buy in, that’s all it is right now, I feel like guys are starting to check out, and I think we feel too comfortable,” alternate captain Reese Laubach said. “We’ve got one game tomorrow to figure it out and bounce back before we enter the biggest part of our season.”

The blue and white started the contest on its heels, with the Badgers winning the opening faceoff and controlling much of the offensive momentum through the first few minutes of play.

The Nittany Lions were first to the penalty box, after Jarod Crespo was called for hooking less than four minutes in, and Wisconsin's No. 4 ranked power play had an early opportunity.

Penn State’s penalty kill held strong, however, and limited the Badgers to three shots on the man-advantage.

The Nittany Lions picked up some offensive momentum after the kill, and just over a minute later, were rewarded for it.

Sophomore JJ Wiebusch found Cade Christenson with a centering pass, and the defenseman fired a shot to the top corner of Wisconsin’s net to give the blue and white an early 1-0 lead.

The lead was short-lived, however. Less than three minutes after Christenson’s goal, Quinn Finley skated in and fired a one-timer from the slot off a pass from Blake Montgomery to even the score for the Badgers.

Tyson Dyck was the next skater to the box, giving the blue and white its first powerplay of the night.

Penn State’s go-to on the man-advantage, freshman standout Gavin McKenna, has been on a hot streak as of late, earning his second Rookie of the Month accolade for his 14 points in the last six games, and that theme continued Thursday night.

Just 10 seconds into the power play, fellow freshman Jackson Smith found McKenna at the point, and the Hobey Baker nominee recorded his 14th goal of the season.

The three scores concluded the first period action, and the Nittany Lions held a 2-1 lead heading into the locker room.

Wisconsin came out of the intermission hot, and the game quickly took a turn for the home team.

Freshman Vasily Zelenov tied the game once again after sneaking a bouncing puck past goaltender Josh Fleming.

Less than two minutes later, forward Adam Pietila’s shot rang off Fleming’s post and created a rebound that Grady Deering batted out of mid-air and into the blue and white’s net to give the Badgers their first lead of the night.

The scoring slowed, and the next few minutes saw a back-and-forth exchange of penalties from both teams.

First, it was forward Ben Schoen to the box for roughing after getting tangled up with a Badger, but Penn State’s penalty kill kept a clean sheet.

Next, Montgomery was called for a 5-minute major and game misconduct after cross-checking Schoen up-high after the whistle.

The Nittany Lions’ full extended power play was quickly negated, after Matt DiMarsico headed to the box for interference 50 seconds later, leaving just over two minutes on the man-advantage after 4-on-4 hockey.

Both teams came up empty on the back-to-back penalties, but the blue and white ended the period on a high note. Alternate captain Aiden Fink, skating in his 100th career game, fired a wrist shot from the faceoff dot to send both teams into the second intermission tied at 3-a-piece.

“I actually thought it was a pretty good game for three and a half periods, and then I really don’t like what went on after that,” coach Guy Gadowsky said. “Thought we were in it, thought it was good hockey, like I said, and then I don’t like the last half of the third period.”

Sticking with the theme of early scoring, Wisconsin retook the lead four minutes into the final period, when senior Simon Tassy stuffed a wrap-around goal past Fleming's pad.

The Nittany Lions struggled to regain offensive control, and the Badgers doubled their lead three minutes later thanks to a goal from sophomore Ryan Botterill.

The next few minutes were a back-and-forth effort, but once Pietila tallied another goal for the Badgers off a pass in the slot from freshman Finn Brink, their win seemed all but imminent.

“We had a chance to win the game, and we made some errors, and then got stupid,” Gadowsky said. “That’s what I don’t like about it, I think you fight right until the end.”

Penn State’s frustration could be felt through the final minutes, adding both a too-many-men-on-the-ice penalty and a slashing penalty after the score.

Wisconsin’s third-period scoring concluded with sophomore Gavin Morrissey netting a power-play goal to seal the team’s 7-3 win.

Up Next

Penn State’s regular-season finale is slated for a Friday night rematch with the Badgers, with a 7 p.m. puck drop set at Pegula Ice Arena.

title: 6 individual Nittany Lions take home record-setting seven AHA women's hockey postseason awards
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State women's hockey's elite personnel set the Atlantic Hockey America (AHA) record of seven postseason awards, earned by five players and coach Jeff Kampersal.

Kampersal won the AHA Coach of the Year Award for the fourth consecutive season, leading the Nittany Lions to a 31-5 overall record, tying the program record for single-season victories that was set in 2025. The coach — in his 14th season at Penn State — led the team to four straight AHA Championship games, winning the previous three before the Nittany Lions face No. 12 Mercyhurst at 2 p.m. on Saturday at Pegula Ice Arena.

Captain Tessa Janecke, already a gold-medalist with Team USA in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games, took home her third Meghan Agosta AHA Player of the Year and AHA Forward of the Year awards.

Our captain is the 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐏𝐥𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐅𝐨𝐫𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 for the third year in a row 🤯Tessa becomes just the second woman in league history to earn the title for three consecutive seasons!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/W2KBX4dq9E— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 5, 2026

She was also named All-AHA First Team and a top-10 finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Award on the national scale. Janecke is now the sole Penn Stater to three-peat the awards and the first to do so since Agosta herself from 2007 to 2009.

Although Janecke missed eight collegiate games due to her Olympic duties, the Orangeville, Illinois, native leads the conference with 44 points.

Alternate-captain Kendall Butze won her second-consecutive Molly Bryne AHA Best Defenseman award, due to her contributing a conference-leading 27 assists, ninth in the nation. Currently with 93 career points, she is the program's all-time leader for points by a defenseman.

Kenny goes back-to-back as the 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐁𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐃𝐞𝐟𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧 🤩Butze becomes the first AHA defender to win the award two years in a row in over a decade!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/IzsqZ2x43N— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 5, 2026

Goaltender Katie DeSa earned back-to-back Brianne McLaughlin AHA Goaltender of the Year awards, previously being named to the All-AHA First Team as well. She holds a .942 conference save percentage with 11 shutouts nationally, leading the NCAA and most in a single-season by a Nittany Lion.

BRICK. WALL. 🧱🥅Katie is the 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐆𝐨𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 for the second season in a row!#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/XrbsCn8wBs— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 5, 2026

Freshman Danica Maynard took home a slew of postseason awards in her first collegiate season, including the Nicole Hensley AHA Rookie of the Year award.

Most points and assists by a freshman defenseman in the country ✅No surprise here, Dani is the 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐑𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐢𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 🙌#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/yLVzSak9No— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 5, 2026

She was also given All-AHA Second Team honors. Her 33 points, a program record for most points in a single season by a defenseman, pace the nation in points by a freshman defenseman and fourth in the overall standings among defensemen.

Maynard also leads the AHA in plus/minus, boasting a +43.

Sophomore Grace Outwater was named the AHA Scoring Champion, collecting 31 points against AHA opponents. She leads the conference with 24 goals, ranking fifth in the nation. Outwater, alongside Janecke, Butze and DeSa, was also named to the All-AHA First Team.

3️⃣1️⃣ points in AHA play.GO is your 𝐀𝐇𝐀 𝐒𝐜𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐢𝐨𝐧 😤#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/yj4vK1rNNV— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 5, 2026

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball travels to Lubbock for three-game series against Texas Tech
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State's first game back in the States was cancelled due to inclement weather. The two-game series vs. St. John’s was shortened to one game, where the blue and white won 12-2 over seven innings.

The Nittany Lions are now set to face off against Texas Tech. The three-game series is taking place in Lubbock, Texas. The first matchup is at 7:30 p.m. Friday, with the second at 3 p.m. Saturday and the third game at 2 p.m. on Sunday.

Penn State is coming off its first win since Feb. 20, beating Indiana State in the Bahamas. That win brought the Lions to a .333 win percentage and third in the Big Ten.

Texas Tech has started its season well with a record of 7-4. The Red Raiders rank seventh in the Big 12, with their last game against CSU Bakersfield where they lost 15-8 on Sunday. The last time these two teams faced off was way back in 1995, where Penn State won in an absolute shootout 24-23.

It’ll be a return to Lubbock for Nittany Lion right handed pitcher Greg Minnick. Minnick initially committed to Texas Tech from Indiana, Pennsylvania but transferred to Penn State last December. He didn’t get a chance to play before transferring and he’s yet to pitch for the Nittany Lions.

Another Penn State player to highlight is Jayden Davis, who leads the Nittany Lions with seven runs and 12 hits. Davis also leads the team in RBIs. Michael Anderson is another hitter that’s stood out in the lineup. He’s earned the nickname “The Magnet” due to how many pitches he’s been hit by. Anderson has posted three home runs, six runs, four RBIs, while getting plunked by six pitches already this season.

On the other side of the field, Linkin Garcia is a Red Raider to focus on. Garcia leads his team in batting average, runs batted in, homeruns, total bases and hits. On the other side of the ball, right handed pitcher Lukas Pirko will likely get one of the starts on the mound. Pirko leads his team in opposing batting average and wins in three starts this season.

Both teams haven’t started conference play yet, each team will start on Friday, March 13. Penn State will face Iowa and Texas Tech will play Kansas.

The Nittany Lions will have their hands full with the Red Raiders, as they’re ahead of the blue and white in every meaningful offensive category. However, Penn State beats Texas Tech on defense, with the same amount of hits allowed but 11 fewer runs conceded.

The three-game series begins at 6:30 p.m. on Friday at Dan Law Field.

title: Report: Former Penn State LB Jesse Luketa signs with New England Patriots
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State linebacker Jesse Luketa is set to start a new chapter in his NFL career.

Luketa is signing a one-year deal with the New England Patriots, according to NFL insider Jordan Schultz.

Sources: The #Patriots are signing former #Cardinals OLB Jesse Luketa to a 1-year contract.The former Penn State standout recorded three sacks and one forced fumble in 10 games in 2024. pic.twitter.com/uMUYnyY9e8— Jordan Schultz (@Schultz_Report) March 5, 2026

Luketa spent the tail end of the 2025 season on the Los Angeles Rams' practice squad before getting released in February. He was previously with the Arizona Cardinals, who drafted him in the seventh round of the 2022 NFL draft.

Throughout his professional career, Luketa has played in 31 games, tallying 35 tackles and seven tackles for loss.

Luketa was in Happy Valley from 2018-21, appearing in 47 games and recording 151 tackles and 11.5 tackles for loss. He was a third-team All-Big Ten selection by the conference media in 2021.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State fires women's basketball coach Carolyn Kieger after 7 seasons
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After seven seasons, it's on to something new for the Lady Lions.

Penn State fired coach Carolyn Kieger on March 5, signaling the end of an era in Happy Valley.

The Lady Lions are coming off one of the worst seasons in Penn State history with an 11-18 record.

Kieger was hired on April 3, 2019, replacing Coquese Washington. Kieger’s predecessor took an assistant coach position at Oklahoma and eventually was named Rutgers head coach.

In her first season with the blue and white, the Marquette graduate led the team to just seven wins, a decline from its 12-win season in 2018.

From there, Kieger saw minor improvements over the next few years, winning nine games in 2020-21, 11 games in 2021-22 and 14 in the 2022-23 season.

In her 2023-24 campaign, Kieger saw her best season yet with the Lady Lions, winning 22 games en route to a WBIT semifinal appearance. While the record was flashy and the deep tournament run was a highlight, the season was a disappointment for fans and players alike after having Sweet 16 aspirations at the beginning of the season.

After losing eight players to graduation and the transfer portal, Kieger’s squad began to struggle in the 2024-25 season.

The Lady Lions won just 10 games, losing 19 of their last 21 games with wins against No. 8 Ohio State and Providence. In the first season where the Big Ten tournament didn’t invite every team, the blue and white missed out.

This letdown carried into the 2025-26 season, as a new roster with flashy athletes like Kiyomi McMiller, Gracie Merkle and Moriah Murray struggled to find success.

Penn State finished with an 11-18 record on the year, with the program’s second-worst losing streak in program history occurring midway through the year with 11 straight losses.

Despite having star players like McMiller, Makenna Marisa and Ashley Owusu throughout her seven years, Kieger’s Penn State legacy will be remembered with four of the five worst losing records in Lady Lion history.

title: ‘That's called sports’ | Penn State men’s basketball reflects on melancholic loss to Ohio State
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State was riding high, fresh off a weekend upset win over Iowa, when the Nittany Lions hosted Senior Night against Ohio State. Senior forward Josh Reed was playing his best ball of the season, and the blue and white were nearly fully healthy for the first time all year.

Yet as soon as action started against the Buckeyes, the Nittany Lions morale and play, which seemed to be approaching its peak this season, immediately plummeted to the deepest depths mentally possible on the hardwood. Almost all those positive trends immediately reversed, and the volatility of Penn State’s mental fortitude was on full display.

“We had the lead about five minutes in, and then it was all snowball from there,” Mike Rhoades said. “Just disappointed, and we really fought the game, and we were the complete opposite on Saturday.”

Behind an emphatic 94-62 Ohio State win, Penn State appeared depleted on the court, and those sentiments were echoed by freshman wing Mason Blackwood postgame when he reflected on his lessons learned throughout his rookie campaign. For a team that’s been tested all season mentally, no better single contest represents that like the loss to the Buckeyes.

“Adversity — it's probably the biggest thing I'll take away,” Blackwood said. “Just learn how to respond when things get tough.”

The Nittany Lions appeared to be lacking in both physicality and talent, with Ohio State having its way with the blue and white throughout all 40 minutes. The writing was on the wall for Rhoades’ unit early in the clash, as the Buckeyes immediately handled everything thrown at them.

Nothing seemed pleasant for Penn State, as it was in an uphill battle on a day that was supposed to be a celebration for Penn State in its final home game of the year.

“The biggest thing I told our guys is adversity in the game,” Rhoades said. “Some guys are better than others at it. When you have personal adversity or team adversity, you got to respond to that adversity.”

One of the biggest mental tests thrown at the youthful Penn State unit was foul calls, a group that still took more trips to the free-throw line than its opponents appeared animated when the officials blew their whistles on calls that the Nittany Lions didn’t agree with.

Early in the second half, guard Dom Stewart was called for a foul and immediately began protesting. So did Rhoades and the Legion of Blue, Penn State’s student section, which chanted its displeasure in the form of a rhythmic “bulls—” chant.

The fans were a little more vocal in their displeasure, but the effect it had on the play on the court was clear — a team already struggling to claw back and find any sense of fortitude was immediately shot in the foot. That response to adversity that Rhoades mentioned wasn’t adequate.

“One of them, he actually messed up the call, and I actually got the foul back,” Stewart said. “A lot of it’s out of my control, but some of it's just like, ‘alright, bro, really, calling anything at this point.’ My hands high in the air, he wasn't even making it. I mean, I get it though, stuff happens.”

Penn State has one game left in its season, and the Nittany Lions have a chance to finish their regular season strong, but it's clear in the psychological department that there are still great strides to be made, and they haven’t been made yet.

The year will finish exactly as predicted in the preseason, stumbling to last in the Big Ten. That’s a tough pill to swallow, but the beating it took on the way to the bottom made it so much more bitter.

“When you feel sorry for yourself or you get frustrated, it makes the game harder,” Rhoades said. “And that you saw that today. You saw that it's disappointing, because I thought on Saturday we were completely the opposite. But that's called sports.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s gymnastics travels to Columbus for multi-team meet
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is heading to its neighbors to the west to compete in another quad-meet against NC State, Ohio State and Eastern Michigan. The Nittany Lions join their competitors in Columbus, Ohio, on Sunday.

This is the second quad-meet in a row for Penn State, so the amount of action and competition it has faced has really made the blue and white put up a fight. The Nittany Lions held their own against three Big Ten teams in the Big Four meet last Sunday, finishing second to Michigan and placing ahead of Nebraska and Washington.

This meet saw the Nittany Lions breaking records, with the final score being the second-highest team score this season, and the fourth-highest in school history. The Big Four meet was also a huge win when it came to personal records for the gymnasts. Beam saw three Nittany Lions grab their career high score.

This gives a bit of added confidence to the blue and white, with it being an obvious sign that the team is finding its footing and figuring out what a consistent lineup can look like for the final weeks of the season.

The end of the regular season is quickly approaching for Penn State, with only this quad-meet and then a meet-up with North Carolina next week on March 13. The team seems to know what

it needs to do to secure solid wins for the rest of the season, and its strongest athletes seem aware of the job at hand.

Dani Latronica, Jessica Johanson, Ellie Monahan, and Ava Piedrahita are the top performers for this team and have been consistent in either matching their personal bests or improving upon these scores all season long. This meet could seem like a chance for the Nittany Lions to test out their newfound winning streak tactics, or to test the waters and see what else they can improve upon.

Ohio State is a fellow Big Ten competitor, but one that the blue and white have not faced this season. The Buckeyes are currently running a 4-6 streak overall, going 4-5 in Big Ten competition. This will most likely be the team that the Nittany Lions will have to focus on, with Ohio State taking down multiple of the same teams as Penn State, and sitting only four spots down at No. 20 in the NCAA rankings.

NC State is also right there in terms of NCAA rankings, sitting at No. 21. The Wolfpack has gone 12-4 overall, and 4-2 in the ACC season. NC State is strong and confident; it has been holding its own and has been making sure to leave a gap in the scores between it and its competitors. This meet could look like a battle between the Wolfpack and Buckeyes for the second-place finish, or possibly the first-place spot.

Eastern Michigan is sitting at No. 51 in the rankings and have a 8-7 record for the season so far, with a 2-3 record in the Mid-American Conference. The Eagles have not seen much SEC or Big Ten action this season, so going up against the established teams in this quad-meet could pose a challenge. However, the team has been putting up pretty fair overall team scores, usually landing in the 195.000 area, so there could be some strong numbers for the Eagles this weekend.

There is plenty of room for surprises and close finishes. What the final placing will really come down to is if the Nittany Lions can continue their high scores on all four apparatuses, and what the other three teams, especially NC State and Ohio State, can do to put themselves on top. No matter what, a good time is sure to be had.

The award is given to the most-valuable player for an individual season in NCAA Division I women's hockey.

Janecke, the two-time AHA Player of the Year, currently leads the conference with 44 points despite missing eight games due to her position on the United States Olympic women's ice hockey team in the 2026 Milano-Cortina Winter Olympic Games, where she won the gold medal. In Janecke's first series back as a Nittany Lion, she scored four goals in two games against Syracuse in the AHA Semifinals at Pegula Ice Arena. Janecke and company will be back in action on Saturday for the AHA Championship game against No. 12 Mercyhurst at 2 p.m.
