title: Off the record | In defense of the spring break hookup
author: Miss Behave
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a third-year Collegian staffer writing under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic are invited to do so through this anonymous form.

Dearest reader,

It’s officially that time of year when suitcases crawl out from under the bed, group chats resurrect themselves from academic despair and everyone suddenly becomes an expert in finding cheap flights rather than self-respect.

We enter spring break with extravagant plans or absolutely no plans at all. Just vibes, a duffel bag and a dangerously optimistic bank account.

And yes, it’s also that special time of year when membership to the Mile-High Club is running a seasonal promotion. After all, nothing says spontaneity quite like deciding that at 30,000 feet in the air, inside a bathroom roughly the size of a carry-on, is the ideal setting to test both your flexibility and your judgement.

The flights are full. The bikinis are ambitious, often resembling dental floss more than an actual swimsuit. And the men are wearing tank tops showing off their flexing muscles, complete with the sexy Ray-Ban sunglasses that look like they’re straight out of the “Top Gun: Maverick” movie.

Spring break, my loves, is all about losing track of time, flirting with questionable decisions and discovering that SPF is just a suggestion when it comes to the skin you really want kissed.

It's a sacred time and place where dancing on tables is considered cardio and tequila is legally recognized as its own food group.

Now, let’s address the delicious elephant in the room: the spring break hookup. The odds of it following you all the way back to State College are slim. Plus, your friends will be too busy losing their own dignity to keep track of yours.

The evidence dissolves in saltwater. The tan fades. The name? Well, you’ll probably already forget it by the time you reach baggage claim.

Spring break is a lawless land of neon wristbands and temporary tramp tattoos that just scream “YOLO.” You will see things. You will do things. You will wake up wondering why there’s sand in places that sand should never, ever be. Or wonder why there’s a mysterious pool float in your hotel room. Consider it all experimental learning and character growth, like academia outside the classroom.

It's a world where breakfast is tequila, lunch is a frozen strawberry daiquiri and dinner is whatever your Instagram aesthetic requires.

And here’s the truth. Being a little wild, even a little more on the feral side, is perfectly fine. A playful, harmless hookup can be surprisingly empowering. It can boost your confidence, remind you that you’re desirable and maybe even cure those “winter uglies” you swore were permanent back in Pennsylvania.

But be warned dear reader, feral doesn’t mean foolish. Drink water. Share locations. Know where your shoes are. A little bit of chaos can be cute. A missing passport and no memory of what happened the night before isn’t.

We’re here for scandal, not to be the main characters in the next Netflix documentary series.

Spring break, my sweet sinners, isn’t about finding your soulmate. It’s about finding out how many margaritas you can responsibly consume while lounging on an all-inclusive beach resort chair. It's about kissing someone whose last name you’ll never learn. It's about realizing that sometimes, the hottest thing you can do is to choose the story you want to create and own it.

So go forth, my spicy little readers, be bold. Be loud. Be responsibly feral. Let yourself flirt outrageously and decide deliberately. If you’re adventurous enough to make a spring break memory, make sure it’s one worth telling. Preferably without legal repercussions.

Yours truly,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey hosts No. 12 Mercyhurst in AHA Championship
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
After a successful sweep over Syracuse in the semifinal round of the AHA tournament, Penn State has found itself in the championship game for the fourth-straight time.

Similar to last season, the Nittany Lions will play in front of their fans at Pegula Ice Arena against No. 12 Mercyhurst at 2 p.m. on Saturday.

“It’s awesome. If we take care of business, we don’t have to travel the rest of the way,” Jeff Kampersal, the team’s head coach said. “We have it really good here, so we prefer to stay home and play in front of Hockey Valley.”

The Lakers’ path

Mercyhurst earned itself a bye week after finishing second in the AHA with a conference record of 17-5-2. The Lakers went on to sweep Lindenwood in two games with a double overtime victory in Game 2.

However, the Lions battled in both games against Mercyhurst, at one point leading the final game by two goals. After a strong display of offense from Lindenwood in the first two periods, the Lakers battled back by scoring two unanswered goals as the final buzzer sounded. An opportunity was left for a player to become an overtime hero for their team.

Ultimately, it was Julia Perjus for Mercyhurst who put the dagger into Lindenwood’s season after Madison Bowtell failed to cover the puck in her crease, leading to an open net for Perjus.

The Stockholm, Sweden, native was the only goalscorer for the Lakers in Game 1 of the series and leads her team in points with 38.

Mercyhurst sits atop the conference in championship wins with 13 and reached its fourth-straight AHA championship after the semifinal sweep.

Storied rivalry

A new chapter renews in the Penn State and Mercyhurst conference rivalry as the squads enter yet another championship game. The Nittany Lions currently hold a three-game win streak over the Lakers in the championship game.

Additionally, the blue and white controlled the regular season series for the most part, posting a shutout in two out of the four games played.

However, in the regular season finale Mercyhurst handed Penn State its first conference loss of the season after an upset win in overtime. Payten Evans scored the game-winning goal for the Lakers after finding the net with four seconds left in overtime.

“Mercyhurst is always well-coached,” Kampersal said. “They’re very physical. They’re going to be hungry this time around, but every game is always like they have good habits and we have good habits. It’s just a matter of making the plays and cashing in on the opportunities when we have them. Using our speed and using our transition speed.”

A returning Tessa Janecke has been nothing short of cashing in on opportunities ever since her first game back from the Olympics. The gold medalist finished the semifinal round with four goals and earned her first hat-trick of the season. Due to this performance, the Orangeville, Illinois, native leads the conference in points heading into the final game.

The stage is set. One team will earn the right to call itself champions and guarantee a spot in the NCAA Women’s Ice Hockey Championship.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: 6 books to read for Women's History Month
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Content Warning: One of the recommended texts,"Women Talking" by Miriam Toews contains mention of sexual assault.

Women's History Month takes place in March, and serves as a time to recognize women's contributions to society. If you're looking for a way to celebrate women's achievements and educate yourself better about the significance of this month, you may want to consider picking up a book.

If learning through reading sounds like it's right up your alley, here are six book suggestions for Women's History Month, all recommended by Penn State professors.

1) "We Do Not Part" by Han Kang

Published in 2021, "We Do Not Part" is a fictional novel that tells the story of Kyungha, a female writer who is living in Seoul. One day, she gets a text from her friend, Inseon, from the hospital and is then asked to go to her house on Jeju Island to look after her pet bird.

Kyungha takes on the journey and must travel through a snowstorm in the barren landscape of Jeju Island, but when Kyungha gets to her friend’s house, she’s seemingly whisked away into a ghost-like world.

Gabeba Baderoon, who recommended the novel, said it uses poetic fiction to honor the tens of thousands of people who were killed on Jeju Island by U.S. supported government forces in the 1940s.

"The story suggests that despite the silence, such suffering does not actually disappear and instead erupts in the lives of the generations that follow," Baderoon, an associate professor of women's, gender and sexuality studies said. "Only overcoming this silence and acknowledging the past can truly open up the possibilities of the future."

2) "Women Talking" by Miriam Toews

Also recommended by Baderoon, "Women Talking" explores the aftermath and decisions of Mennonite women after several sexual assaults take place in their community. The book is based on events in a Bolivian Mennonite colony, and has also been turned into a movie of the same name.

Baderoon recommends "Women Talking" because of its depiction of how a group of women worked together to overcome sexual violence that was regarded as ordinary and unremarkable by the men in their community.

"The novel shows us how the women gather strength and resoluteness from one another and end the injustice they suffer," Baderoon said.

3) "Surfacing: On Being Black and Feminist in South Africa edited by Gabeba Baderoon and Desiree Lewis

This book is a collection of 20 essays that describe a viewpoint of what it's like to be a feminist in South Africa. Baderoon co-edited this book and suggested it because of its "evocative deliberations on art, icons, orgasms and how to live tenderly on the earth."

4) "Burn It Down!: Feminist Manifestos for the Revolution" by Breanne Fahs

Maggie-Rose Condit-Summerson is an assistant teaching professor of women's, gender and sexuality studies who recommended this book because of its ability to tackle a wide array of perspectives including abolitionists, anarchists, transgender and queer organizers, sex workers, witches and more.

This compilation of manifestos reaches from the 19th century to present and explores feminist movements throughout those time periods.

"In our current moment marked by escalating injustice in our communities, this collection of manifestos reminds us that feminist rage has long been a generative force for naming violence and oppression, refusing respectability politics and imagining more just worlds," Condit-Summerson said.

5) "Reproductive Justice: An Introduction" by Loretta J. Ross and Rickie Solinger

"Reproductive Justice: An Introduction" is a wide-ranging depiction of the reality of reproductive rights and justice in our society.

Condit-Summerson described the book as an essential primer for those who want to educate themselves on the histories and practices that shape reproductive freedom and bodily autonomy today, specifically centering the experiences of women of color.

"As luminaries in the fight for reproductive autonomy, Ross and Solinger articulate how reproductive justice moves far beyond the narrow pro- versus anti-choice debates that have long dominated conversations about reproductive rights while providing a powerful framework for organizing in our post-Dobbs landscape," Condit-Summerson said.

6) "Interpreter of Maladies" by Jhumpa Lahiri

Written by Jhumpa Lahiri, "Interpreter of Maladies" is a collection of short stories that follows Indian immigrants and Indian Americans through their struggles with identity and culture, and it won both a Pulitzer Prize and the Hemingway Foundation/PEN award.

Laura Spielvogel recommended this book due to its focus on women's interior lives "to illuminate how gender intersects with culture, migration, family and belonging." Spielvogel, teaching professor of women's, gender and sexuality studies, assigns one of Lahiri's stories in her WMNST 105N Living in a Diverse World course.

According to Spielvogel, each of the stories comes from a different perspective ranging from male to female and young to old and offers a compassionate window into the intimate moments that make us human.

"This beautifully written book transports the reader into the inner lives of others to foster empathy for their rich emotional, cultural and relational experiences," Spielvogel said.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Ireland is calling, pick up the phone | Irish music to listen to this spring
author: Ashlyn Kafer | The Daily Collegian
description:
A period of limbo between Penn State’s State Patty’s and the official St. Patrick’s Day known to the rest of the world has begun.

There’s no better time than the present to fill your headphones or speakers with the music of Irish artists who have brought the sounds and culture of Ireland to the mainland.

Whether you can hear an Irish accent poking out through the lyrics or simply feel the island calling to you in between each musical measure, these artists help to liven the spirit of the season with their contributions from the Emerald Isle.

The Cranberries

With standout hits like “Zombie” and “Linger,” The Cranberries have made their mark on communities across the globe.

The characteristic vocal cadence of lead singer Dolores O’Riordan not only tilts the listener to the band’s Irish heritage, but adds an element of intrigue to each song as O’Riordan’s distinctive twang reimagines the entanglement of melody and lyricism.

The band’s four members met in Limerick, Ireland, as a group of ragtag teenagers looking to take a stab at making music. With the combination of their talents, the group’s attempt to rock the industry became a success, selling over 40 million albums throughout their career.

Fontaines D.C.

Fontaines D.C. is a contemporary band that formed in Dublin, combining smooth, psychedelic harmonies with raw, rock-centric guitar riffs.

The tracks “I Love You” and “Roman Holiday” helped to bring the band into the mainstream light, as well as contributing to the spread of Irish influence with lead singer Grian Chatten’s subtle vocal inflections.

Inhaler

Inhaler has brought a young and fresh perspective to the modern rock scene, intertwining organic guitar harmonics with the sultry vocality of lead singer Elijah Hewson, son of U2 frontman Bono.

The Dublin-based band has slowly increased in fame since their first release in 2017, amassing over a million monthly listeners on Spotify and encouraging young fans to make a pilgrimage to Ireland to catch a glimpse of the members.

U2

Despite lead singer Bono’s unfortunate display of ego in 2014 by automatically downloading U2’s “Songs of Innocence” into every Apple accounts iTunes library, the band’s sound bolstered the rock community in its prime.

“With Or Without You” and “I Still Haven’t Found What I’m Looking For” helped turn the band from a team of Dublin natives to a group of global superstars, garnering over a billion streams on Spotify.

my bloody valentine

Shoegaze pioneers, my bloody valentine, are another group from Dublin, introducing the broader alternative scene to the manipulation of electric guitar in tandem with vocals that blend seamlessly into the backtrack.

Tracks “when you sleep” and “only tomorrow” exemplify the band’s curation of a dreamy tone through the aggressive distortions of electronic sound juxtaposed with gentle drums and easy lyrics.

The band’s contribution to the music industry also shows the versatility of Ireland’s musical influence, from rock-inspired ballads to whimsical waltzes that echo stories of generations past.

Cocteau Twins

While the members of the Cocteau Twins themselves are not from Ireland, the band’s influence is heavily wrought from ancient Irish culture.

The Cocteau Twins have spurred much of its popularity around not being able to understand the lyrics, though the composition of the words being mostly incomprehensible is intentional.

The lyrics are a combination of English, French and Gaelic — an ancient Irish language — words to more easily blend into the natural composition of the sound, creating an entirely new imagination of the dream pop genre.

Scottish lead singer Elizabeth Fraser effortlessly spins the Irish influence into each track, weaving between understandability and the whispers of archaic tongues unbeknownst to the average listener.

Sinéad O’Connor

Sinéad O’Connor is another undeniably Irish artist, from the origins of her name to using her platform for activism in support of the reunification of Ireland’s northern region and the remaining Republic of Ireland.

O’Connor’s music is a mix of alternative, rock and folk sounds, working alongside the air of defiance she holds against oppressive government policies and injustices toward minority groups.

She famously loved her Irish heritage, even once claiming “I am Ireland.” She often emphasized the deep-seated ethnic roots found throughout her discography and mirroring the love for Ireland felt by many artists from the island.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Mike Rhoades addresses his future following Penn State men’s basketball’s loss to Ohio State
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Rhoades walked into the Bryce Jordan Center media room following Penn State’s 94-62 loss to Ohio State. He gave a five-minute press conference with two questions toward the end acknowledging what’s arguably the most important topic surrounding the program: his future.

The Nittany Lions hold a 12-18 record this season, including 3-16 in Big Ten play. With Rhoades in his third year at the helm of Penn State, he explained why he’s still the right head coach for the squad despite the results in 2025-26.

“'Cause I care. I'm going to fight,” Rhoades said. “There's some things you got to deal with. We'll deal with them. Try to keep building the program. It's a different landscape than it's ever been before, so you just got to continue to respond different ways to it and fight for your program and keep pushing.”

Wednesday’s game was the fourth time this season Penn State lost a game by 30 points or more. Those other losses were a 113-72 road loss to Indiana, a 110-69 road loss to Michigan and an 80-46 neutral-site loss to Pitt.

Additionally, the Nittany Lions had a 98-71 loss to Wisconsin in Rec Hall, which was previously the worst home loss by point differential prior to the loss to Ohio State. Penn State also endured losses to Rutgers, Northwestern, Maryland and Oregon this season, and all of those four teams are toward the bottom of the Big Ten standings between No. 14 and No. 17.

Meanwhile, the three conference wins the Nittany Lions have rattled off were all decided by three points or less in games against Minnesota, Washington and Iowa. The other nine wins Penn State has in its overall record were nonconference victories over Quad 3 and Quad 4 teams.

The Nittany Lions’ latest result saw them fail to make a basket for a 13:32 stretch in the first half, which helped cause a 32-point loss to Ohio State.

“We had the lead about five minutes in, and then it was all a snowball from there,” Rhoades said. “Just disappointed, and we really fought the game, and we were the complete opposite on Saturday and today, we really fought the game and just made it a snowball effect on us. Credit to Ohio State, they played a great game. Disappointed, just plenty of mistakes, especially on the offensive end, and then it really impacted our defense.”

Rhoades came to Penn State in 2023 following former head coach Micah Shrewsberry's departure to Notre Dame. While the team had just made an appearance in the NCAA Tournament, only two players remained with the Nittany Lions after the coaching switch, so it essentially had to start from scratch.

In his first season with the program, Rhoades showed some promise as Penn State finished 9-11 in regular season Big Ten play amid a 16-17 season. The Nittany Lions started 2024-25 with a 12-2 record, but they finished with a 16-15 overall record and 6-14 in conference play.

With the end of the 2025-26 campaign nearing, Penn State has just nine Big Ten wins over the last two seasons. It’s also 7-31 over its last 38 games against power-conference opponents, dating back to last season’s loss to Indiana on Jan. 5, 2025, which is when its losing streak that year began.

Rhoades was asked if he felt Wednesday’s home finale could be his last game coaching in the Bryce Jordan Center, to which he gave a direct answer with his contract lasting until the 2029-30 season.

“No, I got four years left of my contract,” Rhoades said. “I'm going to coach. I'm going to coach my butt off. Go as hard as I can. Wake up tomorrow, work hard. Work harder than I ever have and just keep going and keep coaching.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Coach Jeff Tambroni talks Cornell, young players ahead of matchup with Big Red
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State dominated Yale last week and is looking for its first back-to-back win for the first time this season. Penn State has struggled with maintaining momentum this year, and this could be the squad’s first opportunity of showcasing that it has changed.

Jeff Tambroni spoke to the media today. Here's what he had to say.

Preparing for Cornell

The Nittany Lions have the chance to avenge their loss last season in the NCAA Final Four. The blue and white lost 11-9 and are preparing for a different Cornell team than last season.

“It’s probably impossible to replace a guy like CJ Kirst, but they’ve done a really good job stockpiling a group of young men who fit really well into what they’re trying to do and they really haven’t seemed to take too much of a step back from last year to this year,” Tambroni said.

Kirst had 82 goals and 115 points last season, yet Penn State held Kirst scoreless in the game in the Final Four. The Nittany Lions also spoiled the Big Red from going undefeated in the regular season. This season, the offense has been spread out between Willem Firth and Ryan Goldstein, the nephew of Dennis Goldstein, who was National Player of the Year in 1991 for North Carolina.

“Both are extremely skilled, extremely talented, they play off of each other extremely well and they’ve continued that trend,” Tambroni said. “Both of them have done a terrific job of filling in the gap of Kirst and others that they’ve lost.”

The Nittany Lion defense will need to be on high alert on Saturday.

Offense

Penn State’s offense has been a big key to its success so far, and a big part of that is the showing from freshman attacker Jack Iannatuono, who’s been a difference maker this season. Against Yale, the freshman put up a career-high six points in the game and continues to be a big part of the offense.

“As a quarterback, not just an attackman, but a guy that plays below the goal line and manages the ball for us,” Tambroni said. “He does a great job of just working hard every day, recognizing there’s a whole lot more left for his game to improve, for our team to improve, and I think he’s remained focused on that.”

The blue and white have been at it strongest when it can find the back of the net. Penn State has the No. 9 best scoring offense in the country.

“I do think it’s depth. I think we have 10 to 11 guys that have played a regular clip for us. We have four attackmen that play consistently throughout the course of each game and you know really seven middies that have played, when everyone’s healthy,” Tambroni said. “So I think the depth of that group has really helped.”

While Penn State has lost guys like Matt Traynor and TJ Malone, the squad has been able to have more depth this season.

Alex Ross

Alex Ross isn’t just Penn State’s top defensemen he’s also one of the top defensemen in the country. The senior was just selected to the USILA Team of the Week and has won Big Ten Defensemen of the Week earlier this season.

“Alex has certainly grown throughout his years here. I would even say significantly throughout his senior year, and he does not get too emotionally high or low. I think that’s a big piece of why he has been so successful for so long,” Tambroni said.

He’s also been a leader on the team and has helped other players on the back end. With a player like Patrick Keenan having his first full season as a full-time starter, Ross has been someone he could lean on for advice.

“I think when our younger guys, in particular, like a Patrick Keenan or Brad Piffath or a Dillon Torggler, guys that are filling in now around him and the close defense, see the way that he manages things emotionally, I think it’s become a huge boost for us,” Tambroni said.

Ross has been a leader on and off the field and has been a game-changer this season.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Senior Night Blues | Penn State men’s basketball suffers blowout loss to Ohio State on Senior Night
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has had a long season of ups and downs, but it got one more chance to close out its home game schedule on a high note against an opponent that’s become all too familiar: Ohio State.

However, the Buckeyes came to State College and rained on the Nittany Lions’ parade, handing the blue and white a staggering 94-62 loss to ruin Josh Reed’s senior night in Penn State’s final home game of the season.

Ivan Jurić won the opening tipoff for Penn State, but an empty possession turned into an Ohio State board and an easy two points followed for the Buckeyes as they drew first blood.

The scarlet and gray got out to an early 7-0 lead but Jurić drew a blocking foul on his bucket for an and-one opportunity, though missing his shot from the charity stripe to keep it a 7-2 game.

Seven-footer Jurić made up for his miss on the following possession though, as a Reed defensive board sparked a big man three-pointer that made the Legion of Blue go ballistic.

At the under 12-minute mark media timeout, the Buckeyes held a 12-9 lead with the last basket going to Christoph Tilly, with Devin Royal and Penn State’s Freddie Dilione V missing a pair of three-pointers before the whistle.

From that point on, it was all Ohio State, as the Buckeyes were in the midst of an 11-0 scoring run when the under eight-minute media timeout hit.

Triples from the scarlet and gray’s leading scorer, Bruce Thornton and leading three-point scorer John Mobley Jr., right before the whistle, only added insult to injury.

“Yeah, listen, that’s great coaching,” Ohio State coach Jake Diebler said. “I’m kidding, obviously. Going into this game, we felt like we had to make the extra pass. We felt like we had to play inside out, and I thought our guys executed that at a really high level … When you have a guy like John Mobley on your team, he’s capable of breaking the game open with the three ball at a high level.”

Penn State’s best three-point shooter, Eli Rice, couldn’t get his layup to fall with just about five and a half minutes left in the first frame, leaving the blue and white’s grim deficit at 11-26.

The Nittany Lions’ deficit only worsened after that moment.

As the four-minute mark timeout approached, Mobley knocked down another from beyond the arc after an Amare Bynum basket, leaving Penn State jarred in its huddle as the score became a 33-11 Buckeye lead.

“I just think, since we were missing shots, that was affecting the defense,” Dominick Stewart said. “We couldn’t sustain our defense, so it’s pretty easy for them to go on a run.”

Mike Rhoades and Penn State, desperately needing any kind of scoring, finally got it in the form of a Dilione trey as he moved up to five points on the night.

The blue and white’s second three-pointer of the game was needed, but with Ohio State holding a commanding 43-22 lead just seconds before the half, it was clear that it wouldn’t do much to change the course of the game.

Halftime arrived, and the Nittany Lions, facing a gigantic hole, needed to turn the tide – and fast.

“We really struggled offensively in the first half,” Rhoades said. “The lack of finishing at the rim and not getting clean shots really did then start impacting … It snowballed today against us too many times.”

In the other locker room, though, the Buckeyes were likely eager to get back out on the court and keep their feet on the gas.

The second half began on a weird note, as an Ohio State travel led to a Dilione triple.

Dilione’s make was then matched by a Royal three-pointer that was countered with a Stewart three-ball, all of which made it a 49-27 Buckeye lead.

There had been several questionable calls and rulings throughout the affair, but a goaltending call on Dilione after an impressive block sent the Bryce Jordan Center into an uproar.

“I try not to worry about that,” Stewart said. “But some of it is just like, alright bro.”

Following the call, the crowd voiced its disapproval, with the Legion of Blue letting some expletives fly, all while Rhoades got in the face of the official who made the call.

If the controversial rulings weren’t enough to suck the life out of the blue and white faithful, back-to-back three-pointers from Bynum and Mobley, who hit his from way at the top of the key, were certainly enough to do so.

Still with 15 minutes left on the clock, fans and students alike took to the stairs, flooding the exits as Penn State faced a drowning 31-60 deficit.

Yet another Mobley triple moved him up to 20 points on the night, as Penn State’s leading scorer, Dilione, knocked down another three-point shot of his own to improve to 12 points in the game, though his squad was still staring at a 29 point deficit.

There was no drama the rest of the way, but a Justin Houser three-pointer – his third of the season – and a Stewart four-point play gave the Legion of Blue one last chance to celebrate something.

The Buckeyes never let up and were putting the hurt on the blue and white, despite their already mammoth-sized lead.

With just minutes left to play, Ohio State was draining its three-pointers like it was the late stages of a March Madness game, adding a cherry on top of their rout over the blue and white, walking away with a 94-62 victory when the clock showed nothing but zeroes.

“My last game here? No. I got four years left on my contract,” Rhoades said when asked about his future with Penn State.

Injury Report

After exiting the game against Iowa with an undisclosed injury, Kayden Mingo was listed as ‘out’ entering Wednesday night’s game against Ohio State, but was considered “day-to-day” prior.

Up Next

Penn State hits the road and heads to Piscataway, New Jersey on Sunday to take on Rutgers in the blue and white’s final game of the regular season before the Big Ten tournament kicks off on Tuesday.

Tipoff is scheduled for noon.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Sigma Nu to return to Penn State in fall 2026
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Delta Delta chapter of the Sigma Nu fraternity will return to Penn State at the beginning of the fall 2026 semester after almost eight years of suspension, according to IFC President Alex Comas during Wednesday’s University Park Undergraduate Association general assembly meeting.

The chapter was suspended on May 18, 2018 by the national organization of Sigma Nu for failing to comply with predetermined requirements over the course of two years, according to the fraternity’s website.

Currently, the Evans Scholar Foundation is renting the former Sigma Nu house from the fraternity, located at 340 Burrowes Road, until 2028. Sigma Nu plans to move back into the house in the fall 2028 semester as part of the chapter's "re-colonization" process, according to the fraternity’s website.

An Alumni Advisory Board will visit campus during the remaining time in the spring 2026 semester to formally announce the chapter’s return and facilitate recruitment of student leaders, according to the Alumnus Newsletter Delta Delta.

Sigma Nu will join the Interfraternity Council (IFC) and the 35 additional fraternities affiliated with Penn State for the 2026-2027 school year, according to Comas.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: The Daily Collegian wins 6 Student Keystone Media Awards in 2026, receives 1 honorable mention
author: Rachael Keller | News Editor
description:
The Daily Collegian won six 2026 Student Keystone Media Awards in Division I schools.

The Student Keystone Media Awards “recognizes high school and college journalism that provides relevance, integrity and initiative in serving readers,” according to its website.

A Division I school is considered a four-year college or university with more than 10,000 students enrolled.

The Collegian placed first in Ongoing News Coverage for its reporting on the Penn State Board of Trustees' decision to close of seven Commonwealth campuses. Pragya Sinha, Teresa Phelan, Alexandra Antonio, Noah Aberegg, Alex Fischer and Mia Lopez were contributors.

This is the second consecutive year that the Collegian has won this category.

Noah Aberegg won second place for Public Service/Enterprise Package for his investigation, “‘It's a disgrace, it's embarrassing’ | Fencers express concerns with coach Matteo.”

Teresa Phelan won second place in Editorial for her piece, “Penn State campus closures undermine its land-grant mission.”

For her work on “Cultural diversity isn't a threat, but the foundation,” Elbia Vidal Castillo won a second place award in the Column category.

The Daily Collegian won second place for the Layout and Design of the Hispanic Heritage Month Edition newspaper. Geraldine Cruz-Hernandez, Kahlie Wray, Jonathon Chiu, Mercedes Hamilton, Andrew Deal and Teresa Phelan designed this paper.

The newsroom also won second place for Excellence in Reporting on Diversity-Equity and Inclusion for its work on celebrating Hispanic students, faculty and culture at Penn State. Jonathan Chiu, Geraldine Cruz-Hernandez, Mia Debelevich, Elbia Vidal Castillo, Herley Gong and Noah Aberegg produced stories for this award.

Additionally, Geraldine Cruz-Hernandez received an honorable mention in the Photo/News category for one of her photos at the second No Kings protest in State College.

Winners for all 24 categories can be found here.

MORE NEWS

title: Penn State baseball dominates St. John's, earns mercy-rule victory
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was back home in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park for its home opener against St. John’s on Wednesday.

The Nittany Lions defeated the Red Storm with a dominant victory of 12-2, giving Penn State a chance to turn its season around early at a 4-6 record.

The blue and white started out the first inning strong, setting the tone for the game with pitcher Frankie Sanchez notching a strikeout to leave the bases loaded, and a scoreless first inning.

Vanderbilt transfer Jayden Davis put Penn State on the board with its first home run, shortly followed by a ripping RBI triple hit by first-year shortstop Preston Yaucher.

Jesse Jaconski was able to run home, followed by a ground ball to second base from catcher Joey Demucci, enabling Yaucher to score, followed by center Maddox McDonald putting the team up 4-0 at the bottom of the second.

St. John’s failed to respond at the top of the third, with third baseman Jayder Raifstanger and right fielder Dylan Fitzsimmons left stranded as the team let up another out. The Nittany Lions didn’t score any runs in their half inning.

The Red Storm put up their first run of the game in the top of the fourth after two walks and a hit-by-pitch put catcher Sean Britt in a position to score. Left fielder Lewis Rodrig sent a hit hard into left field, putting the Red Storm on the board and the score 4-1.

The Nittany Lions responded with their second home run of the night by designated hitter Michael Anderson as he barreled the ball over the left field wall, allowing center fielder Cohl Mercado to score, as Penn State extended its lead to 6-1.

Penn State created a further gap between it and St. John’s, as Anderson blasted a 399-foot home run into center field, where he, Mercado and DeMucci scored, elevating the Nittany Lions’ lead to 9-2. It was Anderson’s second home run of the game as he was the main catalyst of the blue and white’s runs.

The sixth inning remained quiet, with neither team putting up any hits or runs, leaving St. John’s a chance to mount a comeback in the seventh inning.

However, that was silenced, as Anderson continued his hot day, hitting a ground ball through the right-side of the infield, scoring McDonald and DeMucci as the blue and white poured on the runs in the bottom of the seventh. The Nittany Lions led 11-2.

Mercado closed out the game for the Nittany Lions with a walk-off balk, putting an end to their commanding home opener.

Penn State notched 11 hits, three home runs and eight walks in a strong offensive showing. Anderson tallied seven RBIs, going 3-for-4 with a walk.

Up next

Penn State will travel to Lubbock, Texas, to take on Texas Tech with the first game taking place at 7:30 p.m. EST on Friday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey earns slew of All-AHA selections
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
There's a reason Penn State is atop the AHA rankings with a strong supporting cast on the ice.

The Nittany Lions earned nine All-AHA selections, tying the most ever selected in a single season in program history.

Tying program records with 9️⃣ All-AHA selections and 4️⃣ First Team honorees 🤝📰: https://t.co/cWJZMAmqrG#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/w9IiC3AVvK— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 4, 2026

The blue and white also tied the program record for the most First Team selections, as Tessa Janecke, Grace Outwater, Kendall Butze and Katie DeSa earned All-AHA First Team honors.

Outwater and Janecke led the team in scoring, combining for 47 goals on the year. On the defensive end, Butze played in 36 games and recorded 45 blocks, the most on the team.

DeSa was named to the HCA watch list for the Women's Goalie of the Year Award after recording 501 saves and a 93.5% save percentage this season.

Named to the All-AHA Second Team were Katelyn Roberts, Maddy Christian and Danica Maynard, while Mikah Keller and Maynard were named to the All-AHA Rookie Team.

Penn State has a chance to earn yet another AHA title when it hosts Mercyhurst at 2 p.m. Saturday.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State WRs ready to change the recent narrative in 2026
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell and Noah Pauley pulled wide receiver Brett Eskildsen in for an unexpected meeting just hours after Pauley had spoken with the media for the first time as Penn State’s wide receivers coach.

The news they gave Eskildsen left him shocked. Pauley was taking a job with the Green Bay Packers, and the Nittany Lions were left with a position to fill late in the hiring process.

“We hate to see him go selfishly,” Eskildsen said last Wednesday, “but at the same time, that's the dream job for him, so we're really happy for him.”

Eskildsen added that as a Packers fan himself, this is a win-win. His favorite team gets a proven wide receivers coach, and he gets to see the coach who allowed him to follow his dream live out his own.

Campbell and company’s search to replace Pauley lasted 11 days, with Penn State poaching Kashif Moore from Colorado State. Moore had just joined the Rams in December after three seasons at his alma mater, UConn, where he developed Biletnikoff Award finalist Skylar Bell.

“I think we hit a home run with getting coach Moore, and I think everyone's taking it really well,” Eskildsen said. “We're all really excited about coach Moore coming in, leading us.”

Both Moore and his wide receivers have a watchful eye on them, though, after three straight seasons of poor play from Penn State’s pass-catchers. That span featured a catchless Orange Bowl and no receiver accumulating more than 720 yards in one year.

There have been plenty of different people that caught blame. From the wide receivers themselves to former wide receivers coach Marques Hagans and offensive coordinator Andy Kotelnicki, the wide receiver room has been a sore subject for Penn State.

The players have taken it among themselves to get on the same page ahead of spring practice, as quarterback Rocco Becht said his group and the receivers have met both inside and on the practice field.

That extra work is crucial for Eskildsen and Chase Sowell, especially, as Becht said both have been dealing with small injuries.

“It's been awesome,” Becht said. “We've had a bunch of meetings, just myself and the receivers, and going over formations, concepts, motions, adjustments, and we've been able to get on the field a couple times … But I think those things that you can do when the coaches aren't around, when the coaches aren't looking, how many times you can get into the film room when the coaches aren't there. Just that extra work has been valuable for all of us.”

A fresh start may be exactly what the Nittany Lions need to bring success back to their wide receivers. Campbell brought in six new faces for the position, two of whom were Iowa State’s leading receivers last season, and they’re ready to make a statement.

“I've heard rumors, things about Penn State receivers have struggled this and that, but I think this room is coming together really good,” Eskildsen said. “I think we got a lot of talent and a lot of big plays ahead. I think we got a really good room, and the determination factor is really high. We're ready to change that narrative.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Find your people, find your passion | Spring New Organization Involvement Fair showcases what campus life has to offer
author: Serina Chan | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State’s New Organization Involvement Fair showcased clubs and organizations to help students discover their next hobby or community from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. in the HUB-Robeson Center.

The categories of clubs ranged from political interests to gaming, encouraging students to get more involved in future semesters.

Present at the fair, Phi Chi Pre-Medical Society, a coed, brother organization to the Phi Chi Medical Fraternity, has already accumulated over 20 members. The organization encouraged undergraduate students to participate in their three-day rush process planned for after spring break.

Nyssa Behl, a member of Phi Chi, said the fraternity is aiming to gather like-minded students in pre-health to find opportunities in service, professional development and leadership.

“As many opportunities we’re going to have, building community at Penn State is a huge factor,” Behl, a second-year studying nursing, said. “Being able to go to events with a group, go to chapters together, making substantial connections and having everyone become friends with each other is another reason why Phi Chi is so important.”

If pre-health is not your interest, the involvement fair also represented the Alexander Hamilton Society, an organization hoping to connect Penn State students with prominent figures in national security and international affairs.

Dawson Coventry, a society member, said the group focuses mainly on highlighting the diversity within academia.

“We can bring professionalism within the organization but also be able to teach students about foreign policy,” Coventry, a third year studying international politics, said. “The two most important fundamentals for us is for the diversification not only in degrees for international politics and political science, but also branching to economics, business and so much more.”

The fair also highlighted clubs that help students adopt new hobbies. The Fiber Arts Club is welcoming any student interested in learning more about felting, knitting, sewing and other forms of textile crafts.

Julia Fani said joining the club offered her an opportunity to explore a new area of creativity different from her usual work.

“I personally wanted to join the Fiber Arts Club because I’m a stem major and there’s not really an outlet for me to be creative,” Fani, a second-year studying mechanical engineering, said. “A lot of people in the club are stem majors as well. We all come together to do something outside of our regular routine.”

Non-profit and volunteer-based clubs were also at the involvement fair, one including Spectrum Empowerment.

Harshita Ganga, a member, said the club works to empower children with developmental disabilities and to spread awareness about the topic.

“We’re going to be hosting events for children with developmental disabilities and be able to expose them to science and robotics,” Ganga, a first year studying premedicine, said. “Just exposing them to anything they can’t get in school.”

Ganga also said the organization conducts one-on-one experiences between volunteers and children to help practice social skills.

Additionally, the Gujarati Student Association, Domino Club, Fly Fishing Club and more attended the involvement fair to spread awareness about what their organizations have to offer.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey Olympians to be honored during Thursday men's hockey game
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After competing for their countries last month, three Penn Staters will be honored during Thursday's men's game.

Stop by tomorrow night’s @PennStateMHKY game to see our Olympians in a ceremonial puck drop and get their autographs during the first intermission 🇺🇸🇮🇹🇸🇪#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/Z0NHJpsx7e— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 4, 2026

Tessa Janecke, Nicole Hall and Matilde Fantin will be part of a ceremonial puck drop against Wisconsin on Thursday, followed by autographs during the first intermission.

Janecke and Team USA won gold at the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics, defeating Canada 2-1 in the gold medal match.

Sweden and Hall finished fourth after falling to Switzerland, 2-1, in overtime of the bronze medal game, while Fantin and Team Italy were eliminated in the quarter finals after a 6-0 loss to the U.S.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse dominates Colgate as undefeated season continues
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s hot start to the Kayla Treanor era continued with another dominant victory on Wednesday afternoon.

The Nittany Lions welcomed Colgate for a Wednesday matinee, dominating 20-8 inside Holuba Hall.

Unlike Penn State’s typical quick starts to games this year, it was the Raiders who went up 1-0 after 45 seconds, as Amelia Reinholt scored her first of the day. However, junior Delaney Radin got things tied up with a free-position goal, recording her ninth goal of the year.

The Nittany Lions quickly got in the driver’s seat as senior Payton Wainman netted her 17th and 18th goals of the season — with the latter tying freshman Rocquette Allen for the team lead.

Following Radin and Wainman’s success on goal, junior Kelly MacKinney had her time to shine, scoring her 10th goal of the year. Radin recorded the assist on the score, putting her at 28 points on the year.

The fourth Nittany Lion took control on offense halfway through the first quarter, leading to a goal by Mount St. Mary’s transfer Anna Salerno to take a 5-1 lead.

Less than a minute after Salerno’s goal, MacKinney scored her second of the day, but was quickly evened out with Colgate’s second goal.

Wainman scored her third goal of the game for the third time this season. With a little over a minute left in the quarter, MacKinney recorded a hat trick of her own, too.

Just 11 seconds into the second quarter, Salerno found the back of the net again, scoring her second goal of the day.

Coming off a dominant showing at Cornell, where goalie Sydney Manning recorded 10 saves for a 52.6% save percentage, her success continued on Wednesday as she recorded six saves in the first half for a 66.7% save percentage.

After nearly 10 minutes of no offensive success from either side, MacKinney scored her fourth goal of the game at the 05:52 mark to put the Nittany Lions at 10 goals on the day.

Allen came right back and scored her first goal of the game, putting her at 19 in her historic freshman campaign. Senior Maddie Greco scored a little over a minute later, marking her fifth of the year.

The Nittany Lions’ success continued through the rest of the half, as freshman Hannah Hoyt scored — followed by the Raiders’ third goal of the day — to take a 10-point lead.

Coming out of the half, Allen crossed the 20-goal mark in her young career, but was quickly brought back to a 10-goal game off another Colgate goal.

Following Wainman and MacKinney’s first-half hat tricks, Salerno recorded her first hat trick of the year as she found the back of the net for her third goal against the Raiders.

Colgate’s Aubrey McMahon scored her second of the day on a man-up opportunity following a yellow card on Dakota Cristo, making it a 10-goal game.

Penn State’s lead continued to dwindle as the fourth quarter was underway, with Charley Drouin scoring the Raiders’ sixth goal of the game less than 30 seconds into the final period. However, Salerno quickly brought it back to a double-digit lead with her fourth goal off a free position shot.

The Nittany Lions had their biggest lead of the day as Erika Ho scored on a man-up opportunity, which was further extended with Wainman’s fourth goal of the game, a score from Porter Rhoades and freshman Anna Mellinger’s first career goal — evened out by two Colgate goals — as Penn State earned its seventh straight win to open the year.

Injury report

Senior Erika Ho was out of the starting lineup for the third straight game on Wednesday after suffering an undisclosed injury against Bucknell. Ho played limited minutes against Cornell and Colgate.

Up next

Penn State will travel to California to take on San Diego State at 3 p.m. on Sunday. The Aztecs are 1-3 to start the year.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State OL Anthony Donkoh to miss spring practice
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Another Nittany Lion will miss spring practice.

According to Mark Brennan of 247Sports, Penn State offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh won't be available for spring ball.

Per the report, Donkoh underwent an unspecified "medical procedure." Donkoh recently spoke to the media in February at the THON Explorers Program.

Donkoh is a two-year starter on the offensive line, starting last year at right guard while the year before he held down the right tackle spot before a season-ending injury late in the year.

He'll join tight ends Andrew Rappleyea and Gabe Burkle as players confirmed to miss spring practice. Linebacker Tony Rojas and safety Jeremiah Cooper are trending toward missing spring ball as well. Additionally, quarterback Rocco Becht will be limited following a pair of surgeries on both his shoulders last year.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: University-affiliated travel to Middle East suspended indefinitely
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State announced Tuesday that university-affiliated travel to the Middle East will be suspended indefinitely following an advisory issued by the U.S. Department of State.

The suspension affects any official Penn State travel that has not yet left the United States.

Study abroad programs, beginning in January and February, in the Middle East region include Jordan, Abu Dhabi, and Israel.

Students, faculty and staff who are already abroad will receive updates via Penn State Global, according to a Penn State news release.

Countries under the university travel suspension include: Afghanistan, Bahrain, Cyprus, Egypt, Iran, Iraq, Israel, Gaza and the West Bank, Jordan, Kuwait, Lebanon, Qatar, Oman, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Syria, the United Arab Emirates (including Abu Dhabi and Dubai) and Yemen.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State notebook | Clarisa Crowell talks weekend performance, preparing for what’s next
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 24 Penn State is gearing up for its final non-conference weekend before booking a flight to the West Coast to face Oregon State and open Big Ten play against No.17 Oregon. The Nittany Lions will play four games at the Hokie Invite in Salem, Virginia.

The blue and white is coming off a 3-2 weekend at the Tennessee Invitational, with its losses coming against No. 1 Tennessee.

Coach Clarisa Crowell discussed Penn State’s performance last weekend, as well as its upcoming competition, during Wednesday's media session.

Britton stands tall

Sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton was the lone returner from the Nittany Lions’ pitching staff last season. In 2025, the LaVale, Maryland, native’s strong suit was her work in relief, posting five saves on the year.

However, in the blue and white’s first meeting with the Lady Volunteers on Friday night, Britton got the starting nod and stood tall against the nation's best.

“I think (Britton) had one of the best pitching performances against Tennessee that I’ve seen (Britton) have since she’s been here,” Crowell said. “If you just look at her development, I’ve been very pleased and I’m proud of how much she has developed in the course of the year. She’s a much better pitcher this year at this point in time than she was last year.”

The sophomore pitched four innings, allowing zero runs, three hits and three walks, while posting six strikeouts. Britton’s strikeout total was the second most tossed against Tennessee this season.

“(Britton) had command over pitches that night, I thought her offspeed was working well and keeping them off time,” Crowell said. “She did exactly what we needed her to do and she executed our game plan at a very high level against Tennessee."

Weekend takeaways

Penn State combined for just two runs and four hits in its two games against the Lady Volunteers; granted, Tennessee has the nation's best ERA at 0.53.

Aside from the Lady Volunteers, the Nittany Lions earned two wins over NC Central and one against Appalachian State. In these matchups, the blue and white combined for 30 runs, while allowing just five.

“Tennessee is one of the best pitching staffs in the entire country, they have only one time given up more than one run, so we knew going into that series it was going to be tough,” Crowell said. “But I love the way the offense responded in the other three games against Appalachian State and NC Central.”

So far this season it hasn’t just been the offense guiding Penn State, but pitching has been firing on all cylinders as well. The Nittany Lions’ 1.98 ERA ranks 10th nationally and the staff has accumulated 119 strikeouts on the year.

In Sunday’s weekend finale, junior right-hander McKenna Young posted the 16th no-hitter in program history with five strikeouts as the blue and white defeated NC Central 14-0 in five innings.

“We came into this year saying we’re going to be a very well-balanced team, meaning we can hit, we’re going to pitch strong and we’re going to play solid defense, and I think we’ve seen that,” Crowell said.

Going 1-0

With a 16-4 record, Penn State has had a lot of its success building off its previous performances. Before falling to Tennessee last Friday, the Nittany Lions posted a win streak that spanned 10 games.

The blue and white have had its fair share of tough opponents and will continue to face more in the future.

“We went to Tennessee because we wanted our student-athletes to play in an electric environment, which really that was,” Crowell said. “They need to be ready when we go to Oregon to play in their stadium in front of thousands of people.”

Penn State will have a few tune-up games before it faces the Ducks to start Big Ten play; one of these teams is No. 11 Virginia Tech. The Nittany Lions face the Hokies at 3:30 p.m. on Saturday to close out its upcoming weekend.

However, Crowell’s squad isn’t immediately focused on Virginia Tech, but rather taking things one game at a time, to keep the team's 1-0 mentality.

“For us, it's really about one game at a time, one team at a time, doesn’t matter who we’re playing,” Crowell said. “The Virginia Tech game on paper is a really big game, but our kids are locked and loaded right now to go 1-0 against Radford in game one.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball adds home game against Bucknell
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added another home game to its schedule after a few games were previously canceled due to inclement weather.

🚨Game Added🚨We have added a home game against Bucknell on Wednesday, March 25 at 5:30 p.m. to our schedule!#WeAre pic.twitter.com/zDvKV3hD6d— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) March 4, 2026

The Nittany Lions will face Bucknell at 5:30 p.m. on March 25 in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park. Penn State dominates the series, holding a 40-7 record against the Bison.

The blue and white already had an away game against Richmond canceled on Feb. 22 and a home game against St. John's canceled this week.

Coach Mike Gambino's squad has started off rocky with a 3-6 record, but the Nittany Lions host their home opener against St. John's at 4 p.m. on Wednesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | Hey doc, listen up!: The importance of compassionate, thorough healthcare providers
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
“Wait a minute, back up. How long has this been going on?”

I blink at the doctor as she stops me. I’m mid-listing (and simultaneously dismissing) a symptom that, over the years, I’ve had doctors blame on everything under the sun: my stress, my workload, my (alleged) anxiety, my age, my attitude, me.

“Um, about three years,” I say, trying to cover my complete and utter shock at the idea that she might find this important. “I have a document…”

Her eyebrows knit together as I tell her every instance. Then she says, “Why would they say that was normal? That’s not normal at all.”

I leave that day with not only a treatment plan for the issue I came in for, but with orders for various tests and an arsenal of knowledge on what might happen next. The doctor gives me reassurance, too: I believe you. I’m glad you’re asking questions. You shouldn’t have to tolerate this.

Later, my dad asks me how it went, how she was and if I “got a nice one this time.”

“Worth every penny,” I squeeze out, and burst into tears.

I haven’t always been so lucky, and I’m far from alone in that. 52% of patients in the United States feel their symptoms have been dismissed by healthcare professionals, according to the 2022 MITRE-Harris Poll on Patient Experience.

This may not be surprising for some, but the number rises alarmingly when you factor in things such as race and sexual orientation.

Anecdotal evidence will tell you much of the same thing. When I interviewed sources for my article on endometriosis, I was told about providers dismissing patients due to age, weight, reproductive choices and perceptions of the patient’s lifestyle.

I myself have had doctors accuse me of all sorts of things, from being “hyperfocused” on my symptoms to feeling “impossible” pains, only for another doctor to say, “No, that’s a real issue.”

And if they can’t blame your symptoms on you, there’s a chance the provider will blame them on a mental health condition, whether you have one or not.

That attitude only further stigmatizes mental health conditions, characterizing people who have them as liars and driving individuals away from a diagnosis, for fear that future providers won’t be able to see past it.

And real mental health concerns are, unfortunately thanks to stigma, treated by the world at large as character flaws. So the final diagnosis becomes: personal failing.

Even if they do treat you, in my experience, many have been so mean about it that I left the appointment wondering why I’d bothered.

I feel the need to clarify that I do have a profound respect for healthcare providers, especially in our post-pandemic world. Many are compassionate, innovative and dedicated to their field and patients.

And I understand the old adage, “When you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras.”

More often than not, it’s something simple. And in our healthcare system, you don’t want to order an MRI for the common cold and have the patient foot the bill.

But where in that saying does it tell you to not stick your head out the window and check? Especially when nothing else makes sense?

Put another way: should blame and dismissal really be the default?

As college students, the general assumption about our demographic is that we’re spry, healthy and simultaneously anxious and unmotivated.

But for many, if not most of us, one or more of those things just isn’t true. Sure, it’s a reasonable generalization, but every individual is worth the effort it takes to check — because even if some of those assumptions are true for someone, there are nuances to it.

Someone can be anxious and have something wrong with their heart, for example. They can be athletic and motivated, and still regularly be debilitated by pain.

Things don’t necessarily cancel each other out, and the thing is, healthcare providers should know this. But in a world where so many sometimes act like they don’t, I want you, reading this column, to know that.

No matter who you are or what your life looks like, you deserve a doctor who'll chase down every possibility, who’ll be happy to see you out of their office because you’re progressing, not because they’ve shut you up.

If you’re dealing with a physical or mental health issue, I want you to know that your symptoms are more than likely real. You live in your body and mind, and you can be trusted to know when something’s wrong.

I encourage you to advocate for yourself, even when it’s hard. Because above all, you deserve to achieve whatever health goal you have. Know your rights, ask questions and don’t let anyone lead you away from the truth.

I hope that one day, things look up for you, and you can say exactly what I said to my doctor when she told me something was wrong: “Oh my god, I knew it.”

MORE COLUMNS

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s hockey readies for regular-season finale against No. 11 Wisconsin
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A season filled with high expectations has gone according to plan for No. 6 Penn State, which enters its final regular-season series primed for another crack at a national championship.

After a shootout win followed by an overtime loss against Notre Dame last weekend, the 20-10-2 Nittany Lions return to Pegula Ice Arena for a two-game set against No. 11 Wisconsin. The series starts at 7 p.m. Thursday and continues at 7 p.m. Friday, with both games streamed on Big Ten Plus.

When these sides met in late January, Penn State outscored the Badgers 10-3 en route to a series sweep. But the real fireworks occurred postgame when Wisconsin coach Mike Hastings refused to shake Guy Gadowsky’s hand after the series finale ended with a brawl that resulted in 11 players receiving game misconducts.

Gadowsky said the hostility was “just hockey.” And while Penn State owns a 99% chance of making the NCAA Tournament, according to Playoffstatus.com, its coach refuses to take the Badgers lightly.

“We are not in yet. It's not mathematically locked yet. So I think that's important to know,” Gadowsky said. “This is a great dress rehearsal for what playoff hockey is going to be like … Wisconsin needs to win to get in. We need to win to mathematically lock it up. I think this is going to be a great opportunity as a dress rehearsal for the playoffs.”

Scouting Wisconsin

Wisconsin was rolling early en route to a 15-3-2 record through its first 20 games. But it’s been a bumpy ride since.

The Badgers are just 4-8 over their last 12 contests, including a six-game losing streak that dropped them outside the USCHO’s top 10. And despite that recent slide, Penn State defenseman Jarod Crespo said the team refuses to look past what it expects to be another competitive series.

“This weekend is a precursor to playoffs,” Crespo said. “(Wisconsin) is fighting for a spot. We're still fighting for a spot, as much as it doesn't look like it. This is going to be playoff hockey this weekend, so to kind of keep that edge, just focus on this weekend and play like it's a playoff game both nights.”

Gavin Morrissey leads Wisconsin with 29 points and 23 assists — seven more helpers than his next closest teammate. Morrissey didn’t play in the January series between these teams, so this will be his first look at this year’s Nittany Lions.

Christian Fitzgerald, who in January recorded two points against Penn State, and Quinn Finley are Wisconsin’s top goal scorers. Fitzgerald’s 15 goals pace the Badgers and are tied for the eighth-most in the Big Ten.

Daniel Hauser has started 23 of Wisconsin’s 32 games in net. The freshman goaltender sports a 16-6-2 record, which ranks third in the Big Ten, and a 2.48 goals-against average that ranks fourth.

White Out Energy

Friday’s contest will serve as Penn State’s annual White Out game, which encourages fans to wear white and create an electric atmosphere rarely seen across college hockey.

The Nittany Lions knocked off then-No. 2 Minnesota in last year’s “Wear White” game. They’ll look to replicate that success amid a sea of white in their regular-season finale — one that could hold major seeding implications ahead of the NCAA Tournament.

“It’s the White Out. It’s one of the weekends we look forward to,” senior defenseman Carter Schade said. “In all three years I’ve had here, it’s the craziest atmosphere I’ve ever seen.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Worst moments during Penn State women’s basketball’s losing season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State finished the 2025-26 season in the losing column, marking its sixth losing season in seven years under coach Carolyn Kieger.

In addition to the overall 11-18 record and missing the Big Ten tournament for the second straight year, there were many painful moments for the Lady Lions, which summed up the overall struggles this year.

Upset loss to Rutgers

The Lady Lions weren’t favored to win many games when the Big Ten schedule began. However, one that they were supposed to win was against Rutgers, when the blue and white welcomed it in for the White Out game on Jan. 18.

After falling 22-17 to open the first quarter, the Lady Lions outscored the Scarlet Knights in Quarter 2 and 3, entering the final 10 minutes up by three points.

Kiyomi McMiller and Gracie Merkle each played a big role through the first 30 minutes, tallying 18 and 13 points, respectively. Down the final stretch, Rutgers got the better hand and defeated Penn State 76-72 as the Lady Lions continued searching for their first conference win of the year.

Kiyomi McMiller vs Indiana

McMiller was playing her best basketball in February, tying Caitlin Clark’s Big Ten record of six straight 30-point games.

Coming off a 40-point game where she surpassed 1,000 career points, McMiller’s fate changed in the final game of the year against Indiana.

McMiller shot the ball 12 times — nearly half of her typical attempts — and made just one shot as she was held to four points against Indiana.

In what was a must-win game to clinch a Big Ten tournament seed, Penn State’s biggest star fell short of her typical production as the Lady Lions fell 93-59.

2nd quarter against Arizona State

Penn State’s return to the Bryce Jordan Center was met with a big challenge against then-undefeated Arizona State, which had former Lady Lion Gabby Elliott and former assistant coach Daniel Barber on the squad.

After a strong first quarter put the blue and white ahead by five points, the Lady Lions were held to just four points in the second quarter while the Sun Devils pulled ahead 33-22.

Penn State got off 11 shots in the quarter, which was the lowest of the night, shooting at an 18.18% success rate. Merkle started the quarter with a layup 55 seconds into the action and finished things with the only other basket coming with just nine seconds left in the quarter.

Gracie Merkle’s injury

While Merkle played a big role in the team’s victories this year, Penn State could’ve had more success if she were available the entire season.

The 6-foot-6 center missed a pair of games against Oregon and Minnesota to end January before playing against Purdue on Feb. 4 and missing a game against Michigan State on Feb. 7.

Outside of those three games, Merkle scored double-digits in all but one contest as she averaged 19.2 points and 8.2 rebounds per game this season.

11-game losing streak

A majority of the Lady Lions’ 18 losses came during their losing streak from December into January, when Penn State lost 11 straight games — tied for the second-most in school history.

After opening the 4 Tha Culture Holiday Classic with a win against VCU, Penn State dropped the final game of its nonconference schedule against Richmond before losing 10 straight Big Ten contests.

The Lady Lions were blown out by Iowa and UCLA before falling by just one point to Wisconsin. The blue and white then experienced six double-digit losses over the next seven games, with its four-point letdown to Rutgers being the only exception.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Review | Nosh Kosher Deli in North Campus is a 5-star experience
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
In need of some Jewish penicillin?

Penn State opened Nosh Kosher Deli in Warnock Commons in fall 2025. This restaurant in the dining hall sells a wide variety of kosher food, rooted in Jewish heritage.

It also offers other options ranging from create-your-own sandwiches and hot dogs to chocolate-y desserts.

Nosh opening offers a more cultural dining option at Penn State and kosher foods for those who eat by this diet, and frees students from the pizza slices and salads that are offered at other spots on campus.

Here’s my review of a few of the deli’s offerings, through the lens of my Jewish heritage.

Matzo ball soup

I can’t get enough of it.

Opening the lid to the bowl, I was nervously anticipating a diner-styled matzo ball, round and dense. But I was pleasantly surprised to see a bowl of soup that looked like the ones I eat at Passover Seder.

The matzo ball itself is light, fluffy and flavorful. You can really taste the matzo meal flavor in it, which is perfect for the dish — you know it was made just right.

Although the broth was a little bit darker than I’ve seen in the past, it was filled with delicious spices. However, I did miss the inclusion of vegetables in the soup, that's just my personal preference, but others may prefer it without.

I would recommend this dish to anyone at Penn State, especially if you’re feeling unwell, as it helped clear my cold right up.

Rating 9.5/10

Corned beef

I’ve never been a huge fan of corned beef, but I wanted to give it another try with Nosh.

If you have special dietary needs, this sandwich is offered with gluten-free bread. I appreciated the inclusion, so this gave bonus points to the restaurant and the dish.

However, I was not as moved by this dish like I was by the soup. The corned beef was a little too cold for my liking, but was as salty as it should be and very flavorful.

There are some toppings that come on the sandwich, so if you are not used to the addition of dressing and slaw, feel free to take it off just as I did.

So, while it’s not my favorite, this dish may still be the perfect lunch or dinner for many other students on campus.

Rating: 5/10

Potato latke

As I opened the container, I saw the round latkes, cooked with shredded potatoes, matzo meal, onions and oil.

They were very flavorful with all of the ingredients involved — each one was salty and savory — but the texture was not quite right in my opinion.

As I pulled the latkes apart, I couldn’t always see the strings of potato threaded and fried together in the dish. The texture was smoother, almost like mashed potatoes, as you chewed it.

Although this is how many restaurants make their latkes, it’s just not my preference.

The dish was lacking in crunch overall. I received three latkes in my order, all warmed, and while some had a crunchy outside, not all did, which I feel is a key characteristic of a traditional latke.

Rating: 6/10

Chocolate rugelach

I was a little overwhelmed at the appearance at first, as chocolate was oozing out of this pastry. But, boy, was the chocolate delicious.

As a chocolate fan, the flavor was perfect, sweet and smooth. The rugelach was soft, filled with sweet sugar and containing layers of chocolate.

While this dessert is typically very flaky and a bit more tan, this one was softer and pale, but still had some flakes within the dough.

I’ve eaten some like this in the past, and trust me, any rugelach is good rugelach.

This pastry was very tasty, flavorful and perfect for when you need that chocolate fix. I finished all three … rough day. It will boost your serotonin and improve your mood.

Rating 8.5/10

Final Thoughts

I think if you’re a Penn State student who enjoys Jewish cousine, you should definitely give Nosh a try. It’s great there’s a campus spot for those adhering to a kosher diet.

It really satisfied my craving for comfort food, especially with the matzo ball soup, so I will surely be back for more.

So, whether this is your kind of food or not, come “Nosh” on some Kosher eats and sample the rest of the menu.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: 9 movies to watch en-route to your spring break destination
author: Suzain Khan | The Daily Collegian
description:
Travelling for spring break is never just about the destination. It’s about the trip outfit, the overpriced iced latte, the group chat chaos and the delusion you adopt somewhere between boarding and baggage claim. In between the hustle and bustle of getting to your destination, what better way to pass the time on your trip than with a movie that matches the vibe?

Here are nine movies to download before you switch to travel mode, and to help deal with the bumpy ride.

1. “Mamma Mia!”

If you’re going somewhere warm — or wish you were — then this flick is mandatory. The chaotic energy, the dramatic friendships and the spontaneous breaking into song are exactly what every spring break group chat thinks their trip will include.

Bonus: you’ll feel 100% more glamorous looking out the plane window while “Dancing Queen” plays in your headphones, and Pierce Brosnan’s singing will remind you that even your worst karaoke endeavor will still sound good to someone.

2. “The Devil Wears Prada”

If in need of an airport confidence boost, “The Devil Wears Prada” will be sure to make you think your sweat set is actually couture.

There’s something about watching ambition, fashionable outfits and sharp one-liners while walking through a terminal that makes you feel important, even if you’re just flying economy with a backpack that’s barely zipped.

When embodying the attitude of Miranda Priestly, it’ll be giving “I have places to be.”

3. “Little Miss Sunshine”

“Little Miss Sunshine” is peak road trip chaos but make it emotional.

If you’re crammed in a car with friends, slightly irritated but pretending you’re not, this one hits. It’s awkward, funny and weirdly comforting, just like every slightly dysfunctional group dynamic that somehow works anyway.

4. “Eat Pray Love”

For the solo travelers or anyone pretending they’re on a spiritual journey when it’s really just a week-long break, Julia Roberts and her performance in “Eat Pray Love” serve as food for the soul.

Watch this if you want to romanticise everything: your coffee, your journal entry and the way you stare out the window like you’re rethinking your entire life.

Will you find yourself in seven days? Probably not. But it’s the aesthetic that counts.

5. “The Parent Trap”

Nostalgia is undefeated and Lindsay Lohan’s dual performance as twins Annie and Hallie will be sure to remind you of your childhood.

There’s something about watching this mid-travel that feels correct. Maybe it’s the summer camp energy or maybe it’s the chaos of switching identities. Either way, it makes travelling feel lighter, and will give you and your friends a new goal to learn the infamous handshake.

Also, with an elite comfort movie like this one, no stress is allowed.

6. “Crazy Rich Asians”

If your spring break budget doesn’t match your imagination, then pretend it does with the ultimate travel movie.

Luxury, dramatic romance, stunning scenery — it’s aspirational in the best way. You may be splitting an Airbnb six ways, but spiritually, you’re arriving at a private island wedding just like Constance Wu and Henry Golding, so use this movie to manifest.

7. “Before Sunrise”

This one goes out to all the overthinkers. There’s something about travelling that makes you reflective, and watching strangers talk about life, timing and connection while you’re in transit somehow feels poetic.

It’s quiet, thoughtful and slightly delusional which is perfect for staring out a window and pretending you’re in an indie film.

8. “The Hangover”

Not aspirational, just realistic and a tad bit over the top humor to distract you from the remaining hours until you reach your destination.

If your friend group is already slightly concerning, this is the perfect pre-trip omen. Watch it as a cautionary tale, or a blueprint.

9. “Call me by your name”

For when you want the trip to feel aesthetic, immerse yourself into Luca Guadagnino’s film packed with warm scenery, slow conversations and emotional tension. Watch this and suddenly your destination feels like a coming-of-age film set in Europe, even if you’re just going to the neighbouring state.

Spring break trips come and go quickly. The tan fades, the group photos get posted and suddenly you’re back in class pretending you didn’t just live your best life for seven days.

So, download the movie, romanticise the turbulence and make the way there feel cinematic.
