title: Penn State notebook | Guy Gadowsky talks injuries, looking ahead and more
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
With No. 6 Penn State’s regular season coming to a close this weekend, Guy Gadowsky met with the media on Tuesday ahead of the final conference series.

The Nittany Lions are returning home after a series with Notre Dame, which held two consecutive overtimes, locking up the No. 3 seed in the looming Big Ten tournament.

The blue and white will host No. 11 Wisconsin for a Thursday-Friday series at Pegula Ice Arena later this week before postseason play begins.

Lessons from South Bend

If the Nittany Lions wanted to make a last-minute push to climb the conference standings, they needed two sound victories against the Fighting Irish. Instead, the unranked Notre Dame team took Penn State to extra minutes both nights.

The blue and white took the extra conference point in the shootout on Friday, while the Fighting Irish found the back of the net just 47 seconds into overtime on Saturday.

Both nights featured late comeback bids from Penn State — first erasing a three-goal deficit, then finding the tying goal with under three minutes to play — which is something Gadowsky praised.

“It seems like we’ve had this conversation before…as many times as it happens, it doesn’t get less impressive,” Gadowsky said. “(The players) keep impressing us, the coaching staff, with their ability to come back. We keep striving for a situation where we won’t have to.”

While the comebacks were a highlight of the weekend, they shone a light on the recurring issue of slow starts plaguing Penn State.

“We will certainly coach systems and objectives and habits, but you don’t want to coach desire and effort,” Gadowsky said. “I think that’s something that should be ingrained in what Penn State hockey is, and (the players) agree, so they agreed that moving forward, that’s on them to be ready to compete.”

Injury updates

A light in the darkness that has been the injury conversation surrounding the Nittany Lions recently is the return of sophomore forward Braedon Ford.

“(Ford) was awesome,” Gadowsky said. “He really takes pride in doing all the little things that really matter to a team and to set up his teammates for success.”

Sophomore Charlie Cerrato has been a big name on the injury list since going down in early January, and Gadowsky also provided an update on the star forward’s status.

“It’s still day-to-day,” Gadowsky said. “No decisions will be made on this weekend until after tomorrow.”

The leading scorer at the time of his injury, Cerrato’s status has seemingly improved over the course of the last few weeks, although he has yet to make his debut back on the ice.

Looking ahead

Penn State looks to take on Wisconsin in its regular-season finale, and both teams have stakes on this weekend’s outcome.

“This is a great dress rehearsal for what playoff hockey is going to look like,” Gadowsky said.

While the Nittany Lions are in a pretty healthy spot to claim a second consecutive bid to the NCAA Tournament, nothing is set in stone. The Badgers, on the other hand, are sitting right on the bubble of playoff hockey.

“Sometimes you get to the last conference games of the season, and you’re talking about two teams that are either locked in or locked out…that’s not the case,” Gadowsky said. “Wisconsin needs to win to get in, we need to win to mathematically lock it up.”

The blue and white were in a similar position to the Badgers last year on the cusp of a tournament bid, and the approach entering the final series will stay the same.

“It’s still, you want to be the best team going into it, whether you’re a lock for the tournament or not, and that’s sometimes a lot easier said than done,” Gadowsky said. “It’s important to do our best to make sure we’re prepared to be the best team.”

title: ‘Wrestling is not close your eyes and feel movements’ | Mitchell Mesenbrink’s mind boosts on mat success
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
It isn’t all just about wrestling for Mitchell Mesenbrink. In his redshirt junior season, he’s showcased more and more of his personality, which features singing and philosophy.

Mesenbrink has connected his off the mat interests into wrestling, which has played a part in the Nittany Lion losing only one match across his collegiate career.

“Every time I listen to music, I want to enjoy listening to music,” Mesenbrink said. “I'm not putting on music just to have a background noise, I want to enjoy what I'm listening to. So I think that kind of translates parallels to wrestling. Like when I go out and wrestle, I want to enjoy it. I don't want it to be like a job.”

Mesenbrink is one of the most dominant wrestlers in the sport today. However, when he’s not on the mat, he’s reading up on some of his favorite philosophers, including Socrates, Aristotle and Plato.

One of the favorites for the Hodge Trophy, Mesenbrink has been dominant, winning all 19 of his matches this season by bonus points. When he’s on the mat, there’s a high likelihood he’s intentionally, or unintentionally working his mind like one of his favorite philosophers.

One of Aristotle's major philosophies focuses on empirical observation, which is the process of gathering information about the world through the direct use of the senses, per Plato Academy.. In wrestling, two of the five senses — touch and sight – are major components to the sport.

“I think psych, knowing how people work, and then also how you can make relationships with people is good, and I think is probably a key and a tool that you're always going to be able to use,” Mesenbrink said.

For the Hartland, Wisconsin, native that’s a highly important technique and skill to use given the fact he doesn’t look ahead to his matches. With the Big Ten Championships days away, Mesenbrink doesn’t look at seeding, or who his next opponent could or will be.

“I'm gonna be honest, I don't even look at the bracket,” Mesenbrink said. “Even after I wrestle, I don't look at the bracket.”

Despite this potentially being unusual for wrestlers, who would seemingly want to know who their next opponent is and what their next opponent tends to do, it hasn’t had any consequences for the reigning national champion.

Mesenbrink is 15-1 in the Big Ten and NCAA Championships, with his only loss coming in his redshirt freshman season in the national championship match against Iowa State’s David Carr. Even with that loss, Mesenbrink was right in the match and had opportunities to win against a wrestler who’s now taken the world championship stage by storm.

This is where that mentality of using those senses really comes into play, as the 165-pound wrestler hasn’t lost in almost two years.

“I think there's definitely little things that you can implement in your style, and also, in the nonverbal situations as well,” Mesenbrink said. “Wrestling is not close your eyes and feel movements, like you got face-to-face interaction. You got all different types of things that can be used psychologically to or against your advantage.”

Penn State has been a dominating force not only on the national stage, but also on the conference stage in the Big Ten Championships. This year, the conference title will be battled for at the Bryce Jordan Center, home to some of Penn State’s bigger duals. Some Nittany Lions might find an advantage in that, but Mesenbrink just sees it as another day in the office.

“What are the advantages of having it here and being in a familiar environment?” Mesenbrink said. “I kind of enjoy being on the road, too. Like it doesn't really matter where it is, either way it's a wrestling match, either way you throw someone up there and we wrestle. So it's kind of a regardless thing. I'll do it regardless of where it is.”

In his bracket, he could potentially face Iowa’s Mikey Caliendo for the eighth time in their

careers, or face Purdue’s Joey Blaze for the first time in their collegiate careers.

“I think there's challenges in both, where you're trying to hit new stuff or same stuff on new or old opponents,” Mesenbrink said of the pair. “There's a duality between both of it. It can be different and it can be the same.”

The Big Ten Championships are ultimately a preparation aspect for the National Championships, which become preparation for the World Championships, as the revolving door of preparation has become a major focal point under Cael Sanderson.

“It helps in constantly getting better at wrestling, but not only that, I think, as an opportunity to kind of keep getting better and seeing the things that I'm working on,” Mesenbrink said.

title: ‘I got something to prove’ | Penn State CB Audavion Collins wants everyone to remember his name in 2026
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
When Audavion Collins transferred to Penn State from Mississippi State in May 2023, he did so because of cornerbacks coach Terry Smith. When Collins announced his return to Penn State for his redshirt season, he did so because of Smith.

“Coming through the portal, coach T always had faith in me,” Collins said of Smith last week. “He’s a guy that I can depend on. I could talk to him about anything. Knowing he was coming back was real big for me.”

With his return comes new expectations, as expressed by both himself and Smith. Collins is now the oldest cornerback in the room, so Smith has tasked him with being more of a leader.

“It's my room, so therefore I got to do things that I didn't do last year,” Collins said. “Bringing other people along and being more of a leader. I most definitely have to take a step in doing that. Getting on corners, safeties, more. Coming in together and we working out together, other than me coming in and working by myself to try to pull everybody along.”

Collins said emerging as the veteran presence and the voice people listen to was a bit of an adjustment at first, but he then added he’s finding his rhythm with the newfound responsibility, and it’s nothing he can’t handle.

Part of that change has to do with growth, a noticeable personal development since Collins set foot in State College in 2023. The Covington, Georgia, native said he’s “way different.”

“Young man when I first got here,” Collins said. “I was lost, still trying to find myself. Now, I got everything figured out.”

He credited older cornerbacks in the room over the years for helping him find his footing. Collins name-dropped Kalen King, Johnny Dixon and Jalen Kimber as former position mates who aided him.

But Collins hasn’t just grown off the field; he’s made strides on it. Smith and James Franklin praised him during the 2024 offseason as someone who has made the biggest strides among the whole team.

He flashed that season, but was slightly buried on the depth chart. In 2025, Collins earned himself a full-time starting gig, finishing the campaign with 47 total tackles and three pass breakups, both of which were single-season career highs.

“Always willing to improve in everything. If you want to be the best, you got to always be willing to work,” Collins said. “Even though I know I'm physical, I could be more physical. I could be more attached to the hip on my coverage. There’s always more, especially when you try to chase greatness.”

There’s an argument to be made that Collins wasn’t talked about enough for his play last year. From a cornerback's perspective, it’s hard to be brought up when the ball isn’t thrown your way, because all that is for the position is doing your job.

Not being brought up isn’t anything new to Collins. He was a lower-rated, 3-star recruit via the 247Sports composite rankings in the 2022 recruiting class. When he left the Bulldogs to hit the portal, he publicly announced offers from Western Kentucky and Akron in addition to Penn State.

Collins said he felt like they tried to toss him “under the rug.”

“I've been an underdog my whole life, so it's not new to me,” Collins said. “I got something to prove. I always feel like I got something to prove every day.”

Collins returned to the Nittany Lions for the 2026 season with a sense of appreciation for the program that recruited him out of the transfer portal four years ago.

With one more year of eligibility left, Collins wants to make the College Football Playoff and win a national championship, giving back to a program that he believes has changed his life. As far as what he wants to accomplish for himself?

“The world gonna see pretty soon,” Collins said.

title: 'Hadestown' takes Penn State to the underworld
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
The touring cast of the Tony Award-winning musical “Hadestown” took the Eisenhower Auditorium to the underworld Monday night with a tragedy of romance, trust and song.

The musical, a retelling of the Greek myth of Orpheus and Eurydice, uses rustic aesthetics, jazz and rock music, political upheaval and poverty to intertwine the lovers’ story to that of Hades and Persephone. It transforms the myth to an even more relatable story about love and loss.

Cadence Jabco said the story of Orpheus and Eurydice is one of her favorite tragedies, and Greek mythology has been one of her special interests for almost a decade. Even though she loves the original story, she said she enjoyed the adaptation.

“It’s obviously not exactly the same because there’s some liberties taken with Hades and Persephone’s side of the story,” Jabco, a Walmart employee, said. “I think they’re doing a really good job with the different liberties taken with the musical itself.”

She especially enjoyed the scene where Orpheus and Eurydice met. Jabco said she enjoyed the “New Orleans” aesthetic and energy of the scene.

The performance’s playbill highlighted only 17 cast members, but they still offered an energetic show that connected with the audience.

Sierra Rosensteel said she really appreciated the energy level the small cast was bringing.

“I liked the moment where Persephone comes up to the real world,” Rosensteel, a teacher at Mount Nittany Elementary School, said. “When everyone is celebrating with her, that’s a really fun moment.”

While they all watched the same show, audience members attended for a variety of reasons: Jabco was attracted by her love of mythology, but Rosensteel was gifted tickets for Valentine’s Day. On the other hand, Laura Schlappich went to Eisenhower Monday evening because she is a longtime fan of Hadestown.

“I love this musical so much,” Schlappich, a second-year studying microbiology, said. “In this specific production, I loved all the vocalists because everyone was super strong. I loved Hades and Eurydice specifically.”

Schlappich also complimented the way the touring set was modified to allow for travel. She explained the Broadway set has a turntable and a substage that aren’t feasible in every location and said that, even though she wondered how they were going to make it work, she appreciated the show’s dynamic choreography.

Aside from its focus on mythology, the musical also has thematic undertones that connect it to ongoing political and socioeconomic debates. Although it can seem dreary, it ultimately offers a message of hope.

“I love Greek mythology, and I also love things that tie in critiques of the world that we’re in,” Schlappich said. “It combines those two things, the Greek tragedy and the current very negative view of the world that we’re in right now, but infuses it with a message of hope that I think is so important to keep in our minds and in our hearts if we want things to improve and want things to be better for ourselves.”

Even though much of the story was focused on the original tragedy and the new message of hope, the musical also brought to life the love of Orpheus and Eurydice.

Jabco was quick to point out that their love story does not have a happy ending — it is a tragedy, after all. But, for some attendees, that is part of what makes the story so special.

But, for some attendees like Schlappich, who had two identical streaks of mascara under her eyes, that is part of what makes the story so special.

"Hadestown" will come to the Eisenhower stage again Tuesday night at 7:30 p.m. Tickets can be purchased online through the Centre for the Performing Arts.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Drew Allar headlines key performances by Penn State players at 2026 NFL Scouting Combine
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Nine ex-Nittany Lions received an invite to the NFL Scouting Combine, and all of them had an opportunity this past week to make an impression in front of NFL personnel.

They were measured, participated in on and off-field drills, met with the media and had formal and informal gatherings with teams at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis.

Here’s how the week went for former Penn State players.

Is Drew Allar’s stock rising?

Of all the players that previously suited up in the blue and white, Drew Allar arguably had the biggest opportunity to make a name for himself.

It was in question whether he would actually participate, suffering a season-ending broken left ankle injury against Northwestern in October, but last week Adam Schefter of ESPN reported that Allar was cleared to throw, which ended up being the case. Allar told reporters in attendance that if he needed to go out and play a game the next day, he would be able to.

Allar didn’t run, though, which will be the case for his Penn State Pro Day at Holuba Hall on March 18.

During his media session, Allar said he believes he has the best arm in the draft. Despite the highs and lows of his Penn State career, it’s hard to argue with Allar, as that skill was on display on Saturday. As a result of his performance, he had social media buzzing about the intangibles he brings to the table.

Allar was a 5-star prospect out of Media High School in Ohio before arriving at Happy Valley. There were expectations he would be a first-round pick, and with a strong senior year in 2025, that would’ve been more than likely the case.

But he never truly panned out the way he was projected to. There were flashes at times, but a pair of game-sealing interceptions against Oregon, another one against Notre Dame and him disappearing in big games harmed his reputation.

Yet this isn’t a strong draft class at quarterback, so Allar can benefit from it. Indiana’s Fernando Mendoza seems like the clear-cut pick at No. 1 to the Las Vegas Raiders, while Alabama’s Ty Simpson is mocked to be the next gunslinger off of the board.

Due to Allar’s intangibles and his physical frame, he could be the third or even second quarterback off the board, potentially passing the likes of Miami’s Carson Beck and LSU’s Garrett Nusmeier, if teams are willing to bet on his potential over his production.

It’s another example of NFL representatives believing they can fix him, which could benefit Allar if it pans out, but his professional career could fall short if he doesn’t see strides over his early years or doesn’t fall into the right situation.

Defensive linemen impress

Much like Allar, Dani Dennis-Sutton and Zane Durant had an opportunity to see their stock increase with a strong performance at the combine.

The tandem of defensive lineman were the first of the former Nittany Lions to participate in on-field testing, and the duo set the tone early with a strong start.

Dennis-Sutton put together a historical performance, as the 256-pounder finished No. 1 in the broad jump and No. 6 in the 40-yard dash among defensive ends this year. Those finishes, paired with others across the board, gave him a 9.96 relative athletic score, a cumulative measurement for the combine. He placed ninth among defensive ends dating back to 1987, according to Math Bomb.

Durant made waves for his time in the 40-yard dash, 4.75 seconds. That number was good for No. 1 among defensive tackles and was quicker than NFL quarterbacks Patrick Mahomes and Dak Prescott. He also placed No. 3 in the vertical jump for his position group.

In recent years, Penn State has seen defensive lineman hear their name called early with guys like Abdul Carter, Chop Robinson and Micah Parsons. While Dennis-Sutton and Durant likely won’t find themselves getting picked in Round 1, their showings at Lucas Oil Stadium could’ve solidified themselves as Day 2 picks.

The rest

While Allar did end up participating in on-field activity in Indianapolis, Nick Singleton did not. The running back suffered a broken right foot at Senior Bowl practice in Mobile, Alabama, resulting in surgery.

He attended the combine on a motorized scooter, where he said he likely won’t be available for Pro Day but hopes to work out for teams before the draft. Singleton was viewed to be in the top tier of tailbacks before injury.

Singleton’s running mate in Kaytron Allen did participate in on-field drills but didn’t perform in any athletic tests. He’s been safely mocked in the middle rounds.

Offensive guard Vega Ioane has seemingly cemented himself as a first-round pick, and unless something significant happens to occur between now and the draft, he should hear his name called early. He did not do any athletic testing drills either, but had an on-field workout.

Offensive tackle Drew Shelton has put together solid outings and should be picked in the middle rounds. Among their position groups, Shelton was tied for eighth in the broad jump while Wheatley was tied for ninth.

Tight end Khalil Dinkins finished tied for second among tight ends in the three-cone drill. If selected, he’d likely be a late Day 3 pick. Teams might take a shot on him due to the recent run of Penn State tight ends in the NFL as well as Dinkins’ athletic profile that landed him a spot on the “Freaks List” written annually by Bruce Feldman of The Athletic.

While he didn’t receive an invite, long snapper Tyler Duzansky was one of a select few in his position to work out in front of scouts on the first day.

title: ‘Northern schools can fish too’ | Penn State Bass Fishing Club qualifies for championships
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Penn State Bass Fishing Club traveled 20 hours down south hauling a boat and trailer, facing a Florida cold front and launching across 50 miles of rough water to make its mark on the Lake Okeechobee College Series tournament.

Representing Penn State, fishermen David Buccafuri and Ryan Small placed 11th out of 220 in the tournament and qualified for the August 2026 Strike King Bassmaster College Championship in Minnesota.

“The first day, we finished 29th with just over 10 pounds of fish, the second day we caught three,” Small, a third-year studying wildlife and fisheries science, said. “The last boat to come in knocked us back to 11th place, the very last boat. Even so, we were just ecstatic that we finished so high.”

Small said he estimates the group’s move from 29th to 11th to be the biggest placement jump in the tournament.

The second day they had caught their qualifying fish at six pounds, six ounces — a fish Buccafuri, a fourth-year studying landscape contracting and design, said they didn’t even think was a bass at first.

“It's exhilarating, it's blood pumping, you get filled with adrenaline, but at the same time it's scary,” Small said. “When you have that big fish on the line, you don't want to lose it, because losing a fish that big would make or break.”

Small said the group fishes well together, and between all of them there’s a lot of experience.

“The reason we did well is we were in the right spot, we were doing the right things,” Small said. “We had the experience and the knowledge that it's one thing to catch a fish, but it's the next thing to get it in the boat.”

While only those placing in the top ten were financially compensated, Buccafuri and Small also entered themselves into a separate tournament strictly for participants with Johnson Outdoors’ equipment, which the group is sponsored by. Upon winning that bracket, they received a $4,000 set of shallow water anchors for their boat.

“That was huge for us, and it was huge for the company,” Small said. “Out of 220 boats, only ten or 11 had their product.”

With the support of an estimated 15 sponsors and strong alumni relationships, the club has about 45 members. The top 15 qualify for the tournament team. Locally, it fishes in Bald Eagle State Park, the Susquehanna River and Raystown Lake.

Bass Fishing Club President Brenden Leahy knew he wanted to join the club ever since he was in grade school, when he saw a booth advertising it at an outdoor show in Harrisburg.

“It's a really cool experience,” Leahy, a third-year studying community, environment and development, said. “As a club, and even all the other schools, we’re able to gather in one place and have a friendly competition, and share all kinds of things with each other and meet a ton of new people.”

He said fishing is more intricate than what others often think, and is more than a dock and worms.

“We talk about weather, we talk about water temperature, times of year, why fish are going to be here and on the other side of the lake,” Leahy said. “There's a lot of very specific details that go into fishing that makes it so special, and why we all like to share this passion because there's always something to learn, there's always something to discover.”

In going into the Okeechobee tournament, Leahy said the mindset to do whatever it takes was crucial. With careful planning and preparation, the group’s decision to take the risk and travel from the lake’s north to south end would put them in the best position for fish catching.

“We went across the middle of the lake, and there were like three foot waves out there higher than the sides of the boat,” Small said. “If the waves get too rough, if your boat takes too much water, it can get very dangerous very quick.”

With other tournaments approaching and the team looking forward to the championships in August, Small said the club will conduct research as to what it will face. The biggest resources are YouTube and other Bassmaster websites with content from prior tournaments, according to Small.

“You want to try to mimic exactly what they were doing, if they were doing well,” Leahy said. “Obviously it's not going to be exactly the same, but your goal is to get close enough so that you can replicate it.”

While bass fishing is a southern sport, going forward, Small said the club’s recent success proves they have the capability, the skill, knowledge and passion to compete.

“A lot of the southern schools do this, they take it seriously … they just fish every tournament everywhere,” Leahy said. “A lot of the northern schools get a bad rep, and I don't really think they get noticed. This is an example that northern schools can fish too.”

In addition to bringing in fish, the club also brings members closer together, according to Buccafuri.

“Friendships that I've made just from joining the club, it's been awesome,” Buccafuri said. “I’m graduating; I'm just going to fish constantly. I'm grateful to have made the friends that I have, and we're a very close knit group.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Spring Break outfits that won’t wreck your bank account
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
Spring break is right around the corner, and whether you’re heading to the beach, hopping on a flight with your friends or just romanticizing your week off in your childhood bedroom, it’s the perfect excuse to refresh your wardrobe.

Even if your bank account is still recovering from textbooks, coffee runs and that one “quick” online shopping spree that somehow turned into $147, you can still put together cute outfits without totally draining your money.

Looking good for spring break does not have to mean checking your balance after and immediately stressing. A little self-control before you checkout can go a long way.

Here are some simple places to start your spring break shopping.

Look downtown

Before you start panic-ordering online at 1 a.m., take a walk downtown and actually look around. Connections and Urban Outfitters are perfect if you’re trying to find something trendy or at least Instagram approved.

Connections is a good spot for going-out tops and dresses for dinner plans, even if the plan is just taking pictures before appetizers. If you want something that pops in photos and makes your friends say “Wait, where is that from?” this is your moment.

Urban Outfitters has easy, beachy looks everyone’s obsessed with. Think flowy pants, simple tees, fun bathing suits and pieces that stand out just enough. It can definitely be pricey, but the sale section is always worth checking. Even one statement item here can upgrade basics you already own.

Target and Old Navy

If you just need something simple you can throw on with anything, Target and Old Navy are always reliable. Target has seriously stepped up their bathing suit game, and Old Navy is basically the undefeated champion of affordable jean shorts and everyday tanks.

Are these outfits going to go viral? Probably not. They are dependable, go with everything and will not make your bank account suffer. Sometimes, it’s just the accessories that do the work. A little layering and a fun bag suddenly makes it look like you planned the outfit days in advance.

H&M and Forever 21

For something trendier that still won’t stress your budget, H&M and Forever 21 are worth checking out. They’re perfect when you want one fun statement piece without spending your entire week’s worth of coffee money on it.

H&M makes it easy to find dresses and coordinating pieces that look planned, even if you grabbed them last minute while speed-walking through the mall. It’s the kind of place where you can wear something to dinner and then throw on sneakers the next day and style it completely differently.

Forever 21 is a go-to for fun, colorful or bold styles. It’s not made to last a long time, but it’s perfect for beach pictures and going out to eat. Just go in with a little self-control because somehow three tops always end up in your hands and suddenly you’re explaining yourself at checkout.

Pacsun, Zara and Aerie

Pacsun is great for casual clothes that feel ready for the beach. It’s an easy spot to find outfits that just work. You can throw something on and know it looks good, perfect for a beach day when you don’t want to overthink it.

If you need one outfit that looks a little more dressed up, Zara is the move. It’s great for going out, hanging with your friends or one night you decide to dress up just because you can. You probably don’t need a full haul, but one strong piece can pull your trip outfits together and make the rest of your suitcase look better.

Aerie is the quiet overachiever of spring break shopping. The bathing suits are cute and actually comfortable, which feels rare and slightly magical. It’s also ideal for oversized crewnecks, comfy sets and easy travel outfits for the plane ride home when you’re tired, a little sunburned and not interested in wearing real clothes.

TJ Maxx and Marshalls

TJ Maxx and Marshalls are chaotic shopping experiences in the best way. There’s no real system to it — just racks and vibes. You walk in looking for one thing and somehow leave holding something completely different.

It can take a minute to find something you love. You might have to dig through a few confusing patterns, winter sweaters that have no business being there and at least one item that makes you stop and question everything. If you’re patient, you can walk out with something that actually looks good and still save money.

When you find that one good piece between two random ones, it feels like a win. A little chaotic, but worth it.

ASOS, Princess Polly and resale apps

If you prefer online shopping (aka shopping in your pajamas), stick to the sale sections on ASOS or Princess Polly and use discount codes wisely. The codes are out there — you just have to look.

If your trip is coming up fast, Amazon can be a helpful last-minute option. Make sure to read the reviews and check the photos before you buy anything. Customer photos can seriously save you. It can really help when you realize you’re missing one last thing.

You can also try Depop or Poshmark to search for specific brands — including Urban Outfitters — at lower prices. A lot of sellers are other college students, which means you can find barely worn clothes for much less. It’s affordable and gives “I thrifted this” vibes without spending three hours digging through racks.

Spring break style should feel fun, not stressful for your wallet. Rewear what you already have, add a few key pieces and remember your future self will appreciate not maxing out your credit card. Your vacation look will hit harder if your bank account survives it.

MORE BLOGS

title: Penn State men’s basketball hosts Ohio State for Senior Night clash
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
For Penn State, only a pair of contests stand between it and the postseason. One home and one away, and it presents a good opportunity to finish 2026 strong.

The Nittany Lions earned their most validating win last week after a disappointing season paid off with a hard-fought upset over Iowa. Now the blue and white have a chance to pound the pavement and keep the results coming.

Penn State will host Ohio State for its final home game of the year with tipoff scheduled for 7:30 p.m. Wednesday.

Buckeyes' back-and-forth battles

Ohio State arrives in Happy Valley with a very interesting form and outlook, having only won two of its last five. Over that stretch, the Buckeyes faced four ranked opponents, Virginia, Wisconsin, Purdue and Michigan State.

Ohio State pulled off upsets over No. 24 Wisconsin and the highest-ranked opponent it faced over that stretch, No. 8 Purdue. The games against the Cavaliers and Spartans, despite losses, were both decided over only two possessions.

“Ohio State had a great win yesterday against Purdue,” Rhoades said at weekly media. “It's life in the Big Ten. We just got two big days here to get ready for Wednesday night, Senior Night.”

The Buckeyes may arrive in town without an AP ranking and a record that places them in the middle of the Big Ten pack, but they boast a resume that proves they can go toe-to-toe with anyone.

Senior vs. senior squabble

As Rhoades mentions, Ohio State enters for the Nittany Lions’ Senior Night, which may as well be “Josh Reed Night,” as the forward stands as the lone blue and white senior. Funny enough, the Buckeyes enter into town with temporary addition Puff Johnson, who joined midseason for a sixth year and actually celebrated senior night with Penn State last season, though he didn't play.

In that previous senior night, the Nittany Lions lost by four points to a ranked Maryland squad, and while it looks to be just as difficult a task this season, Reed has been on top form entering the game.

The Atlanta native logged double-digit points in his last 13 games, the longest streak of his career, and is fresh off a career-high 25 point night against Iowa. But Reed isn’t the only upperclassman looking to make a splash on Wednesday.

Buckeye star guard Bruce Thornton has scored over 20 points in five of his last six outings, which boosted him to 2067 career points. This means if he drops 29 against the Nittany Lions, he will become the Ohio State all-time leading scorer, surpassing Dennis Hopson’s 2,096 from his stint in the 1980s.

Thornton isn’t alone; joining the senior in the backcourt is sophomore John Mobley Jr., who follows him with 15.3 points per game.

“Thornton and Mobley get it going,” Rhoades said at weekly media. “They're two of the best guards in our league, maybe in the country.”

Stats, Evan Miyakawa’s outlook

In most, if not all, Ohio State holds the statistical advantages over Penn State across the board, with the Buckeyes’ middle-of-the-pack rankings in most categories outpacing the Nittany Lions’ bottom-tier statistical figures.

It reinforces the very real threat that the scarlet and gray pose when entering the Bryce Jordan Center, and that’s no secret for Rhoades and Co. Penn State lost by six when the two squads met in Columbus in late January, as the Buckeyes got hot offensively, shooting 56% from the field.

“They have their certain ways they're going to go about it,” Rhoades said. “If that gets going, teams are really hard to beat. Ohio State's one of those for sure.”

Statistician Evan Miyakawa’s advanced analytic formulas mirror those surface-level advantages that persist on the Big Ten stat sheet – the Buckeyes rank as a top-five offensive team in the conference, and seventh overall in his ratings.

Another bad sign for the blue and white, but it gets even worse with Miyakawa tabbing Ohio State as one of the most consistent teams in the Big Ten, while Penn State is one of the most volatile in performances.

It isn’t all bleak on the stat sheet for the Nittany Lions. Miyakawa has them ranked No. 4 in the Big Ten by pace and second in home field performances. When comparing five-man units as well, the Nittany Lions' best group has an efficiency rating of 48.2 over 70 possessions, while the Buckeyes post a 37.5 over 158 possessions.

Ohio State surely has the necessary starpower to bring home the win, but it’s just about countering a Nittany Lions’ unit that can cause some rumblings when fully clicked. There are some possible disruptions to that, however, star freshman Kayden Mingo enters for Penn State as day-to-day, while Ohio State is mostly healthy.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey sweeps Syracuse, advances to championship game
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State is heading to its fourth-straight AHA championship game after successfully sweeping Syracuse over the weekend.

With a 7-0 shutout win in Game 1 and a gritty 2-1 win in Game 2, the Nittany Lions managed to sweep their conference rival in all three series this season. After outscoring the Orange 9-1 through two games, the blue and white head into the championship game with momentum.

Slow start, strong finish

While the box score shows the opposite, Penn State started Game 1 against Syracuse on the slower side. Following the Nittany Lions’ bye week after finishing first in the AHA, signs of inaccurate passes and failure to transition from defense to offense plagued the Nittany Lions early.

That was until Tessa Janecke found the back of the net with just over six minutes left in the opening period of the series. The tally ultimately started a barrage of goals for the blue and white.

“In the beginning, we were a little slow, like we hadn’t played or we had a bye week. Basically, our passing was a little off,” Kampersal said following the Game 1 win. “Then, when we got that first goal, we started getting it going. The last ten minutes of the first (period) were good, and I thought the second and third were really good.”

Although Game 2 was a low-scoring affair, the Nittany Lions came out of the gates faster and made more accurate passes when compared to the opening game.

Syracuse played a more competitive game, forcing Penn State to take the series in a battle instead of a repeat of Game 1.

“I thought we did come out stronger than yesterday. I would have liked to have seen a couple more goals on the board in the first,” Kampersal said. “Getting the win was good, but (we) got a win in a different way where we had to battle a little bit.”

The Nittany Lions utilized one-time opportunities in the series opener, which was quickly shut down in the following game. The blue and white’s adaptability was put to the test by changing its game to a more physical approach, leading to a successful bounce back.

Olympic takeover

The last time Penn State’s lineup saw Janecke, Matilde Fantin and Nicole Hall was on Jan. 24 in a 7-0 win over Delaware. It didn’t take long for the three forwards to find themselves on the scoresheet.

In their return from the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan, Italy, all three Olympians scored a goal in Game 1 against Syracuse, with Janecke scoring her first hat trick of the season.

“It’s great to have (the forwards) back. I was just saying they’re a special group,” Kampersal said. “It’s been a special year and the fact that four players that haven’t been with us for a little bit all get on the scoreboard was kind of cool. Kudos to them and the entire group.”

After spending nearly a month away from the team and collegiate action, the pace of play could affect the players, leading to quick adjustments. However, it was quite the opposite for gold medalist Janecke in her return. The Orangeville, Illinois, native tallied five assists in the Olympics and returned to Happy Valley in dominant fashion, finishing the semifinal round with four goals.

“Realizing how many more games you have (at Penn State) and just being grateful to play with this group,” Janecke said. “I feel like when you come back from something like that, you definitely see more openings and it’s like the game slows down for you. So, I had a lot of opportunities today.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State softball found success in Tennessee Invitational
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
After Penn State’s successful undefeated weekend in the Sunshine State, the Nittany Lions traveled to Knoxville, Tennessee, for their fourth straight weekend series. The blue and white went 3-2 in Rocky Top, with tough losses in both of its matchups against No. 1 Tennessee.

Penn State started its weekend with a shutout, 6-0 win against N.C. Central. But, it was a split performance for the first day of the Tennessee Invitational, as the blue and white fell short against the host orange and white, 4-1.

Stepping back into the dirt Saturday, the Nittany Lions faced Appalachian State for their third game, coming out victorious after a 10-5 win. Going head-to-head against No. 1 Tennessee for the second time of the weekend, the blue and white put up a tough fight but ultimately fell short once again, losing 3-1.

To close out their weekend in Tennessee, Penn State dominated N.C. Central for its second game of the weekend, run-ruling it in five innings, 14-0.

Pitching depth

In the circle this past weekend, the Nittany Lions were a force to be reckoned with.

Right-hander Mackenzie Duncan started the weekend off for Penn State in the circle against N.C. Central, completing her first complete game in her collegiate career. She notched the first individual complete-game shutout by a Nittany Lion so far in the 2026 campaign, hitting a career-high of nine strikeouts and zero walks.

Abigail Britton, the right-handed pitcher, opened up the matchup against No.1 Tennessee. The LaVale, Maryland, native threw a staggering 4.0 shutout innings, allowing three hits and striking out six Lady Volunteers.

Right-handed pitcher Bridget Nemeth got the start in the circle for the Nittany Lions' second matchup against host Tennessee. She threw 5.2 innings, allowing two earned runs and striking out three batters. Nemeth threw one of her best innings in a single game since her freshman season in 2024, despite the loss.

Right-handed pitcher McKenna Young closed the weekend by spinning the 16th no-hitter in Penn State history, becoming the most dominant player on the field while collecting her first no-hitter at the collegiate level. She was just short of a perfect game because of one passed ball, but Young had no errors and struck out five batters.

Penn State's pitching staff has all the right tools and has utilized them effectively, creating a dominant force in the circle.

Newcomers

So far in the 2026 campaign, it has been the freshmen who have taken control and created the most opportunities for Penn State.

Once again, leading the blue and white was freshman third baseman Allison Oneacre. She was the only Nittany Lion with hits in both games on Friday, hitting a homer against the Lady Volunteers, becoming just the third batter to send it over the fence against a Tennessee pitcher in 2026.

Going 2-for-3 with a double and a home run against Appalachian State, the Alpharetta, Georgia, native followed that performance with a single off the wall against the top-ranked team in the nation.

Freshman Gabby Gradishar went 2-for-3 against N.C. Central, and the sixth inning was powered by the Champion, Ohio, native, as she got a two-RBI double.

Freshman first baseman Kirsten Finarelli was able to blast a homer, and freshman left fielder Breanna Hanik collected a pair of doubles against Appalachian State. Hanik ripped an RBI single against Tennessee to left field, bringing in the only Nittany Lion run of the game.

The blue and white’s offense was powered by the skilled signing of the 2025 class.

Penn State closed out its weekend in Knoxville and heads for its fifth straight week of nonconference tournament competition. The blue and white prepare to head to Virginia to participate in the Hokie Invite, notably facing No. 11 Virginia Tech. With a 16-4 overall record, the Nittany Lions aim to bounce back and extend their winning streak.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State DT Ty Blanding working to take next step ahead of redshirt junior year
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Defensive tackle Ty Blanding put three words on his phone’s lockscreen last summer: urgency, consistency and intentionality.

Blanding said proving himself has been one of his priorities as his role in the defensive line rotation has grown throughout his career, and those three traits are what he’s focusing on ahead of his redshirt junior year in 2026.

“When (defensive line coach) Deion Barnes was here, that's one thing we talked about, how I could improve as a player,” Blanding said last week. “My first year here, I didn't really play much, I was still developing. My second year here, I played a little bit. My third year, that's when I started to play a little bit. I really had to get the details of things down. Like I said, I gotta be more urgent, more consistent.”

Blanding was getting involved in the rotation last season, appearing in all 13 games and tallying nine tackles and two tackles for loss. He said he felt like he had more urgency, consistency and intentionality toward the end of the year, gaining more trust from his coaching staff.

The Bronx, New York, native was labeled a 247Sports composite 3-star in the 2023 class and redshirted his freshman year. He saw more playing time in 2024 by appearing in six games.

Blanding could’ve entered the transfer portal following last season due to the James Franklin firing, but that wasn’t an option he considered much as he already spent three seasons in Happy Valley.

“I didn’t really think of leaving, honestly,” Blanding said. “I knew like Penn State, we’re gonna be a good school. We're gonna have good people regardless. I feel like I'm comfortable here. I'm about to graduate, so I was like, I'm at a good school. I’ll get a really good degree from here.”

Blanding added he chose to remain at Penn State because of the culture new head coach Matt Campbell brought. He’ll also have a new defensive coordinator in D'Anton Lynn and defensive line coach in Ikaika Malloe.

While discussing the depth at defensive tackle during his National Signing Day press conference, Campbell rhetorically asked if Blanding would take another step forward this year, saying, “that’s gonna be great to watch.”

Blanding thinks he’s improved each season individually, which could set him up to take another leap in 2026.

“I feel like every year as a player, I've gotten better and better and better,” Blanding said. “I feel like last year was the step I needed. I got a lot of practice reps, but sometimes the practice reps aren't like the in-game reps. I got a lot of in-game reps. I feel like that's what I need to capitalize now. I feel like I know the game better. I could read the game better. It's slower for me, and I'm not as nervous as I was.”

Penn State has added size on the defensive line through the transfer portal this offseason. Blanding is working to bulk up, saying he’s up to 300 pounds to go along with his listed height of 6-foot-1.

While gaining size, Blanding said he’s kept his explosiveness as a defensive tackle. He said the last time he ran a 40-yard dash, he clocked in at 4.82 seconds.

It’s all part of the work he’s doing to fit into his new coaches’ style of play while staying true to the three traits on his lockscreen.

“I feel like we’re gonna rotate, gonna have different schemes, gonna have a five d-tackle front, pass front and stuff like that,” Blanding said. “I had to gain weight. I'm gaining 10 more pounds, so my goal weight is from 290 (pounds), 295 (pounds) to like 305 (pounds). And I feel like I’m working better at d-tackle, so I feel like I'm adjusting to their scheme.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | Morality meets modernity
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
Proselytizers and campus preachers see our campus as a moral battleground. They aim to save students’ souls from secularism and university culture.

While I fully appreciate the effort — I’m always in favor of more religious Christians — it seems as though these missionaries view Judaism as incomplete, a part one to an epic sequel.

Once, an unsuspecting Mennonite was so taken aback by me being Jewish, that her jaw dropped and she wasn’t able to finish her lines before I walked on.

I’ve been the first Jew several students on campus have met, which is unsurprising based on where the university draws from and the fact that Jews only make up about 2% of the American population.

This has never bothered me before, and most people are quite curious, creating a beautiful dialogue regarding our different faiths. Mostly, I hear from Mormons. They bravely face a rather hostile population, and I applaud them for their efforts. I know they, in their view, are attempting to save souls through Christ.

When an evangelist asks me if I’d like to hear a message, my go-to line is, “I’m a Jew. Thanks, though.”

But when I share I’m a Jew, they sometimes seem extra eager to “share a message” and tell me I can still come to Church.

Judaism is unique in we don’t believe everyone has to be a Jew to go to heaven or share in the World to Come. As long as you follow the seven Noahide laws, you’re in. In fact, Jews don’t proselytize at all.

A religion like Christianity, on the other hand, believes that everyone must follow their religion for salvation, hence the recruiters.

Judaism isn’t a stepping stone to Christ. Again, I greatly appreciate converters’ willingness to go out on a limb and attempt to save my soul, but I’m not your target audience.

I’m already religious, just not in the same or prescribed way. Though I know the goal for them is for everyone to be a Christian, I hope that this is enough for now as so many students claim secularism.

What I personally wish for the country in general, and campus in particular, is a strengthening of the religion students are already a part of. Christianity is obviously the default, and I hope our preachers lean into this.

With the right target, missionary is an important part of our country. It emphasizes one universal truth: there’s one set of rights and wrongs for all.

Modern campuses and students often claim, “my truth.” No longer do many people hold onto the truth, only each person’s subjective truth. Which, if each person can claim a different reality, negates reality at all.

Missionaries aim to correct this error.

Even as a religious outlier, I, too, see campus as a moral battleground, one that no longer teaches ethics or classics. Campus can be reverted to its original glory if only we prioritize the recruitment of those who already tangentially subscribe to our theology.

Chabad on Campus is a great example of this. They aim to reach all Jews, no matter what their level of observance. They’re not in the business of conversion, but rather the strengthening of Judaism as it already is.

This is your sign to use your influence, however small you may believe it is, to impact those around you for the good. To bring them to religion, to truth.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Season recap | Penn State women’s basketball continues decline in 2025-26 season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another year under coach Carolyn Kieger, another losing season for Penn State.

Despite entering the season with March Madness aspirations, the Lady Lions once again missed the NCAA tournament — and for the second straight year the Big Ten tournament — with an 11-18 record.

For the past several years with Penn State, Kieger has boasted her team’s ability to play into March. However, the Lady Lions have been far from that, not making the tournament since 2014.

This year, Penn State entered the year with one of its best rosters yet, composed of a lot of young talent.

Sophomore Kiyomi McMiller was the biggest name on the 2025-26 roster after transferring from Rutgers after her freshman year. McMiller’s first season with the Lady Lions produced one of the highest-scoring campaigns in Penn State history, scoring 627 points on the year.

Alongside the Silver Spring, Maryland, native was Gracie Merkle and Moriah Murray, who both entered the transfer portal last offseason and ultimately returned to Happy Valley.

Penn State’s season began with four straight wins, including an upset of Saint Joseph’s on the road. Merkle was a major contributor early in the season, averaging 19.5 points through the first four games.

Freshman Tèa Clèante also had a strong start to her collegiate career, going on to average 8.7 points per game with 10 double-digit games.

After falling in a high-scoring, 100-93 outing against Princeton where Merkle scored a career-high 39 points, Penn State began to struggle some, losing three of its next five games.

The second of which came against Arizona State, as the Lady Lions scored just four points in the second quarter. In a scrappy game down the stretch, freshman Shayla Smith had a career-night, scoring 12 points.

Penn State ultimately fell to three-time NCAA champion South Carolina in enemy territory before picking up its last win of 2025 against VCU. Senior Maggie Mendelson made her Lady Lion debut after spending the last two years with women’s volleyball, and scored eight points with seven rebounds and three assists.

With four losses to their name, the Lady Lions' losing season took off as Big Ten play rolled around, tying their second-worst losing streak in school history with 11 losses.

Despite the team's struggles, McMiller began to showcase her dominance as 2026 was underway, picking up her first of many 30-point games against Wisconsin.

After many more losses — including an upset loss to McMiller’s former team, Rutgers — the Lady Lions emerged in the win column on Feb. 4, defeating Purdue on the road in overtime. McMiller recorded her second 30-point game of the year, starting a streak of six straight 30-point outings.

Over that stretch, the blue and white were playing its best basketball of the year as it moved from last in the Big Ten to a possible tournament spot.

The Lady Lions kept things close against then-No. 12 Michigan State at home and No. 20 Maryland on the road, before winning three straight against Northwestern, Rutgers and an upset over USC.

Against the Trojans, McMiller surpassed 1,000 points in her young career, recording a career-best 40 points. Merkle also had another double-digit outing, something she failed to do in just one game this year.

Following its hot stretch and energizing win against the Trojans, Penn State had an opportunity to clinch its first Big Ten tournament bid since 2024, but fell 93-59 to Indiana as McMiller was held to just four points in the game.

For the seventh time in eight years, the Lady Lions’ season ended in a losing record as they packed their bags early and missed an opportunity to play in March.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Recap | Penn State men’s hockey struggles in trip to Notre Dame
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After having success against Notre Dame when it came to Happy Valley earlier in the season, Penn State had a different outcome when it traveled to South Bend.

The Nittany Lions took things to overtime on both Friday and Saturday, earning a shootout victory in Game 1 and an overtime loss in Game 2.

Between an interesting goalie series and struggles on the faceoff, here’s where things went wrong in the final road series of the year.

Rollercoaster for McKenna

Coming off a historic weekend with two goals and eight assists, many thought McKenna was playing his best hockey of the year with the postseason on the horizon.

However, the Notre Dame series was a slightly different story, as he was held to three assists and no goals. Still a strong weekend, but down from the series a week prior, where McKenna was a sparkplug on offense.

The projected No. 1 pick in the 2026 NHL Draft put up a tied game-high seven shots on Friday and just one shot on Saturday.

McKenna’s forte this year has been his assists, as he leads the team and is tied atop the conference with 33 assists on the year. But once again, that area of his game was another struggle this weekend, tallying just one assist on Friday and two on Saturday.

Despite the slower weekend statistically, the highlight of McKenna’s matchups with Notre Dame was his shootout-winning goal on Friday.

Faceoff struggles

Notre Dame came into Friday’s contest with just a 6-22-4 record, a team Penn State statistically should’ve beaten if it executed the basics, even on the road.

However, the Nittany Lions struggled in many aspects this weekend, with the most notable being faceoffs.

Friday night was the more successful of the two, although Penn State only won 43.1% of faceoffs. Ben Schoen led the way, securing six of his nine attempts, while Aiden Fink and Shea Van Olm, typical wingers, struggled the most with just a 33.3% success rate.

Coming into the weekend, the blue and white had won a slim majority of its faceoffs this year, sitting at 52% win percentage.

On Saturday, that number dropped even more as the Nittany Lions earned just 34.4% of faceoffs, with Schoen once again leading the way with 62.5% success. On the other end, Nic Chin DeGraves, Reese Laubach and Luke Misa won just 13 faceoffs lost a combined 32 attempts, with Chin DeGraves going just 1-for-6.

Fleming to the rescue

Josh Fleming has had a slightly better record than Kevin Reidler this year, and that was on display once again this weekend.

Fleming recorded 33 saves against the Fighting Irish on Friday, as he recorded a 91.7% save percentage.

The freshman goaltender faced an onslaught of shots in the first period as Notre Dame recorded 16 shots, 13 of which were on goal and Fleming saved.

Arguably the biggest moment of Fleming’s weekend was his shootout success on Friday night, as he went 3-for-3, denying the Fighting Irish from tying things up from McKenna’s early shootout goal.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Preview | Penn State women's lacrosse hosts Colgate
author: Aeryn Ajot | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State begins March with a game against Colgate at noon on Wednesday. It has a chance to continue the winning start, with a 6-0 record, it’s looking like a swift matchup in the midday competition.

With a strong history against Colgate, the Nittany Lions have a chance to continue that dominant history with their game against the Raiders.

Past matchups

The blue and white went against Colgate for the first time in 1989 and competed in nine more games since. The first game against the Raiders was a 22-3 victory. The program accounted for a combined score advantage of 151-52 in total competition against the Patriot League.

Since then, the Nittany Lions have competed against them numerous times, all ending in a celebratory victory. However, the most recent contest was over 15 years ago, in 2010, ending in a 17-7 blue and white victory. This next game will mark their 11th game against one another, and potentially another win against Colgate in Penn State’s books. Even with past success, both teams have changed since, so this rematch will be sure to cause new contention.

2026 Colgate

The Raiders currently holds a 2-2 record with losses to Binghamton and No. 5 Johns Hopkins.

Opening its season, Colgate defeated St. Bonaventure and Niagara, 14-12 and 16-10, respectively, but began a losing streak in its third game.

However, with a losing streak over the past two weeks and momentum gone, picking up a win against No. 17 Penn State may prove more difficult than previous successes.

2026 Penn State

The Nittany Lions sit pretty with a No. 17 ranking, beating out ranked teams in Pitt and Cornell with close calls — an overtime win against the Panthers, 12-11 and the Big Red at 11-9.

However, against unranked teams, the blue and white have found easy victories around the corner. The team defeated Drexel in a crushing 16-3 final.

Penn State has momentum coming off its sixth victory, and nothing is getting in its path to a flawless season.

Top performers

The 2026 Big Ten Women’s Lacrosse Preseason Players to Watch List included three Penn State players: Erika Ho, Sydney Manning and Rocquette Allen.

Ho stood out against Drexel with her impressive leadership and on-field attack. All the while, Allen made a career-high of three assists, and Manning captured four saves during the first half of the Drexel game.

Another player to watch out for is redshirt freshman Lilly Spiker, who, after her first playing season, has been ranked high in caused turnovers in the Big Ten.

With a surplus of talent on every corner for the Nittany Lions, the afternoon game will be a determinant on whether the Raiders has what it takes against a ranked team.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Cael Sanderson talks Big Ten Championships, Mitchell Mesenbrink’s off-mat pursuits
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has a big week coming up. The squad has the Big Ten Championships at the Bryce Jordan Center this weekend and the program has six No. 1 seeds in the tournament.

Ahead of the beginning of postseason wrestling, Cael Sanderson spoke to the media on Wednesday afternoon for the first time since the regular season ended.

Here’s what he had to say.

Big Ten seeding

The seedings came out for the Big Ten tournaments, and while Penn State had six No. 1 seeds, there was a key wrestler not listed.

That wrestler would be No. 1 Levi Haines, who is the No. 1 ranked wrestler at 174 pounds in the InterMat rankings and is undefeated. He is seeded No. 2 behind Nebraska’s Christopher Minto, who Haines defeated in the head-to-head matchup earlier this year.

“What happened isn’t going to work right? There needs to be a common sense application,” Sanderson said. “I don’t know if it’s too late to receive it as coaches, but maybe we should. It’s stuff (that) doesn’t make sense.”

Coaches can challenge if it’s within 15 points and there’s a chance that this could be the case.

“We do need to do the best that we can to make sure these guys get to the the seeds that they earned throughout the season,” Sanderson said.

Big Ten Championships

In the Big Ten Championships, wrestlers will be taking the mat for multiple matches a day. It could be anywhere from two to three matches in either day, which could give the wrestlers an advantage.

“I think our guys are always in top shape. Conditioning has always been a factor for us and our guys, and I think it gives them a lot of confidence,” Sanderson said. “We put in a lot of work in the last couple of weeks, and we’ve still got some work to do, but we feel pretty darn good with our conditioning. We’re ready.”

Not only are the Nittany Lions preparing physically, but they’re mentally preparing for this tournament. While wrestling is a team sport, it also can be a solo sport, and Sanderson emphasized that the program will be prepared in both ways.

“I think it all goes hand-in-hand. It’s a year-long plan, the physical training, the mental training, a lot of it's just your perspective and changing regardless of the circumstances,” Sanderson said.

Mitchell Mesenbrink

Mitchell Mesenbrink is one of the best wrestlers on the mat. He is undefeated, ranked No. 1, and is looking to become a back-to-back national champion.

But he’s more than that, as according to Sanderson, he has some interesting hobbies off the mat.

“He’s very creative and he’s very ambitious,” Sanderson said. “I mean, he loves music. He’s a very talented musician. He reads a lot. On off days when he’s off the mat, he’ll be sitting in the booths reading a book. I think he’s a kid who loves life and he lives life to the fullest.”

Sanderson highlighted his love of philosophy and music.

“He loves philosophy, he loves discussing all things,” Sanderson said. “He sings a lot. I think he plays a lot of instruments. He’s talked about playing the piano. I think he could play more than one instrument, but I don’t know for sure, but I know he’s passionate about everything he does.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball goes 1-2 in the Bahamas
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State Baseball traveled to Nassau, Bahamas, to face off against Indiana State for a three-game series from Feb. 27 to Mar. 1.

This was the first-ever NCAA Division I baseball contest to ever take place in the Bahamas. It allowed Penn State to improve to 3-6 on the season, despite picking up two losses and only one win.

In the first two matchups of the series, Penn State went winless, losing by small margins. The Nittany Lions lost the first game 8-5 and the second game 2-1, adding to their rough start to the 2026 season.

Fitzgerald and his defense

In the first game, Penn State starting pitcher Colin Fitzgerald started on the mound and pitched for six innings.

Fitzgerald put forth a balanced performance, striking out 10 out of 24 batters faced and only allowing one walk. However, he let up four runs, six hits and a wild pitch, but this doesn’t land solely on him.

Fitzgerald's ratio of strikeouts to players faced was solid, but the rest of the team's fielding allowed four runs and dug Penn State a tough hole to climb out of.

Offensive Revival

The third game is when Penn State finally came out on top in the series, and it came in extra innings. Like Saturday’s loss, the run difference was marginal. Going into this game, Penn State was on a four-game losing streak, with only two wins on the season, despite playing only nonconference teams.

The majority of the game was back and forth, with Penn State falling behind until the Nittany Lions pulled of the win in the 11th inning.

Little by little, Penn State had been chipping at the Sycamore's lead for three innings, and finally evened the score in the ninth inning, sending the game to extra innings.

This was a huge turning point for the blue and white, as the late-game resurgence carried the momentum they needed into extra innings.

This was a much-needed win to end their trip, as returning home with no wins could’ve been detrimental to their confidence as they enter the Big Ten season on Mar. 13 against Iowa.

Until then, Penn State has three more nonconference teams to face, its next match the Nittany Lions’ home opener against St. John’s on Wednesday, after its Tuesday match was canceled due to weather.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State delays Tuesday classes due to winter weather
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has delayed all campus classes, activities and work from 5 a.m. to 10 a.m. on Tuesday due to winter weather.

State College will be in a Winter Weather Advisory issued by the National Weather Service (NWS) from 4 a.m. to 1 p.m. Tuesday. As of 8 p.m. Monday, the NWS says State College is expected to receive one tenth of an inch of ice and less than an inch of snow, making difficult travel conditions possible.

The only people who should report to campus are essential employees, according to a PSU alert.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: How Penn State players are making the effort to learn more about each other
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s roster consists of 52 returnees, 23 former Iowa State players, 17 other transfers and 15 signees, leaving no lack of variety on Matt Campbell’s first team in Happy Valley.

But with all kinds of different player origins comes the difficult task of having them all get to know one another. It’s something Campbell and the team are still working on, but the camaraderie is far better now than it was in January.

“It's a great challenge to try to mesh a team,” linebacker Kooper Ebel said last Wednesday. “We've got guys that have been at Penn State. We got guys that are coming from Iowa State. And then we got guys coming from all over, so a lot of people from a lot of different backgrounds, different cultures, and we got the challenge of, ‘Hey, how can we make this mesh in a short amount of time?’”

Campbell has been spearheading a lot of the efforts to make the guys more comfortable around one another. He’s forced players to sit next to someone new at dinner or lunch, taken them bowling and formed small groups where people can be open about their history without feeling the pressure of standing in front of the entire team.

In fact, the Nittany Lions’ first-year head coach is so focused on team bonding that he planned a second bowling outing for Valentine’s Day without realizing the occasion.

“That didn't go over well with a lot of guys. But we've talked about doing that again,” linebacker Caleb Bacon said. “Just doing some team cookouts … kind of hanging out with guys, all things like that, even just going to hang out on a Saturday, just watching sports, do something with the guys.”

Team meetings are also more integrated, as linebacker Tony Rojas said they used to sit by position under James Franklin, but now everyone just sits wherever they want with whoever they want.

That was a bit awkward at first, according to quarterback Rocco Becht, as some guys didn’t want to step on each other’s toes or were more comfortable talking to people they already knew well.

“I would say the first couple of weeks was a little bit awkward with everybody kind of coming in at once,” Becht said. “It was 50/50 transfers, 50/50 Penn State guys. And that takes some time. You got to build relationships. You got to get to know people.”

Getting to know each other has also meant helping each other out. Running back Quinton Martin Jr. said he and fellow running back Cam Wallace have been showing their new teammates around State College and campus, while Iowa State transfer Carson Hansen helps them with understanding Taylor Mouser’s playbook.

It takes everyone opening up, though, to truly mold the team together. Ebel said he got up in front of his teammates to tell them his story, and others have done the same as the players continue learning more about each other.

“I think it's just guys putting down their barriers, getting used to each other, hanging out outside of the facility, things like that,” running back James Peoples said. “And I think we've been doing exactly that. And I think we've really been gelling together really well. I mean, guys are coming from everywhere, but I think that just makes it even more unique. Guys have their own experiences, their own stories, and you coming together for all the same cause is just something special.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Big Ten recap | Problems for Purdue, basketball squads post needed wins
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
It’s officially March, and that means postseason basketball is just around the corner. As March Madness and the 18-team Big Ten Playoff approach, both national contenders and those who find themselves filling out the standings look to make rumblings with their last games of the regular season.

The conference approaches its tournament in Chicago, and on the court, the play has already been just as effervescent as the hype.

Let’s recap the action around the Big Ten for the last time of the regular season.

Purdue gets fraud checked

Purdue had a great season on its quest to put together a run at postseason spoils, and it looked like throughout the year that it would make good on the attempt. Guard Braden Smith looked like he would be on pace to challenge Bobby Hurley’s all-time assist total, and while the senior recently became the fifth player in NCAA history to reach 1,000 assists, the Boilermakers as a whole haven’t had that same recent success.

In its last two games, Purdue sits winless. First, it fell to Michigan State at home in Mackey Arena 76-74, which marked the first loss to the Spartans at home since 2014. Smith finished with a Purdue high of 12 points, with a game-high ten assists.

Leading Tom Izzo’s unit was Carson Cooper with 15 points and the nation's assist per game leader, who finished with Jeremy Fears Jr., with 12 points and six assists.

Purdue had a chance to bounce back in its next game against a middle-of-the-pack Ohio State squad, but instead suffered an even worse defeat, losing 82-74. Smith bounced back with a 20-point night, but a pair of Buckeyes who dropped the same or more helped Ohio State cruise to victory.

Purdue started at No.8 in the AP Poll before both games, but following the losses, the Boilermakers plummeted to No. 15.

Penn State makes moment, Michigan bounces back

Penn State, which hasn’t had a good season by most metrics, didn’t appear as that same team on a warm Happy Valley Saturday. The Nittany Lions hosted Iowa, one of the most efficient three-point shooting teams in the Big Ten, while the blue and white were among the worst at defending that same shot.

Penn State rattled off nine made threes to a 60% clip from downtown, and cruised to an upset victory over the Hawkeyes, 71-69, thanks to a late-game Iowa foul that sent Freddie Dilione V to the foul line for the Nittany Lions’ late win.

Michigan, which finds itself at the top of the Big Ten, put up just the performance it needed in the past week. The Wolverines lost their second game of the season against another National Championship contender, Duke, in a close contest. But in the past week of play, Michigan bounced back against No. 10 Illinois with a 14-point statement victory to prove that, despite the doubts raised against the Blue Devils, Michigan still has a real chance at national glory.

The Big Ten tournament seedings look solidified with Michigan in the top spot, and Michigan State and Nebraska in an end-of-season battle for the second spot. The middle of the standings is likely to see small shakeups, as Maryland, Oregon and Penn State sit in a standoff for the bottom three seeds. Currently, the blue and white hold the sole spot at the bottom by one game.

title: ‘It’s flown by really fast’ | Josh Reed looks back on collegiate career ahead of Penn State’s Senior Night
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
About four years ago, Josh Reed was a 247Sports composite 3-star out of Pace Academy in Atlanta. He signed with Cincinnati as he began the next step of his basketball career.

Now, Reed will be the lone senior celebrated during Penn State’s Senior Night festivities against Ohio State at 7:30 p.m. Wednesday inside the Bryce Jordan Center. He’s played 126 games throughout his collegiate career, which is set to come to a close with the Nittany Lions’ season ending soon.

“It’s flown by really fast, kind of just looking back, it feels like just yesterday I was a freshman coming into college,” Reed said. “Now it's my last home game, so kind of a mix of emotions. I'm excited, but also, it's a little sad at the same time, but just looking to get a win. That's the biggest thing.”

Reed’s first collegiate appearance was with the Bearcats during a game against Northern Kentucky on Nov. 16, 2022. He scored five points in nine minutes as he garnered a role in the rotation during his freshman year.

The forward continued to be a rotational player at Cincinnati as his career went on. Reed made nine starts across 33 appearances, making some flashes of his potential toward the end of last season with a 19-point performance against Oklahoma State in the Big 12 Tournament.

Following his third season with the Bearcats, Reed entered the transfer portal in search of a new school for his final year of eligibility.

“One of the things that I told coach Rhoades when I was on the phone with him when I was on the portal was that I wanted to go somewhere where I can win and also have the best year of my career,” Reed said. “That's probably the biggest thing, just being at a place where I can be pushed by my coaches and teammates daily to get better, put in the work and see it show up on the court.”

Reed committed to Penn State last April, and he’s the only senior on the team’s roster, which is made up of mostly young players. He’s become a consistent starter for the first time in his career, starting in 28 of 29 games played this season.

The 6-foot-8, 230-pounder is putting up career numbers as he’s seeing more usage this year, averaging 11.7 points and 4.0 rebounds per game to go along with 50.9% shooting from the field. Reed is arguably the Nittany Lions’ most consistent player as of late, scoring in double figures in the last 13 straight games.

“I feel like this has definitely been the best year of my career,” Reed said. “I've been able to kind of play with a level of confidence that shows up on the court, and then just having my teammates back and having my coaches and teammates trust as well, like that's definitely helped me a lot.”

Reed played a main role in Penn State’s most recent game, which was a 71-69 win over Iowa last Saturday. He scored a career-high 25 points on 9-of-12 shooting in addition to four assists and three rebounds.

Reed’s career is peaking as his collegiate playing days are nearing its end with the Nittany Lions set to honor him on Wednesday. He’s one of Penn State’s team captains, and he’s known within the program for his character.

“He treats people with great humility and great kindness,” Rhoades said. “He's a very, very good teammate. He cares about his teammates, and he shows that he's not a screamer or a hollerer. He doesn't holler at guys like that. When he does it’s to get things right, but he does it in a way of helping guys, not knocking them down. So it means a good demeanor that way. The other thing is, he's accountable for himself. When he makes a mistake, he doesn't have an excuse or doesn't shy away.”
