title: Social media reacts to Penn State wrestlers’ pre-seeding in Big Ten Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
As the Big Ten Championships pre-seedings were released, people took to X to reveal their thoughts about some of the pre-seeds in weight classes.

The first seeding that sent social media into a frenzy was Levi Haines at No. 2 at 174 pounds. Nebraska’s Christopher Minto was given the top seed despite Haines beating Minto.

Lol, wtf. Levi Haines the 2 seed and Minto the 1.— Justin Basch (@JustinJBasch) March 2, 2026

LEVI HAINES THE 2 SEED?!— Kaiteee (@xOkaiteee) March 2, 2026

How in the world is Penn State wrestling star Levi Haines not No. 1 at 174 for Big Tens?— Joe Smeltzer (@joesmeltzer775) March 2, 2026

Haines defeated Minto in the Bryce Jordan Center dual on Jan. 30 by an 8-6 score, notching two takedowns late to get the victory.

Additionally, Haines is the top-ranked wrestler in the InterMat rankings, with Minto slated at No. 4, behind Iowa’s Patrick Kennedy, who also beat Minto during the dual season, but the Haines victory is what grabbed social media’s attention.

Ahh yes Levi Haines is the 2 seed lol behind the guy he beat and also has 3 other loses… lol what— Hunter Garman (@hunter_garman) March 2, 2026

Penn State’s Levi Haines is the no. 2 seed at 174 for the Big Ten Tournament. Make it make sense…— Zach Seyko (@zach_seyko) March 2, 2026

Two Penn Staters that could see tough early matchups are Rocco Welsh and PJ Duke.

Welsh, who is ranked No. 1, could see No. 8 seeded and No. 4 ranked Angelo Ferrari of Nebraska. Ferrari was likely punished due to an injury and missing time, but a potential national-championship esque matchup in the quarterfinals will set up for a potentially crazy Saturday-afternoon match.

Duke is the No. 2 seed, with No. 1 in the country Brandon Cannon as the No. 7 seed. Cannon and Duke was also hyped to be a potential national championship match, but Cannon’s lengthy injury dropped down his seeding.

It could be another early first-day match between Cannon and Duke if Cannon is able to compete during the Big Tens.

Brandon Cannon the 7 seed. 😳👀Would setup a match in 2nd round with the 2 seed, PJ Duke.— Justin Basch (@JustinJBasch) March 2, 2026

duke-cannon quarters is stupid also— Brandon Hess (@Brandon18602767) March 2, 2026

Congratulations to Rocco Welsh on the No. 1 seed. As a reward, you get Angelo Ferrari in the quarterfinals.— Clay Sauertieg (@ByCSauertieg) March 2, 2026

At 184, Rocco Welsh is the #1 and Iowa’s Angelo Ferrari is the #8 seed. Unfathomable.— K.J. Pilcher (@kjpilcher) March 2, 2026

All of the stir-up from the pre-seeds could be for nothing, as every seed could be challenged. Depending on what coaches challenge, the potential matchups could be completely different and no matter what the seedings are, the Big Ten Tournament will have many NCAA Tournament seeding implications.

Whether those happen in the quarterfinals, semifinals or finals, these matchups could happen no matter what.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Mike Rhoades gives update on Kayden Mingo, takeaways from Iowa win
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State picked up its third Big Ten win of the season over the weekend when it earned a 71-69 victory over Iowa on Saturday, and Mike Rhoades was back in the Bryce Jordan Center media room on Monday for his weekly press conference.

Rhoades offered an update on guard Kayden Mingo’s availability moving forward, in addition to explaining what mounting a winning streak could do for the program as the Nittany Lions enter the final week of the regular season.

Here’s more of what Rhoades had to say.

Kayden Mingo status update

Rhoades gave a status update on Mingo, who is dealing with an undisclosed injury after the Iowa win.

“Just day to day,” Rhoades said. “We’ll just see how he feels later today and tomorrow and go from there.”

Mingo first showed signs of an injury when he subbed out at the 10:04 mark of the second half in Saturday’s game. He returned to the matchup with 7:55 left, but he then exited a second time with 4:04 left, which is when he went straight to the locker room with trainers.

Following the final buzzer, Mingo came back out on the court to celebrate with teammates and participate in the singing of the alma mater.

Mingo is having a standout freshman season as Penn State’s starting point guard, averaging 13.7 points, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game across 26 appearances. He suffered a broken nose in early January that caused him to miss three games, and the Iowa game was the first matchup in which he didn't have to wear a face mask.

Guards Freddie Dilione V and Melih Tunca are able to take on ball-handling duties if Mingo is unable to play.

“We want (Mingo) back,” Rhoades said. “We need him, but we got to be ready if he's not ready to go so that those two guys have a clear understanding of the game plan. That's the key, especially as you go throughout the year. Here's the clear understanding of what we need to do in this game, and it's my job to make sure those guys know that coming into the Wednesday game with Kayden or without.”

Potentially ending season on win streak

With the win over Iowa, the Nittany Lions have a chance to end the regular season on a win streak. They have a game against Ohio State on Wednesday night before traveling to face Rutgers on Sunday.

Stringing together a few victories to close out the year could help the program heading into the offseason.

“It just gives everybody confidence and belief in what we're trying to do,” Rhoades said. “I've said it, this is all different. This landscape is different than it's ever been, and you got to navigate it all. What helps is positive things, and winning is No. 1 on that list. And treating your kids the right way, having success on and off the court, making guys better so they can get closer to their dreams and their goals.”

It’s been a tough season for Penn State, which is 12-17 overall and 3-15 in Big Ten play. The team currently sits in last place in the conference standings, projected to get the No. 18 seed in the Big Ten Tournament.

In other words, potentially picking up a few more wins down the stretch could help with buy-in for the future of Rhoades’ program, whether it be from Penn State’s administration or prospective recruits.

With the Nittany Lions realistically eliminated from making either the NCAA Tournament, the NIT or The Crown, Rhoades said playing spoiler and messing up the Big Ten standings can bring some motivation.

“As a coach, we all try to find angles and edges to motivate and get guys fired up,” Rhoades said. “The other side of it is, when you're playing for that, you're trying to find an edge and bring your team great awareness of what needs to be done so you can go achieve your goals.”

Turnovers issues

Turnovers have become an issue for Penn State’s offense as of late, as the team just had 15 turnovers against Iowa. The Hawkeyes turned those turnovers into 27 points, which was 39.1% of the points they scored.

Multiple times throughout Saturday’s game, the Nittany Lions mishandled the ball and gave Iowa essentially a free possession.

“It's those ones where you cannot defend,” Rhoades said. “I'd rather us throw it out of bounds and get back and set our defense and give ourselves a chance to get it back. Then, you just put the ball out there. Guys slaps it, boom. They get a layup. You can't defend that, so those are the killers.”

Earlier this season, Penn State was in the top five in the country when it came to not turning the ball over, according to Rhoades. Now, as the team is 18 games into its Big Ten slate, it’s averaging 11.3 turnovers per conference game with a turnover margin of -0.4.

Furthermore, three occasions in the second half of the Iowa game saw Penn State turn the ball over right after gaining possession via a turnover by the Hawkeyes. The Nittany Lions won, but it was times like those where they could’ve had a more comfortable scoring margin.

“When you get a turnover, you want to make them pay for that turnover,” Rhoades said. “Don't give it back. And then the other one, we just got to be strong with the ball. So I thought the other day we knew they collapsed really hard. I think we were a little soft with the ball at times. I thought late in the game we were much stronger and played off two feet, but we can't give that ball up.”

title: UPUA announces election information, executive tickets
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This story has been updated on March 5 to include another set of candidates.

The University Park Undergraduate Association (UPUA) has started to announce executive campaigns for the 21st general assembly alongside information for the upcoming election.

The three tickets currently include Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon, and Kat Hogan and Maria Lopez and Akito Hatch and Chloe Smith.

Mann, running for president, and Calderon, running for vice president, are campaigning on the platform of creating an environment that best represents students.

Hogan, running for president, and Lopez, running for vice president, have set their campaign focus on the goal of empowering undergraduate students.

Hatch, running for president, and Smith, running for vice president, have centered their campaign on institutional accountability and transparency.

Executive ticket candidates were made public today, the first day of campaign registration. All candidates, including executive and at-large representatives, have until 5 p.m. on March 16 to register for the election.

Voting day will fall on April 1. All undergraduate students at University Park are allowed to vote.

title: Penn State wrestling earns 6 No. 1 spots in pre-seedings for Big Ten Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After a dominant season, the Big Ten Championships pre-seedings have been released.

Six Penn State wrestlers earned top spots in their respective weight classes — Luke Lilledahl at 125 pounds, Shayne Van Ness at 149 pounds, Mitchell Mesenbrink at 165 pounds, Rocco Welsh at 184 pounds and Josh Barr at 197 pounds.

At 133 pounds, Marcus Blaze, who was the regular season No. 2 in the InterMat rankings, earned the top spot over reigning national champion Lucas Byrd of Illinois. Byrd was ranked No. 1 ahead of Blaze during the year. The two didn’t face off during the regular season.

Despite being No. 1 in the InterMat rankings, Levi Haines at 174 pounds received a No. 2 label behind Christopher Minto of Nebraska. Haines beat Minto 8-6 when the pair faced off this year.

Braeden Davis was given the No. 7 seed. Davis will have to finish right around his seeding, as the Big Ten earned seven-automatic qualifiers. He could still earn an at-large bid if he were to falter at the Big Ten Tournament.

At 157 pounds, PJ Duke received the No. 2 seed for the Big Ten. Nebraska’s Antrell Taylor is seeded above him.

Cole Mirasola was also given the No. 4 seed in a deep Big Ten bracket. Mirasola sits behind Taye Ghadiali of Michigan, AJ Ferrari of Nebraska and Nick Feldman of Ohio State at heavyweight. Mirasola lost to Ghadiali and Ferrari during the regular season but did beat Feldman.

Seeings are not finalized and can change up until Friday.

All 10 Penn State wrestlers will compete for the individual and team Big Ten title, with the first round starting at 10 a.m. Saturday in the Bryce Jordan Center. Consolations will start at 5 p.m. Saturday, with semifinals taking place at 7 p.m. that day. Consolation semifinals and seventh-place matches beginning at noon Sunday.

Championship, third place and fifth-place matches will begin at 4:30 p.m. Sunday in the Bryce Jordan Center.

title: Former Penn State women's basketball coach Coquese Washington fired from Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following the worst performance in the Big Ten, Coquese Washington is off to find a new home.

Washington was let go as head coach at Rutgers on Monday afternoon, according to her Instagram.

The Notre Dame alum spent four seasons with the Scarlet Knights, amassing a 42-84 record.

Prior to her time with Rutgers, Washington spent 12 years in Happy Valley, leading the Lady Lions to four straight NCAA tournament appearances from 2010 to 2014.

Washington helped Penn State to a 209-169 record before she was let go in 2019, when Penn State ultimately hired Carolyn Kieger.

title: Penn State Baseball faces St. John’s in two-game home series
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State Baseball returns from its trip to the Bahamas with two more losses and one win. The Indiana State Sycamores beat the Nittany Lions 8-5, 2-1 and lost 9-8 in their three-game series in Nassau. Before their trip out of the country, Penn State took on Richmond in a four-game series, winning one and losing two, with the fourth game cancelled due to weather.

The Nittany Lions will start a two-game series in Happy Valley against St. John’s on Tuesday and into Wednesday. Tuesday's matchup is at 5:30 p.m., and Wednesday’s game is at 4:00 p.m.

Penn State continues its rough start with a 3-6 record to start the season. St. John’s looks like a good team for the blue and white to get back on track, given its similar early troubles. Beginning their season with a record of 1-9, the Red Storm has been held at one run or fewer in six of their games. St. John's has a chance against Penn State after getting swept in Lexington by the Kentucky Wildcats. In all three games, the Red Storm failed to get over one run.

The last time these two teams played each other was in 2018. The Lions lost 18-4 in a blowout at a neutral site. Both teams sit second to last in their respective divisions, St. John’s being part of the Big East.

Both Penn State and St. John’s have yet to play a conference game. The Nittany Lions begin conference play against the Iowa Hawkeyes on March 13th at home. The Red Storm has to wait until April 2nd to face off against Seton Hall to start their conference games.

Nittany Lions' pitching should be a key to victory, with St. John's struggling to produce offensively in most of their games so far. A player to look out for is Dylan Fitzsimmons of the Red Storm, who leads his team in hits with 14.

Batting has been a weak point for both teams this season. Penn State has only gone above three runs four times this season. The Red Storm only hit the ball twice in their first matchup against Kentucky, then seven times in the second game, and six times in the last of the series.

The series between Penn State and St. John’s begins Tuesday at 5:30 p.m. at Medlar Field.

title: The most obscure games in Penn State men’s basketball history
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State endured a weird season in 2025-26 by most metrics; it staggered to the bottom of the conference as predicted and struggled to make good with the best Big Ten recruiting class.

Almost nothing on the hardwood except those flubbed performances went as expected for Mike Rhoades’ squad. However, in the blue and white’s decades long history, there lie a few games more bizarre than the ones of this season.

1902

Starting off in the early days of the 20th millennium, the Comp. B, 5th Regiment and Penn State faced off in 1902, and the score was a staggering 58-2 in Penn State’s favor. Not much else is recorded of that game, but it’s a nice introduction of the bizarre contests later to be detailed and requires mention on this list.

The game was weird in these early eras of hoops; in a 1902 win over Williamsport, professors from both schools even took turns as the rules officials during play.

1903

Both The Free Lance, one of the Collegian’s former names, and basketball — invented by James Naismith in 1891 — were in the beginning stages of development during the early 20th century. No better intersection shows this than the early publications of this outlet’s sports section, which even referred to baskets as “goals” instead of points.

The games of the incubation period were bizarre, and reporting often toed the line between commentary and reporting. Here’s a look at The Free Lance reporting on a road trip during the 1903 season:

“In no game does the condition of the floor or playing ground count for so much as in basket ball, and the playing ground seems to have been one of the causes of the defeats in almost every case.”

So why is this the case? Indiana hosted the blue and white in an opera hall and played their own ruleset. Grove City had a court that was too small to have out-of-bounds regions and at Westminster, the “hall” had posts in the middle of the playing surface — all factors which assisted the opposition.

That set was possibly the most ordinary group of games played that season. 130 seasons of action in program history, almost 3,000 games played, and only one tie. In basketball, the mere idea of a tie seems strange. But in that early period of hoops, it wasn’t that bizarre.

Documented details on the tie are limited. The game was played in February 1903 and ended in a 13-13 tie against Williamsport on the road; only the La Vie student yearbook and the program record book showed evidence of the game.

1904 – 1945

In the 1904 season, Penn State then played the Ex. 9th Regiment twice, once away and once at home, both ended in double-digit losses. La Vie recorded the first contest on Feb. 13 1904, with the blue and white falling 38-19, the second came as the last game of that same year on March 23, which ended 20-10.

In January of 1905, the State Collegian reported that Penn State basketball faced and defeated Williamsport High School to a score of 33-13; not much out of the ordinary happened in the game despite the different levels of institutions.

Clashes between wacky schools and Penn State are quite common. Penn State defeated the Great Lakes Naval Training Station 48-22 in 1919 and the Navy Pre-Flight School 51-43 in 1943.

1984-1991

Russia and America have always been at odds, and in the Cold War era, those tensions were at their highest. The one avenue of life where that tension wasn’t evident was in sport, and Happy Valley served as the center stage for this diplomacy a multitude of times.

In 1984, Penn State hosted a Yugoslavian club team in an exhibition contest before the season. The Nittany Lions had little knowledge of their opponents heading into that contest, and not much is reported about how the exhibition played out, but it must have gone well for them to do it again.

More was reported about the second game, when the Cuban National Team participated in a United States exhibition tour in 1991, with Penn State among the opponents. The international foes, arriving from a country still led by Fidel Castro.

The absurdity began even before tipoff, when three Nittany Lions were charged in court with harassment and theft of an SSA Radio. The Cubans also suffered from visa issues when entering the country. Penn State gave the "Comrades from the Caribbean,” as the Collegian at the time put it, their third straight loss of its American road trip, 61-51.

The women's team, on the other hand, lost 99-95 against the Cubans.

Also in the early 90s, Penn State faced off with a few club teams from the USSR, and on Nov. 7, 1990, it hosted Stroytel Club, but a day later, a more insane foe stepped into Rec Hall.

Maybe they read up on their history, but the Soviet National Trade Union All-Stars arrived in town with hot pink jerseys with black stripes, fitting of the occasion against a school that once donned the same colorway– imitation is the purest form of flattery.

That’s just a coincidence, sure, but one thing the Russians did read was their scouting report; they cruised to an 85-70 win, as the Nittany Lions allowed more points in that exhibition than they did in any game the season prior.

Penn State also had its string of hosting international club teams; foes from Sweden and Siberia were some of the bunch. The women’s team also got in on the fun, as Czechia sent over a club team, as well as Denmark with team Amager, which thumped the Lady Lions 106-36 in 1989.

2003

Continuing the trend of weird scrimmage opponents, at the turn of the millennium, the Nittany Lions faced the Nike Elite team, a ragtag bunch of misfits, unwanted at the pro level but trying to change that on a team in its third year ever in existence.

The Nittany Lions fell 90-78. 74-year-old Glenn Sergent led Nike Elite in the final crusade of his coaching career. His squad traveled by bus between back-to-back games across state lines, sometimes missing pre-game shootarounds, and often had only the luxury of a Holiday Inn hotel.

2025-2026

While unique opponents don’t make as frequent appearances on Penn State’s schedule anymore, this season actually saw the Nittany Lions hit the road for one of their weirdest games of all time.

In the offseason, it was announced that New Haven and Penn State agreed to a three-year scheduling deal, and in 2025, the first season of the agreement, the blue and white hit the road to face the Chargers in their third-ever game at the Division I level.

The Hazell Center is the third-smallest venue in the sport, seating just over 1,000 people. When Penn State traveled to town, it posted an 87-43 win, with it crammed at full capacity. It’s definitely a sight, and it resembled a Penn State hoops game at one of the blue and white’s satellite campuses – the Altoona campus’ venue seats nearly double the capacity.

The blue and white now hit the hardwood as it enters a new era. It has an opportunity to improve the program's trajectory and maybe even add an even crazier addition to the list — its first national title win.

title: Yoga, theme dinners, wrestling championship | This week in State College
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian

Here’s a look at some events to add to your calendar before heading home for break.

Yoga & Meditation

The event is for all-levels and registration is not required, offering participants the opportunity to engage their mind and body while taking part in this restorative practice.

Additional certified instructor-led sessions will continue from 5:30 to 6:30 p.m. in 107 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center and Wednesday noon to 1 p.m. at the Palmer Museum.

The show will run through Friday, and tickets can be bought online through the Penn State Arts Ticket Center.

The Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity will host a moodboard making event in celebration of Women’s History Month.

Participants will be provided with all supplies needed, and also have the opportunity to learn more about the historical significance of women’s self-expression throughout history.

The event will take place from 5 to 8 p.m. in HUBLL011 in the HUB.

Cafe Laura Theme Dinner, Bridgerton Night – Diamonds of the Season

This themed dinner offers those dining the experience of original and innovative cuisines based on Netflix’s “Bridgerton” series. All courses are prepared by Penn State Hospitality Management students.

Table reservations can be booked here, with a six-person maximum.

Penn State Baseball vs. St. John’s

The Nittany Lions will face off against St John’s Red Storm at 5:30 p.m. at Medlar Field. This event is free to the public.

Wednesday, March 4

Penn State Women’s Lacrosse vs. Colgate

This event is free to the public.

Men’s Basketball Senior Night vs. Ohio State

The blue and white will meet the scarlet and gray for a 7:30 p.m. tip-off in the Bryce Jordan Center.

Tickets can be purchased via Ticketmaster and the game will also be available for streaming through Peacock.

Thursday, March 5

Spiritual Wellness: Meditation with O-An Zendo

Hosted by the Center for Spiritual and Ethical Development, the first of this two-part series will introduce participants to zen tradition and Buddhism-related meditation techniques.

Session one will take place from 3 to 4:30 p.m. in 107 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center’s Memorial Lounge.

Men’s Ice Hockey vs. Wisconsin

Tickets can be purchased via Ticketmaster. The game will be available for streaming through Big Ten Plus.

Friday, March 6

Women’s Tennis vs. Michigan State

This event is free to the public.

Saturday, March 7

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Eating through Penn State with Anthony Provenzano
author: Carolena Bongi | The Daily Collegian
description:
What began as one-take food reviews on a private account has grown into a TikTok series during one Penn State student’s final year. Anthony Provenzano has taken his once private opinions to a more public platform for his senior year.

His reviews highlight food spots around Penn State and downtown State College, and have earned him over 3,400 followers.

“It kind of started in high school,” Provenzano, a fourth-year studying finance, said. “I used to do taco reviews on my private Snapchat story. One bite, say something stupid and rate it.”

The videos were just for friends, and the reviews often centered around different taco spots nearby, Provenzano said. He would give quick reactions inspired by Dave Portnoy’s “one-bite pizza review” style.

Provenzano originally intended for his videos to be an inside joke between friends, but then they gradually became something people looked forward to. After arriving at Penn State, he began to post less frequently. But with graduation approaching, decided he wanted to bring the idea back.

“I was going to go out to eat at these places anyway,” Provenzano said. “I’m only here for a couple more months.”

Since restarting his series publically, Provenzano has reviewed a variety of food spots across campus and downtown.

After trying bowls, breakfast options and go-to bar staples, some favorite spots of Provenzano’s have been Green Bowl and Sowers Harvest Cafe. He said part of his motivation was branching out and trying places he had walked past for years but never bothered to try.

When deciding on a rating, Provenzano said taste is the most important factor, but not the only one.

“The food is definitely the number one thing,” Provenzano said. “But the atmosphere, price, drinks; it's all about the overall vibe and how satisfied I am for how much I spent.”

Luke Washabaugh has known Provenzano since before college, and said seeing the reviews return this year wasn’t unexpected.

“We were kind of begging him to do reviews back in high school,” Washabaugh, a fourth-year studying health policy and administration, said. “It just feels like a natural progression he’s doing reviews at Penn State now.”

For Washabaugh, the videos have become an important part of their ending college experience. Whether it’s grabbing lunch at a spot with a unique menu item or planning future trips with friends, he said the process has added something fun to their final year together.

Connor Casten said he was excited to see Provenzano begin posting consistently again.

“He used to post a lot in high school and get a lot of views,” Casten, a fourth-year studying finance, said. “It was good to see him back.”

One of Casten’s favorite reviews, a “Drinking around State College” video, featured a stop at Doggie’s Pub where they got pizza, a large pickle and included many of their friends.

Casten said there've been moments when he might rate a meal slightly higher, but he believes Provenzano keeps his scores fair.

“He gives an honest opinion, and that’s all you can really ask for,” Casten said.

After graduation, Provenzano plans to move to Austin, Texas, where he hopes to continue trying new restaurants and expanding his reviews. But for now, the three friends are finishing their final few months at Penn State together, and the reviews have become a digital diary of their time together.
